facts.text: Babur's memoirs were originally written in Turki.
Babur was born on October 12, 1493.
Babur became ruler of Farghana at a young age.
Babur's father was Umar Shaikh.
Babur had uncles named Ahmad Miran-shahi and Mahmud Chaghatai.
Babur adopted orthodox Islamic observance.
Babur made his first attempt to capture Samarkand.
Babur successfully captured Samarkand in 1497.
Babur lost both Samarkand and Andijan.
Babur was left with only 200-300 men at one point.
Babur was distressed to tears when separated from his family.
Babur had ambitions to rule.
Babur won his first ranged battle against Ahmad Tambal at Khuban.
Babur married for the first time during his campaigns.
Babur's first child was born while he held Samarkand.
Babur was defeated by Shaibani in battle at Sar-i-pul.
Babur was besieged in Samarkand and suffered great privation.
Babur surrendered Samarkand to Shaibani.
Babur's sister Khanzada was married by Shaibani.
Babur escaped Samarkand and went to Dizak.
Babur wandered bareheaded and barefoot.
Babur's friend Nuyan Kukulash died, causing him grief.
Babur submitted verses to his uncle The Khan.
Babur was poor and in despair while in Tashkent.
Babur planned to go to Khitai.
Babur was wounded in battle.
Babur quoted Nizami when expecting death.
Babur took refuge in the Asfara hills.
Babur spent a year in misery and poverty in the Asfara hills.
Babur planned to go to Husain Bai-qara in Khurasan.
Babur changed his aim and went to Kabul instead.
Babur entered his 22nd lunar year.
Babur was joined by Khusrau Shah's brother Baqi Beg.
Babur was loyal to his half-brother Jahangir.
Babur first saw Canopus during his journey.
Kabul was surrendered to Babur by Muqim Arghun.
Babur received a description of Kabul.
Babur made his first move for Hindustan.
Babur traversed Khaibar.
Babur visited Bigram.
Baqi Beg prevented Babur from crossing the Sind.
Babur's expedition to Hindustan lasted four months and was disastrous.
Babur's mother died.
Babur was ill and stopped a move for Kandahar.
An earthquake occurred during Babur's time in Kabul.
Babur captured Qalat-i-ghilzai.
Babur was summoned by Husain Bai-qara against Shaibani.
Husain Bai-qara died.
Babur obeyed Husain Bai-qara's summons and left Kabul.
Babur traveled to Herat.
Babur claimed ceremonial observance due to his military achievements.
Babur previously abstained from wine.
Babur became inclined to drink wine.
Babur engaged Ma'suma in marriage.
Babur traveled through snow to reach Kabul.
A conspiracy in Kabul attempted to put Mirza Khan in Babur's place.
Babur defeated the rebels in Kabul.
Babur narrowly escaped death in the rebellion.
Babur attributed his safety to prayer.
Babur dealt mercifully with the rebel chiefs.
Babur asserted his aim was to write the truth.
Muhammad Husain Dughlat and Mirza Khan were banished by Babur.
Babur made a spring excursion to Koh-daman.
Babur raided the Ghilji Afghans.
Babur separated the Fifth (Khams).
Babur hunted wild-ass.
Babur won the battle of Kandahar.
Babur entered the fort of Kandahar.
Babur married Ma'suma.
Babur set out for Hindustan leaving Abdu'r-razzaq in Kabul.
Babur abandoned his Hindustan plan.
Babur gave Ghazni to Nasir.
Babur assumed the title of Padshah.
Babur's son Humayun was born.
Babur raided the Mahmand Afghans.
Babur reprieved seditious offenders.
Babur's five-fold victory over hostile champions occurred.
Said Chaghatai took refuge with Babur.
Haider Dughlat took refuge with Babur.
Ismail Safawi defeated the Auzbegs near Merv.
Shaibani was killed by Ismail.
20,000 Mughuls returned to near Qunduz.
Mirza Khan invited Babur to join him against the Auzbegs.
Babur went to Qunduz.
Babur made Said The Khan of the Mughuls.
Babur made his first attempt on Hisar.
Ismail sent Khanzada Begim back to Babur.
Babur asked Ismail for help against the Auzbegs.
Babur made his second attempt on Hisar.
Babur won the Battle of Pul-i-sangin.
Babur put Hamza and Mahdi to death.
Babur occupied Samarkand after a nine-year absence.
Babur gave Kabul to Nasir.
Babur was in a difficult position in relation to Shia Ismail.
Ismail sent Najm Sani to bring Babur to order.
Ubaid defeated Babur at Kul-i-malik.
Babur fled from Samarkand to Hisar.
Najm Sani perpetrated the massacre of Qarshi.
Babur was helpless to prevent the massacre of Qarshi.
Najm Sani was defeated and slain.
Babur did not fight at the battle where Najm Sani was slain.
Babur was attacked suddenly by Mughuls in Hisar.
Babur escaped to Qunduz.
Babur may have spent a year in Khishm.
Ismail took Balkh from the Auzbegs.
Babur returned to Kabul.
Babur's daughter Gulrang was born in Khwast.
Nasir died.
A riot in Ghazni was led by Sherim Taghai Mughul.
Sherim Taghai sought Babur's protection.
Babur's son Askari was born.
Babur visited Balkh.
Babur pursued Muhammad-i-zaman Mirza Bai-qara.
Babur brought Muhammad-i-zaman Mirza to Kabul.
Babur gave his daughter Ma'suma in marriage to Muhammad-i-zaman Mirza.
An expedition to Kandahar returned fruitless due to Babur's illness.
Hasan, Shah Beg's son, fled to Babur.
Hasan stayed with Babur for two years.
Babur began a campaign in Bajaur against Haidar-i-ali Bajuri.
Babur took two forts in Bajaur.
Babur took the Fort of Bajaur.
Babur massacred the people of Bajaur as false to Islam.
Khwaja Kalan was made Commandant of Bajaur.
Babur married Bibi Mubarika.
Babur repopulated the Fort of Bajaur.
Babur destroyed the tomb of a heretic qalandar.
Babur first crossed the Sind.
Babur had a long-cherished desire for Hindustan.
Babur regarded Bhira, Khush-ab, Chin-ab and Chiniyut as earlier Turkish possessions.
Babur sent assurance of safety to Bhira as a Turkish possession.
Babur sent envoys to the Baluchis in Bhira.
Heavy floods occurred in Babur's camp.
Babur punished offenders against Bhira people.
Babur collected agreed tribute.
Babur sent an envoy to ask Ibrahim Ludi for lands once dependent on the Turks.
Daulat Khan arrested Babur's envoy.
Babur's son Hind-al was born.
Babur held drinking-parties.
Tatar Khan Kakar compelled Minuchihr Khan Turk to become his son-in-law.
Babur took the Fort of Bajaur in 925 AH (1519 AD).
Babur massacred the people of Bajaur for being false to Islam.
Babur married Bibi Mubarka in 925 AH.
Babur first crossed the Sind in 925 AH.
Babur regarded Bhira, Khush-ab, Chin-ab and Chiniyut as earlier Turkish possessions that were therefore his own.
Babur had intermittent fever while in Bhira.
Babur shaved his head during this period.
Babur dislocated his wrist during a Yusuf-zai campaign.
Babur first wrote after dislocating his wrist.
Babur received a white falcon as a gift.
Babur finished copying Ali-sher's four Diwans.
Babur issued an order to exclude from future parties those who become drunk.
Babur shot with an easy bow for the first time after his wrist dislocation.
Babur recorded his wish to return to obedience in his 40th year.
Babur had excess in wine as his 40th year approached.
Babur considered beer to have ill-taste and disgusting intoxication.
Babur conducted five expeditions into Hindustan.
Babur defeated Bihar Khan Ludi near Lahor.
Babur occupied Lahor.
Babur stormed, plundered and massacred the people of Dibalpur.
Babur defeated Ibrahim Ludi at the Battle of Panipat.
Ibrahim Ludi's body was found after the Battle of Panipat.
Babur occupied Delhi and Agra.
Babur made the circuit of a Farghana-born ruler in Delhi.
The khutba was read for Babur in Delhi.
Babur received the Koh-i-nur diamond from the Gwaliar family through Humayun.
Babur distributed Agra treasure.
Khwaja Kalan decided to leave Babur's service.
Biban Jalwani revolted and was beaten by Babur.
Rana Sanga failed in his promise to act with Babur.
Babur's son Faruq was born in 933 AH.
Ibrahim's mother tried to poison Babur.
Babur defeated Rana Sanga at the Battle of Kanwa.
Babur assumed the title of Ghazi after the Battle of Kanwa.
Babur renounced wine before the Battle of Kanwa.
Babur destroyed wine and precious vessels as part of his renunciation.
Babur built a commemorative well and alms-house after renouncing wine.
Babur remitted the tamgha tax after his victory at Kanwa.
Babur set up a pillar of heads after pursuing fugitive foes.
Babur visited Biana after the Battle of Kanwa.
Babur visited Miwat.
Babur visited Alwar.
Humayun was allowed to leave Hindustan.
Babur was ill seemingly with fever during 933 AH.
Babur visited Dulpur and ordered a house excavated.
Babur visited Bari and saw the ebony-tree.
Babur had doubt of Bayzid Farmuli's loyalty.
Babur composed a Treatise on Prosody.
Babur visited Kul and Sambal in 934 AH.
Babur had fever and ague intermittently for 20-25 days.
A large mortar burst with fatal result.
Babur started for Holy War against Chandiri.
Babur sent troops against Bayzid Farmuli.
Babur took Chandiri.
Babur ordered a pontoon-bridge across the Ganges.
Babur's artillery was used with effect to cross the Ganges.
Tukhta-bugha Chagatai arrived from Kashgar.
Babur visited Lakhnau.
Babur suffered from ear-ache.
Babur reinforced Chin-timur against rebels.
Chin-timur defeated Bayzid Farmuli.
Babur settled the affairs of Aud (Oude).
Babur swam the Ganges.
Babur was ill for 40 days.
Babur recalled Askari from Multan.
Babur sent Khwaja Dost-i-khawand to Kabul on family affairs.
Askari reached Agra from Multan in 935 AH.
Babur prepared to visit Gwaliar.
Babur bade farewell to kinswomen returning to Kabul.
Babur was given an unsavory medicament.
Babur inspected construction-work in Dulpur.
Babur suffered from ear-ache in Gwaliar.
Babur inspected work in Sikri.
Babur sent an envoy to take charge of Rantanbhur.
Babur made a levy on stipendiaries.
Babur sent letters to kinsfolk in Khurasan.
Babur was strongly attacked by fever in 935 AH.
Babur translated Ahrari's Walidiyyah-risala for his healing.
Babur's son Humayun had a son born.
Babur remitted the tamgha tax.
Babur gave royal insignia to Askari.
Babur ordered the measurement, stations and up-keep of the Agra-Kabul road.
Babur visited Dulpur and inspected his constructions.
Babur decided contingently to go to the East.
Baluchi incursions occurred.
Babur received news of the loss of Bihar (town).
Babur started from Agra to go East.
Mahim ordered her own escort from Kabul to Agra using Babur's style.
Babur watched wrestling.
Babur left the Jumna and disembarked his guns.
Babur traveled by litter.
Babur sent a copy of his writings to Samarkand.
Babur crossed the Jumna at Allahabad (Piag).
Babur was attacked by boils.
Babur sent money-drafts to travelers from Kabul.
Babur had news that the Ladies were on their way from Kabul.
Babur negotiated with an envoy of Nasrat Shah of Bengal.
Babur examined Muhammad-i-zaman's objections to a Bihar appointment.
Babur dispatched troops to Bihar (town).
Babur gave royal insignia to Muhammad-i-zaman.
Duda Bibi and her son Jalal escaped from Bengal to come to Babur.
Babur made further effort for peace with Bengal.
Babur sent an ultimatum to Nasrat Shah.
Babur visited lotus-beds near Arrah.
Babur measured distance by counting a horse's paces.
Babur was angered by Junaid Barlas' belated arrival.
Babur crossed the Ganges by the Burh-ganga channel.
Babur won the Battle of the Gogra.
Babur praised his Chagatai cousins for their services.
Babur crossed into the Nirhun pargana.
Babur made peace with Nasrat Shah.
Babur pursued Biban and Bayzid.
Babur's papers were damaged in a storm.
Babur made a rapid ride to Agra.
Babur was pleased with Indian-grown fruits.
Mahim arrived at Agra.
Shaikh Ghaus came from Gwaliar to intercede for Rahim-dad.
Babur took over Gwaliar in 936 AH.
Humayun deserted his post in Badakhshan.
Humayun went to Kabul and arranged with Kamran.
Humayun sent Hindal to Badakhshan.
Humayun went to Agra and arrived unexpected by his Father.
Babur was ordered to Agra.
Babur's boats were disposed of during military operations.
Babur visited kinswomen in Agra.
Babur's mother Qut-luq-nigar was well-born and a scholar's daughter.
Babur's mother was educated in Turki and Persian.
Babur's grandmother Aisan-daulat was nomad-born and sternly-bred.
Babur's grandmother Aisan-daulat was brave in her opinions.
Babur's sister Khan-zada was five years his elder.
Khan-zada proved her devotion to Babur from his birth to his death.
Babur's grandfather Yunas Khan was a penman, painter, singer, and instrumentalist.
Yunas Khan was an expert in good companionship with even temper and perfect manners.
Yunas Khan lived to be approximately 74 years old.
Babur broke the Law against intoxicants and later repented and returned to obedience.
Babur's father Umar Shaikh was a reader of great books including the Quran and the Masnawi.
Babur took Timur as his great exemplar in both soldiering and chronicling.
Babur made many fruitful gardens and improved arid halting-places.
Babur built little but had small chance to test his capacity for building greatly.
Babur was never rich and was several times destitute in his home-lands.
Babur's sword won wealth that gave riches to his Indian Dynasty.
Babur began chronicling his adventure as a boy with a future task in mind.
Babur chronicled his adventures for approximately 37 years.
Babur accepted reinforcement from Ismail the Shi'a Shah of Persia in 1511-12.
Babur first broke the Law against wine in 1512.
Babur's return to obedience began around 1519-1525.
Babur made a fifth expedition across the Indus that resulted in the conquest of Hindustan.
Babur resolved to practice the rite of intercession and self-surrender to save Humayun's life.
Babur refused the substitution of the Koh-i-nur diamond for his own life.
Babur performed the rite of intercession and Humayun recovered.
Babur fell ill and was bedridden until his death.
Babur died on December 26, 1530.
Babur was first buried near Agra and later near Kabul.
Babur had faith in the rite of intercession.
Babur had a love of Nature throughout his life.
Babur admired great architectural creations.
Babur was influenced by the Court of Sultan Husain Mirza in Herat.
Babur aspired to bring whatever work he took up to perfection.
Babur's autobiography was contemporary with the events it describes.
Babur's autobiography is boyish in his boyhood and matures as he matured.
Babur's autobiography is fresh and spontaneous rather than dulled by retrospect.
Babur was born on February 14, 1483.
Babur ruled successively in Farghana, Kabul, and Hindustan.
Babur's surviving autobiography records approximately 18 years of his life.
Babur's commemorable years span about 47 years and 10 months.
Babur's camp near Hisar was destroyed in 1512.
Babur's book and sections were damaged in 1529.
Babur left a complete autobiography in 1530.
Babur annotated his father's book in 1555.
Babur had an orderly and persistent bias of mind.
Babur had a frank and self-judging habit of mind.
Babur was accomplished and lettered.
Babur was steadfast and honest.
Babur was prideful.
Babur had a frank way of writing.
Babur practiced describing a territory upon obtaining it.
Babur was betrothed at age 5 in Samarkand.
Babur received education in the rules of his Faith.
Babur was appointed to the Andijan Command.
Babur became ruler of Farghana at age 12.
Babur formed a tactical alliance with Shah Ismail that lasted 19-20 months.
Babur was angered by the conduct of the campaign by Ismail's Commanders.
Babur's Transoxus campaign was an episode in the struggle between Shaibaq Khan and Shah Ismail.
Babur sent copies of his book to sons and friends during his life.
Humayun was in power after Babur's death in 1530.
Humayun was exiled from rule in Hindustan for 14 years beginning in 1441.
Humayun entered Dihli in July 1555.
Humayun had marginal annotations in a codex of Babur's work.
Babur's autograph manuscript was seen in Shah-i-jahan's private library between 1628 and 1638.
The Haidarabad Codex dates approximately to 1700 based on paper quality.
Nadir Shah's vengeance on Dihli occurred in 1739.
The Mutiny caused dispersions and fires affecting Indian libraries.
Babur's Hindustan verses were once part of Shah-i-jahan's collection.
Shaikh Zainu'd-din of Khawaf was Babur's Persian secretary.
Shaikh Zainu'd-din wrote the Tabaqat-i-baburi during Babur's life.
The Tabaqat-i-baburi paraphrases the record of a few months of Hindustan campaigning.
The Tabaqat-i-baburi includes an account of the battle of Panipat.
Babur's original codex originally contained much over 382 folios.
Khwaja Kalan Ahraris' Codex was completed in 1529.
Humayun's Codex was completed around 1531.
Muhammad Haidar Dughlat's Codex was completed between 1536 and 1540.
The Elphinstone Codex was completed between 1556 and 1567.
The Elphinstone Codex contained 286 folios in 1816 and 1907.
The British Museum MS was completed in 1629.
The Bib. Lindesiana MS scribe was living in 1625.
The Haidarabad Codex contains 382 folios.
Khwaja Kalan Ahraris' Codex was sent to Samarkand in 1529.
The Elphinstone Codex was bought in Peshawar in 1810.
Nur-muhammad was the nephew of Abu'l-fazl.
The Haidarabad Codex was centupled in facsimile in 1905.
At least two important codices were copied during the first decade of Humayun's reign.
Babur's book was translated into Persian by Abdu'r-rahim by Akbar's orders in 1589.
The Persian translation was made for Abu'l-faẓl's use in the Akbar-nama.
Gaps in Babur's text result from loss of pages.
The gaps in Babur's autobiography sometimes remove records of 6 months, 18 months, 6 years, or 11 years.
Some 12 years of record are lost from the beginning of Babur's autobiography.
Some 16 months of record are lost from the end of Babur's autobiography.
The Shi'a alliance duration was 19-20 months of 917-918 AH.
Babur's Transoxus campaign was between Sunni and Shi'a forces.
Elphinstone believed Babur possessed candour and veracity.
Elphinstone was angered by Erskine's remark about Babur and Alam Khan.
Elphinstone was a student of Babur's book.
Elphinstone was one of Erskine's staunchest helpers in producing the Memoirs.
The death of Yunas Khan occurred in 1487.
The death of Khwaja Ubaidu'l-lah Ahrari occurred in 1491.
Umar Shaikh died in 1494.
Babur had kinsmen in Tashkint.
Babur had kinsmen in Samarkand.
Babur received initiation in school subjects, sport, and the use of arms.
Babur had teachers whose names are not recorded in the surviving text.
Alam Khan requested reinforcement from Babur to oust his nephew Ibrahim.
Jahangir was a rival in the disputed succession for Farghana.
Babur's partisans had the better of Jahangir's in the succession dispute.
Babur testified obeisance before becoming ruler of Farghana.
Displeased kinsmen advanced against Umar Shaikh.
Babur's army passed down from Kabul to Hindustan in 1525.
The year of Babur's victory in Hindustan was 1525 (932 AH).
The Tarikh-i rashidi contains the Babur-Haidar story of the Transoxus campaign.
The Bukhara book is a compilation of oddments mostly translated from compositions written after Babur's death.
The Bukhara book has two parts, each with its own preface.
The first part of the Bukhara book recites Babur's Acts.
The second part of the Bukhara book recites Humayun's Acts.
The Bukhara book is a distinct work mistakenly believed to be Babur's own.
The Haidarabad Codex was collated with the Bukhara book in 1903.
The colophon of the original Bukhara MS was discovered in 1921.
The author of the Bukhara book gave his name, title, and date in the colophon.
Few copies of the Babur-nama seem to have been made.
Not one existing copy of the Babur-nama contains the complete autobiography.
One copy has the maximum of dwindled text shown in the Persian translation of 1589.
Two books have been reputed to contain Babur's authentic text.
One authentic text was preserved in Hindustan by Babur's descendants.
One authentic text issued from Bukhara.
The Hindustan book and Bukhara book differ in total contents, arrangement, and textual worth.
The Hindustan book is a record of the Acts of Babur alone.
Kehr's Codex is the fullest accessible example of the Bukhara book.
Babur's book was fashioned by many men in obedience to dictates given by his autobiography.
Babur and his autobiography have had Earth's immortality for 500 years.
Much information about the Turki MSS was published in the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society from 1900 onwards.
The search for reliable Turki text of the Babur-nama involved examining MSS in distant places.
Some MSS were not traceable during the search.
All MSS whose continued existence was confirmed were examined in England.
Earlier statements about the MSS were superseded by later findings.
The Elphinstone Codex and Klaproth's articles required clearing of transitory matter.
The information about the MSS does not claim to be complete.
Babur's autobiography was originally written in Turki.
Humayun annotated a copy of Babur's autobiography.
Ali'u-'l-katib copied Babur's Turki book on Humayun's order.
The copy ordered by Humayun was finished in February 1531, six weeks after Babur's death.
Muhammad Haidar Mirza Dughlat possessed a copy of Babur's autobiography.
Muhammad Haidar Mirza may have copied the autobiography down to 1512 while with Babur.
Muhammad Haidar Mirza died in 1551.
The Elphinstone Codex was purchased in Peshawar in 1810.
The Elphinstone Codex was found in Peshawar and purchased by Elphinstone.
Elphinstone sent the Codex to Erskine in Bombay in 1813.
Erskine received the Elphinstone Codex after he had already completed work comparing his translation with Leyden's.
The Elphinstone Codex was sent to the Advocates' Library in Scotland in 1826.
Two Persian translations of the Babur-nama were made during Akbar's reign.
The earlier Persian translation was begun in 1583 by Payanda-hasan of Ghazni and Muhammad-quli of Hisar.
Abdu'r-rahim Mirza translated the Babur-nama into Persian on Akbar's order.
Abdu'r-rahim Mirza's translation was presented to Akbar in the last week of November 1589.
Akbar was visiting Babur's tomb in Kabul when the translation was presented.
Jahangir examined a Babur-nama Codex that was entirely in Babur's handwriting except for four portions.
Jahangir attested in Turki that four portions of the Codex were in his own handwriting.
Babur died on December 26th, 1530.
Shah-i-jahan Padshah possessed Babur's autograph Codex.
The Haidarabad Codex may have been inscribed under Aurang-zib Padshah.
The Haidarabad Codex surpassed all other Babur-nama manuscripts in volume and quality.
The Haidarabad Codex was reproduced in facsimile by the E. J. Wilkinson Gibb Trust.
Nadir Shah's desolation of Dihli occurred around 1700.
Dr. John Leyden began to study Turki around 1805.
Leyden took up Turki again in 1810 after returning from Malay.
Leyden sailed for Java in the autumn of 1810.
Leyden died in August 1811.
Leyden's translation of the Babur-nama was tentative and unrevised.
Erskine had prior acquaintance with the Persian original of the Babur-nama.
Erskine finished his translation in 1813.
Erskine dedicated his complete volume to Elphinstone by 1816.
The Memoirs of Baber was published in 1826.
Erskine worked on a secondary source in producing the Memoirs.
Elphinstone deposited his Codex in the Advocates' Library in 1826.
Leyden's papers are located in the Memorial Cottage in Denholm.
The Babur-nama in English was completed in May 1921.
In May 1921 the contents of volumes were completed including the Babur-nama in English and its supplements.
The aims of the Babur-nama translation are to make Babur known in English diction answering to his original words and to be serviceable to readers and students of his book and of classic Turki.
The Bukhara Babur-nama is a compilation of items diverse in origin, diction, and age.
The Bukhara Babur-nama was planned to be a record of the Acts of Babur and Humayun.
The Bukhara Babur-nama ranked for nearly 200 years as a reproduction of Babur's authentic text.
The Bukhara Babur-nama was reconstructed and printed in Kasan in 1857.
The Bukhara Babur-nama was translated in Paris in 1871.
The earliest known example of the Bukhara compilation was finished in 1709.
In the first decade of the eighteenth century Peter the Great gave attention to Russian relations with foreign states of Central Asia.
Peter the Great negotiated with the Khan of Bukhara for the reception of a Russian mission.
A Russian mission was dispatched in 1719 and arrived in Bukhara in 1721.
During the Russian mission's stay in Bukhara, a member of its secretariat bought a Compilation MS. noted as finished in 1714.
Timur-pulad bought the Babur-nama manuscript after coming to Bukhara with the Russian envoy Florio Beg Beneveni.
The book reached the Foreign Office in 1725.
In 1737 George Jacob Kehr obtained the book from the Foreign Office and copied it with meticulous care.
George Jacob Kehr was a teacher of Turki and other languages in the Oriental School.
George Jacob Kehr produced a Latin version of the Bukhara compilation that was a fiasco.
Ilminski edited the Kasan Imprint in 1857 based on Kehr's reproduction.
A collateral copy of the Timur-pulad Codex was made in 1742.
In 1824 Klaproth copied from the Timur-pulad MS. its purchaser's note and other endorsements.
Ilminski produced his imprint in Kasan in 1857, which became de Courteille's source for Les Mémoires de Baber in 1871.
No worker in the series of Bukhara compilation scholars showed doubt about accepting the Compilation as containing Babur's authentic text.
Ilminski stated that a reliable text could not be made from his source but that a Turki reading-book could be made.
Ilminski did not obey the dual plan of the Compilation that Kehr's transcript revealed.
Ilminski thought a better history of Babur could be produced by following Erskine than by obeying Kehr.
Ilminski's transformation of the items of his source misled Pavet de Courteille to over-estimate his Turki source.
The French work on the Babur-nama was hampered by being done in Paris in 1871 when there was no opportunity to learn the contents of Ilminski's Russian Preface.
The author of the Bukhara Compilation was Mulla `Abdu'l-wahhab the Teacher of Ghaj-davan in Bukhara.
Mulla `Abdu'l-wahhab completed the Waqi`nama-i-padshahi on Monday, Rajab 5, 1121 (August 31st, 1709).
The title Waqi`nama-i-padshahi suits the plan of dual histories of Babur and Humayun better than the title Babur-nama.
The colophon of the Mulla does not claim for him to have copied Babur's elder book but to have written down and composed one under a differing title.
The Mulla's deprecation and thanks tone better with perplexing work than with the steadfast patience of a good scribe.
The Mulla is exonerated from suspicion of having caused his compilation to be accepted as Babur's authentic text.
Erskine did not see any example of the Turki text during the progress of his translation work.
Erskine had little acquaintance with Turki.
Erskine's results have been the one introduction of Babur's writings to English readers for almost a century.
Leyden's fragment of unrevised attempt to translate the Bukhara Compilation is a negligible matter.
Erskine was the pioneer amongst European workers on Baburiana.
The wording of the Memoirs would not press back into the Turki mould.
The Memoirs is sound in its matter and partly representative of Babur himself.
The translator of the new Babur-nama has enjoyed an advantage no translator has had since `Abdu'r-rahim in 1589.
Three distinct impressions of Babur's personality are set by his own, Erskine's and de Courteille's words and manner.
The divergencies in portrayal of Babur's personality are partly due to differing textual bases.
The divergencies in portrayal of Babur's personality may result mainly from the use by the two Europeans of unsifted, current English and French.
Turki as Babur writes it is terse, word-thrifty, restrained and lucid.
Turki comes over neatly into Anglo-Saxon English, perhaps through primal affinities.
Babur's use of 'we' is neither regal nor self-magnifying but is co-operative.
Babur's 'we' befits the chief whose volunteer and nomad following makes or unmakes his power.
Babur's 'I' is individual.
The Memoirs varies much from Babur's uses of pronouns.
Babur writes 'There was drinking' to indicate that all or all but a few shared the local wine.
Erskine translates Babur's impersonal statement as 'I drank'.
The importance of observing Babur's limits of vocabulary is significant.
In June 1494, at age twelve, Babur became ruler of Farghāna.
Farghāna is situated in the fifth climate and at the limit of settled habitation.
Farghāna is a small country abounding in grain and fruits.
Farghāna is girt round by mountains except on the west towards Khujand and Samarkand.
The Saiḥūn River flows westward through Farghāna and sinks into the sands below Turkistān.
Farghāna has seven separate townships, five on the south and two on the north of the Saiḥūn.
Andijān is the capital of Farghāna and has a central position.
Andijān produces much grain, fruits in abundance, excellent grapes and melons.
Andijān's fort is the largest in Mawārā'u'n-nahr after Samarkand and Kesh.
Andijān has three gates.
Water enters Andijān's citadel by nine channels.
Andijānīs are all Turks and speak correct Turkī.
The climate of Andijān is malarious and people generally get fever in autumn.
Aūsh is located to the south-east of Andijān, four yīghāch by road.
Aūsh has a fine climate, an abundance of running waters and a beautiful spring season.
Babur built a retreat on the Barā Koh mountain in 902 AH (1496 AD).
Aūsh has no equal township in all Farghāna for climate and pleasantness.
Marghīnān is located seven yīghāch by road to the west of Andijān.
Marghīnān's apricots and pomegranates are most excellent.
Marghīnān's people are Sārts, boxers, noisy and turbulent.
Asfara is in the hill-country, nine yīghāch by road south-west of Marghīnān.
Asfara's people are all Persian-speaking Sārts.
When Muḥammad Shaibānī Khān defeated Sl. Maḥmūd Khān, Babur went into the Sūkh and Hushyār hill-country.
After about a year in the hills, Babur set out for Kābul.
Khujand is twenty-five yīghāch by road to the west of Andijān and twenty-five yīghāch east of Samarkand.
Khujand is one of the ancient towns.
Khujand's pomegranates are renowned for their excellence.
The climate of Khujand is very malarious with much fever in autumn.
Kand-i-badām is a dependency of Khujand with excellent almonds.
Akhsī is nine yīghāch by road to the west of Andijān.
After Andijān, Akhsī is the largest township in Farghāna.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā made Akhsī his capital.
Akhsī's fort is the strongest in all Farghāna.
Akhsī's melons are excellent, with one kind called Mīr Tīmūrī.
Kāsān is a small township to the north of Akhsī.
Kāsān has excellent air and beautiful little gardens.
The mountains round Farghāna contain excellent summer-pastures.
The tabalghū tree grows in the mountains of Farghāna and has red bark.
Turquoise and iron mines exist in the mountains of Farghāna.
If people do justly, three or four thousand men may be maintained by the revenues of Farghāna.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was a ruler of high ambition and great pretension.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was always bent on conquest.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā led several armies against Samarkand.
Yūnas Khān was Babur's grandfather and Khān of the Mughūls.
Yūnas Khān was descended from Chaghatāī Khān, the second son of Chīngīz Khān.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā gave Tāshkīnt to Yūnas Khān.
From 890 AH (1485 AD) to 908 AH (1503 AD), Tāshkīnt and Shāhrukhiya were held by the Chaghatāī Khāns.
In 899 AH (1494 AD), the Mughūl Khānship was in Sl. Maḥmūd Khān.
Sl. Maḥmūd Khān was Yūnas Khān's younger son and a half-brother of Babur's mother.
Sl. Aḥmad Mīrzā was Umar Shaikh Mīrzā's elder brother and ruler of Samarkand.
Sl. Aḥmad Mīrzā had given a daughter to Sl. Maḥmūd Khān.
On Monday, Ramẓān 4 (June 8th), Umar Shaikh Mīrzā fell from the fort of Akhsī and died.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was 39 lunar years old when he died.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was born in Samarkand in 860 AH (1456 AD).
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was the fourth son of Sl. Abū-sa`īd Mīrzā.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was younger than Sl. Aḥmad M., Sl. Muḥammad M., and Sl. Maḥmūd Mīrzā.
Sl. Abū-sa`īd Mīrzā was the son of Sl. Muḥammad Mīrzā.
Sl. Muḥammad Mīrzā was the son of Mīrān-shāh M., Tīmūr Beg's third son.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā's father first gave him Kābul.
Bābā-i-Kābulī was Umar Shaikh Mīrzā's guardian.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was recalled from the Tamarisk Valley to Samarkand for the Mīrzās' Circumcision Feast.
After the Feast, Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was given Andijān.
Tīmūr Beg had given Farghāna (Andijān) to his son, the elder Umar Shaikh Mīrzā.
Khudāī-bīrdī Tūghchī Tīmūr-tāsh was Umar Shaikh Mīrzā's guardian.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was short and stout with a round beard and fleshy face.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā wore his tunic very tight.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was not choice in dress or food.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā wound his turban in a fold, unlike the four-fold turbans worn by others.
In the heats and except in his Court, Umar Shaikh Mīrzā generally wore the Mughūl cap.
Umar Shaikh Mīrzā was a true believer and Ḥanafī in his faith.
Sayyid Qāsim Beg was sent to lead an army against mountain tribes who did not pay tribute.
The army under Qāsim Beg took approximately 20,000 sheep and between 1,000 and 1,500 horses from the mountain tribes.
Aūrā-tīpā was formerly held by `Umar Shaikh Mīrzā.
Shaikh Ẕū'n-nūn Arghūn ordered the death of Khalīfa, an envoy sent to negotiate.
Khalīfa escaped death and reached Babur after suffering great hardships.
Babur and his forces turned back from Aūrā-tīpā due to winter approaching and lack of provisions.
Muḥammad Ḥusain Kūrkān Dūghlāt was given Aūrā-tīpā and held it until 908 AH (1503).
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā led his army from Khurāsān against Ḥiṣār.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā was a shrewd and experienced commander.
Sulṯān Mas`ūd Mīrzā broke camp and retreated to Ḥiṣār without engaging the forces that had crossed the river.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā and Ibrāhīm Ḥusain Mīrzā were sent against Khusrau Shāh.
Muz̤affar Ḥusain Mīrzā was sent against Khutlān.
Sulṯān Mas`ūd Mīrzā fled to Samarkand to his brother Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā.
Ḥamza Sl. and Mahdī Sl. left Shaibānī Khān's service to join Sulṯān Maḥmūd Mīrzā's service.
Ḥamza Sl., Mamāq Sl., Mahdī Sl., and Muḥammad Dūghlāt came to Babur at Andijān in Ramẓān.
Babur rose from his raised seat to honor Ḥamza Sl., Mamāq Sl., and Mahdī Sl. when they entered.
Babur placed the visiting sulṯāns on his right according to custom.
Muḥammad Ḥiṣārī and other Mughūls elected to serve Babur.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā besieged Ḥiṣār with continuous mining and catapult attacks.
The besieged townsmen of Ḥiṣār used countermining to force smoke back on the besiegers.
The townsmen of Ḥiṣār drove off the besiegers by pouring water into the mines.
A tower of Ḥiṣār fort was cracked by catapult and mortar fire and fell at the Bed-time Prayer.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā refused to assault Ḥiṣār at night despite the fallen tower.
The besieged townsmen rebuilt the entire tower before dawn.
The siege of Ḥiṣār lasted two to two and a half months.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā and his troops dismounted about 16 miles below Qūndūz.
Khusrau Shāh arrayed his forces and marched out to fight Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā and his commanders remained in their entrenchments rather than engage Khusrau Shāh.
Khusrau Shāh's forces numbered approximately 4,000 to 5,000 men.
Khusrau Shāh later commanded between 20,000 and 30,000 men.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā marched from his camp and halted at the Alghū Mountain of Tāliqān.
Khusrau Shāh sent his brother Walī to harass Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā from outside.
Muḥibb-`alī the armourer unhorsed some of the Mīrzā's men and cut off heads.
Sayyidīm `Alī and others were unhorsed in fighting with the Khurāsānīs.
Spring rains at Ḥiṣār caused distress in Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā's army.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā arranged a peace and married his son Ḥaidar Mīrzā to Bega Begīm.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā besieged Qūndūz and made peace through Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā's intervention.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā gave Balkh to Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā.
Sulṯān Ḥusain Mīrzā gave Astarābād to Muz̤affar Ḥusain Mīrzā.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā was offended by receiving Balkh while his brother received Astarābād.
Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā's offense led to years of rebellion and disturbance.
The Tarkhānīs revolted in Samarkand in Ramẓān of 901 AH.
Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā was more friendly with the begs of Ḥiṣār than those of Samarkand.
Shaikh `Abdu'l-lāh Barlās was Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā's favorite beg.
Darwesh Muḥammad Tarkhān brought Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā to Samarkand and raised him to be supreme.
The Tarkhānīs treated Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā like a prisoner and took him to the citadel.
Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā escaped the citadel through a water-conduit and went to Khwājakī Khwāja's house.
Khwājakī Khwāja refused to give up Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā to the Tarkhānīs.
Khwāja Abu'l-makāram and other begs rescued Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā and besieged the Tarkhānīs in the citadel.
The Tarkhānīs could not hold the citadel for even a day.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān fled to Bukhārā through the Gate of the Four Roads.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā and Darwesh Muḥ. Tarkhān were made prisoners.
Darwesh Muḥammad Tarkhān was ordered to death.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā was taken to the Gūk Sarāī to have his eyes burned.
The fire-pencil was drawn across Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā's eyes but no harm was done.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā went to Khwāja Yahya's house and later to the Tarkhānīs in Bukhārā.
Muḥammad `Ubaidu'l-lāh became the spiritual guide of the elder prince.
Yahya became the spiritual guide of the younger prince.
Khwāja Yahya followed Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā to Bukhārā.
Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā led his army against Bukhārā.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā defeated Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā in battle near Bukhārā.
Aḥmad Ḥājī Beg was taken prisoner in the battle near Bukhārā.
Most of Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā's men were put to death in the battle.
Aḥmad Ḥājī Beg was put to a dishonourable death by the slaves of Darwesh Muḥammad Tarkhān.
Babur received news of events in Samarkand in the month of Shawwāl.
Babur coveted Samarkand.
Babur got his men to horse to move against Samarkand.
Sulṯān Mas'ūd Mīrzā came to join Babur's effort against Samarkand.
Khusrau Shāh laid hands on his younger brother Walī to reinforce Sulṯān Mas'ūd Mīrzā.
Three mīrzās besieged Samarkand from three sides for three or four months.
Khwāja Yahya came to Babur to mediate an agreement.
Babur met Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā in the middle of the Kohik water with four or five men each.
Babur saw Mullā Binā'ī and Muḥammad Ṣāliḥ in Khwāja Yahya's service.
Mullā Binā'ī was long in Babur's service later on.
Babur retired from Samarkand to Andijān as winter approached.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā retired to Bukhārā.
Sulṯān Mas'ūd Mīrzā had a penchant for a daughter of Shaikh `Abdu'l-lāh Barlās.
Sulṯān Mas'ūd Mīrzā took the daughter of Shaikh `Abdu'l-lāh Barlās and returned to Ḥiṣār.
Mahdī Sl. deserted to Samarkand when Babur was near Shīrāz and Kān-bāī.
Ḥamza Sl. left with leave granted from near Zamīn.
Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā's affairs were in a good way in the winter of 902 AH.
Mahdī Sl. led Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā's troops against `Abdu'l-karīm Ushrit.
Mahdī Sl. struck off `Abdu'l-karīm's hand at the wrist with his Chirkas sword.
The Aūzbeg sulṯāns went to Shaibānī after seeing the Tīmūrid Mīrzās' affairs tottering.
The Samarkandīs drew an army out against Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā.
Bāī-sunghar Mīrzā went to Sar-i-pul.
Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā went to Khwāja Kārzūn.
Khwāja Abū'l-makāram rode against Bukhārā at the instigation of Khwāja Munīr of Aūsh.
The invaders of Bukhārā could make no progress and retired.
It had been settled that Sulṯān `Alī Mīrzā and Babur would beleaguer Samarkand in the summer.
Babur rode out from Andijan in Ramzan (May) to besiege Samarkand.
Babur had previously arranged with Sl. Ali Mirza to meet that summer to besiege Samarkand, with Ali Mirza coming from Bukhara and Babur from Andijan.
Babur sent Tulun Khwaja Mughul ahead with 2 or 300 scouting men.
Babur's forces reached Shiraz two days after overtaking Bai-sunghar Mirza's rear.
Babur gave Shiraz into Ibrahim Saru's charge.
Babur made the Prayer of the Breaking of the Fast at Shiraz.
Babur dismounted in the reserve of Ab-i-yar.
Qasim Duldai, Wais Laghari, Hasan, and Sl. Muhammad Wais came to Babur with 3 or 400 men.
Babur had Mughuls arrested for senseless conduct toward village elders cut to pieces by Qasim Beg Quchin for discipline.
Babur crossed the Zar-afshan river and dismounted near Yam.
Babur's army plundered Muslim traders at Yam but restored all goods by the next day due to army discipline.
Babur lay at Khan Yurti for 40 or 50 days.
Babur sent a party to the Lovers' Cave based on a deceptive message from the fort, resulting in the deaths of four or five household foot-soldiers.
Babur moved out from Khan Yurti against Aurgut fort, which surrendered.
Babur pardoned the offences of Aurgut's defenders.
Babur dismounted in the Qulba meadow behind the Bagh-i-maidan.
Babur moved to the top of Qulba at the back of Kohik.
Babur ordered the execution with torture of Diwana the tunic-weaver and Kalqashuq in blood-retaliation for foot-soldiers killed at the Lovers' Cave.
Babur summoned the begs admitted to counsel and decided to move to winter-quarters at Khwaja Didar.
Babur marched to Khwaja Didar and dismounted in the meadow before it.
Babur moved into Khwaja Didar when quarters were ready.
Shaibani Khan rode light from Turkistan and stood over against Babur's camping-ground.
Babur formed up his available men and rode out against Shaibani Khan.
Shaibani Khan drew off towards Samarkand without engaging Babur.
Shaibani Khan turned back for Turkistan a few days later in disappointment.
Babur got to horse and started from Khwaja Didar for Samarkand when news of Bai-sunghar Mirza's departure reached him.
Babur entered the citadel of Samarkand and dismounted at the Bu-stan Sarai on one of the last ten days of the first Rabi (end of November 1497).
Babur took and occupied the town and country of Samarkand.
Babur ordered people to pace round the ramparts of Samarkand's walled-town, which came out at 10,000 steps.
Babur valued Samarkand as one of the most pleasant towns in the habitable world.
Babur believed Timur Beg made Samarkand his capital and that no ruler so great would ever have made it a capital before.
Babur considered Samarkandis to be orthodox, pure-in-the-Faith, law-abiding and religious.
Babur's men suffered great privation in the town due to lack of seed-corn and money.
Babur's soldiers deserted him in ones and twos, yearning for their homes.
Babur considered Aūzūn Ḥasan a traitor to his salt.
Babur had persistently led his army out while coveting Samarkand.
Babur ruled Samarkand for 100 days.
Babur had approximately 1000 men with him in Samarkand when others deserted.
Babur's father had favored Tūlūn Khwāja.
Babur raised Tūlūn Khwāja to the rank of beg.
Babur considered Tūlūn Khwāja a wonderfully bold and dashing brave.
Babur left Aūzūn Ḥasan and `Alī-dost T̤aghāī in Andijān when his army rode out for Samarkand.
Babur was recovering from illness when he received letters about the siege of Andijān.
Babur became so ill that for four days his speech was impeded.
Babur's speech impairment caused his followers to despair of his life.
Babur was moved by letters from his mothers and Khwāja-i-qāẓī to leave Samarkand for Andijān.
Babur left Samarkand for Andijān on a Saturday in Rajab after ruling 100 days in the town.
Babur reached Khujand on a Saturday.
Babur learned that `Alī-dost T̤aghāī had surrendered Andijān on the day Babur left Samarkand.
Babur believed Khwāja-i-qāẓī was a saint.
Babur had no doubt about Khwāja-i-qāẓī's sanctity.
Babur found it very hard and vexing to lose both Samarkand and Andijān.
Babur had never been cut off from his retainers and country since he had ruled.
Babur had never known such annoyance and hardship since he had known himself.
Babur cried a good deal when his begs and braves deserted him.
Babur had ambition for rule and desire of conquest.
Babur did not sit idle when affairs made no progress once or twice.
Babur had not seen Shāh Begīm and other relations for seven or eight years.
Babur appreciated the melon variety Ismā`īl Shaikhī for its delicate quality.
Babur valued the counsel of his Mughūl begs regarding military strategy.
Babur sent a large army under Muhammad Wali Beg to put down Muhammad Husain Mirza who had taken a hostile position against him.
Sl. Husain Mirza was surprised by Badi'u'z-zaman Mirza and Shah Shuja Beg while lying in the Nishin meadow near Harat.
Sl. Masud Mirza joined Sl. Husain Mirza on the same day as the surprise attack.
Sl. Husain Mirza looked kindly on Sl. Masud Mirza and made him a son-in-law.
Sl. Masud Mirza left Sl. Husain Mirza's service without asking leave at the instigation of Baqi Chaghaniami.
Khusrau Shah blinded Sl. Masud Mirza with a lancet.
Babur moved out of Khujand twice, once for Andijan and once for Samarkand.
Babur returned to Khujand twice because his work was not opened out.
Khujand is a poor place where a man with 200 or 300 followers would have a hard time.
Babur wished to return to Samarkand.
Babur asked Muhammad Husain Kurkan Dughiat for the loan of Pashaghar for the winter.
Babur had fever when reaching Zamin.
Despite his fever, Babur rode 14 or 15 yighach (70 to 80 miles) to Pashaghar.
Babur appointed Ibrahim Saru, Wais Laghari, Sherim Taghai and others to make an expedition amongst the Yar-yilaq forts.
Yar-yilaq was Sayyid Yusuf Beg's territory at that time.
Sayyid Yusuf Beg had sent his younger brother's son Ahmad-i-yusuf to manage the forts.
During that winter, Babur's begs and braves got possession of some forts peacefully and fought and took others.
Sl. Ali Mirza became suspicious of Sayyid Yusuf and his nephew on Babur's account.
Babur had 200 or 300 soldiers when powerful foes were on every side.
Fortune had not favoured Babur when he turned to Andijan.
Babur and his families and following had been in Khujand for half a year.
The Muslims of Khujand had borne Babur's charges and served him to the best of their power.
Babur went to the summer-pastures in the south of Aura-tipa.
Khwaja Abu'l-makaram came to see Babur and was touched with compassion for his state.
Babur was much touched by Khwaja Abu'l-makaram's compassion and pitied him.
A horseman named Yul-chuq appeared with a message from Ali-dost Taghai.
Ali-dost Taghai offered to give Babur Marghiyan and submit to him.
Babur hurried to Marghiyan at sunset without reflecting or delaying.
The distance from Babur's location to Marghiyan was 24 or 25 yighach of road.
Babur rushed through the night to Marghiyan without delaying anywhere.
Babur halted at Tang-ab to cool down his horses and give them corn.
Wais Beg and others represented to Babur their anxiety about Ali-dost's character.
Babur and his companions agreed to continue to Marghiyan despite their fears.
Babur reached Fort Marghiyan at the Sunnat Prayer.
Ali-dost Taghai kept himself behind the closed gate and asked for terms.
Ali-dost Taghai did Babur obeisance between the two gates.
Babur had 240 men with him when he reached Marghiyan.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal had been tyrannical and oppressive.
All the clans of the country were asking for Babur because of Auzun Hasan and Tambal's tyranny.
Babur joined over a hundred men to Qasim Beg to bring over the hill-people of the south of Andijan.
Ibrahim Saru and Wais Laghari and Sayyidi Qara were sent to cross the Khujand-water.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal gathered soldiers and Mughuls and called up men accustomed to serve in the Andijan and Akhsi armies.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal brought Jahangir Mirza with them to Sapan.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal came to Sapan with the intention of besieging Marghiyan.
Though few men were left with Babur after the departure of the Commanders, those there were formed up and sallied out.
Babur's men prevented the enemy from advancing beyond the suburbs.
Page Khalil, the turban-twister, went well forward and got his hand into the work.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal failed to get near the fort on two other days.
When Qasim Beg went into the hills on the south of Andijan, all the Ashpari, Turugshir, Chikrak, and the peasants and highland and lowland clans came in for Babur.
When Ibrahim Saru and Wais Laghari crossed the river to the Akhsi side, Pap and several other forts came in.
Hasan-dikcha gathered together his own following and a body of the Akhsi mob and rabble.
Hasan-dikcha's men black-bludgeoned Auzun Hasan's and Tambal's men in the outer fort.
Hasan-dikcha's men drubbed Auzun Hasan's and Tambal's men into the citadel.
Hasan-dikcha and his men invited Ibrahim Saru, Wais Laghari and Sayyidi Qara and admitted them into the fort.
Sl. Mahmud Khan appointed Banda-ali and Haji Ghazi Manghit to help Babur.
Haji Ghazi Manghit was a fugitive from Shaibani Khan at that time.
Sl. Mahmud Khan also appointed the Barin tuman with its begs to help Babur.
Auzun Hasan started off his most favoured retainers and most serviceable braves to help his men in the citadel of Akhsi.
Auzun Hasan's force reached the brow of the river at dawn.
Babur's Commanders and the Tashkint Mughuls had heard of Auzun Hasan's force's approach.
Auzun Hasan's men did not draw the ferry-boat up-stream in their haste.
Auzun Hasan's men went right away from the landing-place and could not cross for the fort.
Babur's men and the Tashkint Mughuls began to ride bare-back into the water from both banks.
Qarluqhach Bakhshi called one of Mughul Beg's sons to him and killed him.
Babur's men seized and killed most of those in the boat.
Of Auzun Hasan's confidants, Qarluqhach Bakhshi, Khalil Diwan and Qazi Ghulam escaped.
Sayyid Ali, Haidar-i-quli and Qilka Kashghari escaped from Auzun Hasan's trusted braves.
Of Auzun Hasan's 70 or 80 men, no more than five or six got free.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal marched in haste and disorder for Andijan after hearing of the affair.
Nasir Beg was the husband of Auzun Hasan's sister.
Nasir Beg was an experienced man and brave.
Nasir Beg made Andijan fast and sent a man to Babur.
Auzun Hasan and Tambal broke up in disaccord when they found the fort made fast against them.
Auzun Hasan drew off to his wife in Akhsi.
Tambal drew off to his district of Aush.
A few of Jahangir Mirza's household and braves fled with him from Auzun Hasan and joined Tambal.
Babur rode out from Marghiyan at sun-rise and was in Andijan by mid-day.
Babur saw Nasir Beg and his two sons, Dost Beg and Mirim Beg, in Andijan.
Babur questioned Nasir Beg and his sons and uplifted their heads with hope of favour and kindness.
Babur's father's country, lost to him for two years, was regained in the month Dhu'l-qa'da of 904 (June 1498).
Sl. Ahmad Tambal, after being joined by Jahangir Mirza, drew away for Aush.
The red rabble in Aush black-bludgeoned and drubbed Tambal's men out.
The red rabble in Aush kept the fort for Babur and sent him a man.
Jahangir and Tambal went off confounded with a few followers only.
Jahangir and Tambal entered Auzkint Fort.
Auzun Hasan went to Akhsi after failing to get into Andijan.
Auzun Hasan entered the citadel of Akhsi.
Babur set out for Akhsi without more than four or five days' delay in Andijan.
Auzun Hasan asked for peace and terms and surrendered the fort to Babur.
Babur stayed in Akhsi a few days to settle its affairs and those of Kasan and that country-side.
Babur gave the Mughuls who had come to help him leave for return to Tashkint.
Babur went back to Andijan, taking with him Auzun Hasan and his family and dependants.
Qasim-i-ajab was left in Akhsi for a time.
Auzun Hasan was allowed to go by the Qara-tigin road for Hisar without hurt to life or goods.
A few of Auzun Hasan's retainers went with him, the rest stayed behind.
Auzun Hasan's retainers had captured and plundered various Muslim dependants of Babur and the Khwaja.
Babur and several begs agreed to order Auzun Hasan's retainers to give back goods of theirs that were known to be with Babur's men.
Babur now understands that the order to take back goods was a little hasty.
Babur recognizes that with a worry like Jahangir seated at his side, there was no sense in frightening people.
Babur believes that in conquest and government, 100,000 reflections are right and necessary as to the bearings of each decision.
Babur's incautious order caused troubles and rebellions.
Babur's ill-considered order was the cause of his second exile from Andijan.
The Mughuls gave way to anxiety and fear from Babur's order.
The Mughuls marched through Rabatik-aurchi for Auzkint.
Babur issued an order allowing his men to take back goods that belonged to them from conquered territories.
Babur later recognized that his order to allow men to take back their goods was hasty and ill-considered.
Babur believed that conquest and government require careful reflection on the consequences of decisions.
Babur's hasty order caused troubles and rebellions that led to his second exile from Andijan.
Babur's mother had 1500 to 2000 Mughuls in her service.
The Mughul horde rebelled against Babur five times.
Babur believed the Mughuls rebelled not because of poor relations with him but due to their nature, as they had done the same with their own Khans.
Babur favored Khudai-birdi Buqaq among the Mughuls.
Quli Chunaq's father was Khudai-birdi Buqaq.
Quli Chunaq left the rebel Mughuls to bring Babur news of the rebellion.
Babur considered Quli Chunaq's later shameful action to be a product of his Mughul nature.
Babur's begs advised him that the Mughul rebellion was a trifling matter not requiring his personal attention.
Qasim Beg led forces against the Mughul rebels.
Qasim Beg fought Muhammad Arghun and attempted to cut off his head.
Several of Babur's begs were killed in the battle against the Mughuls, including Ali-darwesh Beg and Mirim Laghari.
Samad, Ibrahim Saru's younger brother, was mortally wounded by Shah-suwar in combat.
Shah-suwar was killed by Samad despite being mortally wounded himself.
Babur considered the defeat by the Mughuls to be unseasonably timed.
Tambal brought Jahangir Mirza to attack Andijan.
Tambal camped 2 miles from Andijan in the meadow in front of the Hill of Pleasure.
Tambal attempted to advance past Chihil-dukhteran but was repelled by Babur's forces.
Tambal killed two captured begs, Mirim Laghari and Tuqa Beg.
Tambal laid siege to Andijan for nearly a month before retiring to Aush.
Babur sent commissaries to gather horse and foot soldiers from district armies.
Babur put his trust in God when setting out against Tambal on Muharram 18th.
Babur stayed at Hafiz Beg's Four-gardens to complete equipment preparations.
Babur formed his army with right and left wings, centre, and van.
Tambal attempted to enter Andijan at night but was discovered by the garrison.
Tambal stationed his younger brother Khalil with 200 to 300 men in the fort of Madu.
The fort of Madu was renowned for its strength.
Stones thrown from Madu were larger than any Babur had encountered in previous sieges.
Abdu'l-qasim Kohbur was struck by a large stone from Madu but recovered and rode away.
Yar-ali Balal was struck on the head by a stone from Madu and required trepanning.
The assault on Madu began at dawn and the water-thief was taken before breakfast.
Babur took 60 to 70 or 80 men from Khalil's command and sent them to Andijan.
Babur dismounted at Unjü-tüpa after taking Madu.
Tambal was about one yighach (5 miles) away from Babur's position.
Qaṃbar-ali went into Aush due to sickness.
Babur's camp lay at Unjü-tüpa for a month or forty days with daily skirmishes.
Babur had a ditch dug around his camp and set branches close together where no ditch existed.
Babur's soldiers went out in mail along the ditch at night.
Night alarms occurred every two or three days in Babur's camp.
Sayyidi Beg Taghai was captured by the enemy while foraging.
Khusrau Shah invited Bai-sunghar Mirza to go with him against Balkh.
Khusrau Shah murdered Bai-sunghar Mirza at the Aubaj ferry.
Khusrau Shah bowstrung Bai-sunghar Mirza on the 10th of Muharram.
Khusrau Shah killed several of Bai-sunghar Mirza's begs and household.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was born in 882 AH (1477 AD) in the Hisar district.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was the second son of Sultan Mahmud Mirza.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's mother was Pasha Begim.
Bai-sunghar Mirza had large eyes, a fleshy face, and Turkman features.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was of middle height and an elegant young man at age 22.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was just, humane, and pleasant-natured.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's tutor was Sayyid Mahmud, presumably a Shia.
Bai-sunghar Mirza became infected with Shia heresy through his tutor.
Bai-sunghar Mirza reverted from Shia belief to the pure Faith in Samarkand.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was much addicted to wine.
Bai-sunghar Mirza performed prayers on his non-drinking days.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was moderate in gifts and liberality.
Bai-sunghar Mirza wrote the naskh-talik character very well.
Bai-sunghar Mirza was skilled in painting.
Bai-sunghar Mirza made Adili his pen-name and composed good verses.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's odes were held in such repute in Samarkand that they were found in most houses.
Bai-sunghar Mirza fought two ranged battles.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's first battle was against Sultan Mahmud Khan near Kan-bai in 900 AH (1495 AD).
Bai-sunghar Mirza defeated Sultan Mahmud Khan and beheaded 3 or 4000 Mughuls.
Haidar Kukulash, the Khan's looser and binder, died in Bai-sunghar Mirza's first battle.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's second battle was fought near Bukhara with Sultan Ali Mirza in 901 AH (1496 AD).
Bai-sunghar Mirza was beaten in his second battle.
Sultan Mahmud Mirza gave Bukhara to Bai-sunghar Mirza.
After Sultan Mahmud Mirza's death, his begs made Bai-sunghar Mirza ruler in Samarkand.
Bukhara went out of Bai-sunghar Mirza's hands after the Turkhan rebellion in 901 AH (1496 AD).
Khusrau Shah took Hisar and gave it to Bai-sunghar Mirza.
Bai-sunghar Mirza left no child.
Bai-sunghar Mirza took a daughter of his paternal uncle Sultan Khalil Mirza as his wife.
Bai-sunghar Mirza had no other wife or concubine.
Bai-sunghar Mirza never ruled with independent authority.
Bai-sunghar Mirza's begs were those of his father and paternal uncle Ahmad.
Sultan Ahmad Qarawal sent word to Babur of his intention to join him.
Sultan Ahmad Qarawal came to Babur from Hisar through the Qara-tigin country with his family and dependants.
Qaṃbar-ali recovered from sickness and arrived to join Babur.
Babur took the arrival of Sultan Ahmad Qarawal and his party as a happy omen.
Tambal made no stand at Ab-i-khan and left many tents and baggage.
Tambal went to the village of Khuban with Jahangir, about 3 yighach from Babur's position.
Babur's right wing was commanded by Ali-dost and his dependants.
Babur's left wing included Ibrahim Saru, Wais Laghari, Sayyidi Qara, Muhammad-ali Mubashir, and Kichik Beg.
Sultan Ahmad Qarawal and Quch Beg were inscribed in Babur's left wing.
Qasim Beg Quchini was with Babur in the centre.
Qaṃbar-ali (the Skinner) was in the van with some of the household.
The enemy came out of Khuban arrayed to fight near the village of Saqa.
Babur's foot-soldiers with mantelets were in the rear during the battle.
Kichik Beg fought very well in the battle.
Muhammad Ali Mubashir ranked next to Kichik Beg.
The enemy took to flight when unable to bring equal zeal against Babur's forces.
Babur ordered the heads of all captured enemy braves to be struck off.
Qasim Beg and Ali-dost advised caution and did not pursue far.
Babur dismounted in Khuban village after the battle.
This was Babur's first ranged battle.
Babur attributed his victory to God's favour and mercy.
Babur's grandmother Shah Sultan Begim arrived from Andijan the day after the battle.
Shah Sultan Begim came to beg off Jahangir Mirza if he had been taken.
Babur decided not to move against Tambal in Auzikint due to approaching winter.
Babur returned to Andijan after the battle.
Babur decided to winter near Armyan and Nush-ab in the Rabatik-aurchini.
The hunting grounds near Armyan and Nush-ab were good.
The jungle near the Ailash river contained much bughu-maral and pig.
The scattered jungle clumps were thick with hare and pheasant.
The rising ground near the winter quarters had many foxes of fine colour.
The foxes in that region were swifter than those of any other place.
Babur used to ride hunting every two or three days during winter quarters.
Babur would beat through the great jungle and hunt bughu-maral.
Babur decided to winter near Armiyān and Nūsh-āb in the Rabāṯik-aūrchīnī rather than in Andijān.
The hunting grounds near Armiyān and Nūsh-āb had good game including būghū-marāl, pig, hare, pheasant, and foxes.
Babur hunted every two or three days while in winter quarters.
Babur considered the foxes in that region to be of fine colour and swifter than those of any other place.
Khudāī-bīrdī Tūghchī had recently been favored with beg's rank.
Babur's forces raided the enemy and drove in his herds of horses.
Babur believed that if the whole winter had been passed in those quarters, the enemy would have broken up without a fight.
Qaṃbar-`alī asked leave to go to his district while the enemy was growing weak.
Babur considered Qaṃbar-`alī to be fickle and veering.
Qaṃbar-`alī held the districts of Khujand, Asfara, and Kand-i-badām.
Qaṃbar-`alī had the largest districts and most followers among Babur's begs.
Babur spent 40 or 50 days in the winter quarters.
Aḥmad Beg was the paternal uncle of the full-blood of Sl. Muḥammad Sl. and guardian of The Khān's son.
Beg Tīlba was the elder brother of full-blood of Taṃbal and The Khān's Lord of the Gate.
Beg Tīlba had been in Mughūlistān from birth and had grown up amongst Mughūls.
Beg Tīlba had never entered a cultivated country or served the rulers of one.
Qāsim-i-`ajab was captured by Taṃbal's men while pursuing marauders near Bachrātā.
The Khān appointed his son Sulṯānīm and Aḥmad Beg with 5 or 6000 men to help Taṃbal.
Babur rode to oppose Taṃbal's reinforcements without waiting for his absent men.
The cold during Babur's night march was so bitter that it bit the hands and feet of several men and swelled their ears.
Babur left Yārak T̤aghāī in Akhsī temporarily in Qāsim-i-`ajab's place.
Aḥmad Beg and Sulṯānīm hurried off in disorder upon hearing of Babur's approach.
Babur wanted to attack Taṃbal immediately but was dissuaded by Wais Lāgharī and others.
Taṃbal escaped to Fort Archīān during the night.
Babur camped two miles from Fort Archīān for 30 or 40 days.
Taṃbal's forces made one night-attack on Babur's camp, raining arrows and then retiring.
Qaṃbar-`alī attempted to leave for his district multiple times while Babur was camped near Fort Archīān.
Sayyid Yūsuf of Macham was the head-man of one of the two foothills of Andijān.
Sayyid Yūsuf had revolted from Taṃbal to Babur and from Babur to Taṃbal multiple times between June 1499 and February 1500.
Babur considered Sayyid Yūsuf to be singularly hypocritical and of no standing.
Qūch Beg and his brothers performed well in combat near Bīshkhārān.
`Alī-dost and Qaṃbar-`alī, the Skinner, were unfriendly begs who interposed with talk of peace.
Babur and his well-wishers were utterly averse from making peace with Taṃbal.
Babur made peace with Taṃbal because `Alī-dost and Qaṃbar-`alī were great begs whose opposition could cause problems.
The peace agreement stipulated that districts on the Akhsī side of the Khujand-water would depend on Jahāngīr.
The peace agreement stipulated that districts on the Andijān side of the Khujand-water would depend on Babur.
Aūzkīnt was to be left in Babur's jurisdiction after Taṃbal's forces removed their families.
Babur and Jahāngīr agreed to march together against Samarkand.
Andijān was to be given to Jahāngīr when Babur was in possession of Samarkand.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā and Taṃbal came and did obeisance to Babur after the peace agreement.
Khalīl-of-Taṃbal and Babur's prisoners were released and given robes of honour.
Babur's begs including T̤aghāī Beg, Muḥammad-dost, Mīr Shāh Qūchīn, Sayyidī Qarā Beg, Qāsim-i-`ajab, Mīr Wais, and Mīrīm Dīwān were released by Taṃbal.
`Alī-dost's manners and behaviour changed entirely after Babur's return to Andijān.
`Alī-dost dismissed Khalīfa and seized and plundered Ibrāhīm Sārū and Wais Lāgharī.
`Alī-dost deprived Ibrāhīm and Wais of their districts without fault or cause.
`Alī-dost declared that Khalīfa and Ibrāhīm were in sympathy about Khwāja-i-qāẓī.
`Alī-dost's son Muḥammad-dost set himself up on a regal footing with receptions, a public table, a court, and workshops.
`Alī-dost and his son acted improperly because they had Taṃbal at their backs.
Babur had no authority to restrain `Alī-dost and Muḥammad-dost's misdeeds.
Babur endured many humiliations from `Alī-dost and his son without saying a word.
`Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm was betrothed to Babur by his father and hers, Sl. Aḥmad Mīrzā.
`Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm came to Khujand and Babur took her in the month of Sha`bān.
Babur was not ill-disposed towards `Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm.
Babur saw `Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm once in 10, 15 or 20 days due to modesty and bashfulness.
Babur's first inclination towards `Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm did not last.
Babur's mother Khānīm sent him to `Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm once a month or every 40 days with driving and dunnings.
Babur discovered in himself a strange inclination for a boy named Bāburī in the camp-bazar.
Babur had no inclination for anyone before Bāburī and had not heard or talked of love and desire.
Babur composed Persian couplets about his love for Bāburī.
Babur could never look straight at Bāburī due to modesty and bashfulness.
Babur could not thank Bāburī for coming or reproach him for going away.
Babur once became so confused upon meeting Bāburī face to face that he almost went right off.
Babur wandered bare-head and bare-foot through streets, lanes, orchards and vineyards while in passion for Bāburī.
Babur showed civility neither to friend nor stranger while afflicted with love for Bāburī.
Babur took no care for himself or others while in passion for Bāburī.
Babur sometimes wandered alone over hill and plain like a madman.
Babur sometimes betook himself to gardens and suburbs, lane by lane.
Babur's wandering was not of his choice.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā fell out with Muḥammad Mazīd Tarkhān in this same year.
The Tarkhāns had risen to over-much predominance and honour.
Bāqī took the whole revenue of the Bukhārā Government and gave nothing to anyone else.
Muḥammad Mazīd had control in Samarkand and took all its districts for his sons and dependants.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā and some of his household formed a design against Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān left Samarkand with his following and sympathetic begs.
Sl. Ḥusain Arghūn, Pīr Aḥmad, Khwāja Ḥusain, Qarā Barlās, and Ṣāliḥ Muḥammad left Samarkand with Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān.
The Khān appointed Khān Mīrzā with Mughūl begs including Muḥ. Ḥusain Dūghlāt and Aḥmad Beg to act against Samarkand.
Ḥāfiẓ Beg Dūldāī and his son T̤āhir Beg were Khān Mīrzā's guardians.
Ḥasan and Hindū Beg fled from Sl. `Alī Mīrzā to Khān Mīrzā because of relationship to the guardians.
Muḥammad Mazīd Tarkhān invited Khān Mīrzā and the Mughūl army to meet near Shavdār.
The Mughūls seemed to have thought of making Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān a prisoner.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān and his begs separated themselves from the Mughūl army upon learning of this.
The Mughūls retired without Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān because they could make no stand without him.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā caught up with the Mughūls at Yār-yīlāq and routed them.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's rout of the Mughūls was his one good little affair in his last days.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān and his people despaired of both the Mughūls and the Mīrzās.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān sent Mīr Mughūl to invite Babur to Samarkand.
Mīr Mughūl had already been in Babur's service.
Mīr Mughūl had risked his life in good accord with Khwāja-i-qāẓī during the siege of Andijān.
Babur resolved to move on Samarkand because of the business with Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān.
Babur had made peace with Jahāngīr for the purpose of moving on Samarkand.
Babur sent Mīr Mughūl to give rendezvous to Jahāngīr Mīrzā.
Babur rode out in the month of Ẕū'l-qa`da (June) and came to Qabā with two halts on the way.
News came that Taṃbal's brother Khalīl had taken Aūsh by surprise.
Khalīl had been set free when peace was made with Taṃbal.
Taṃbal sent Khalīl to fetch away their wives and families from Aūzkīnt.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān and his people sent Mīr Mughūl to invite Babur to Samarkand.
Mīr Mughūl had previously been in Babur's service.
Mīr Mughūl risked his life during the siege of Andijān.
Babur made peace with Jahāngīr.
Babur resolved to move on Samarkand.
Babur rode out in the month of Ẕū'l-qa`da.
Khalīl took Aūsh by surprise.
Babur's men of name had scattered to their homes to prepare military equipment.
Babur had relied on the peace and was off his guard against treachery.
The misconduct of begs `Alī-dost and Qaṃbar-`alī had caused Babur to lose confidence in them.
Babur preferred Samarkand to Andijān as a capital.
Qūch Beg's father, Sl. Aḥmad, could not attach himself to Babur due to various ties and attachments.
Qūch Beg went with Babur while his father stayed behind.
Qāsim Beg Qūchīn, `Alī-dost, and Sayyid Qāsim joined Babur in Mahan.
Qaṃbar-`alī went to Akhsī to discuss army-matters with Taṃbal.
Taṃbal laid hands on Qaṃbar-`alī and marched against his district.
Qaṃbar-`alī escaped on foot and reached Aūrā-tīpā.
Shaibānī Khān beat Bāqī Tarkhān in Dabūsī.
Shaibānī Khān was moving on Bukhārā.
Babur placed Qaṃbar-`alī in Sangzār after he came to Babur.
The Samarkand begs under Muḥ. Mazīd Tarkhān came and did Babur obeisance.
Khwāja Yaḥya wished for Babur as pādshāh.
Babur sent his librarian, Khwāja Muḥammad `Alī, to Khwāja Yaḥya.
Khwāja Yaḥya agreed to give Samarkand to Babur.
Sl. Muḥammad Dūldāī's father, Sl. Maḥmūd, fled from Babur's camp and gave information to Sl. `Alī's party.
Ibrāhīm Sārū came and did Babur obeisance.
Muḥ. Yūsuf, the elder son of Sayyid Yūsuf, came to Babur.
`Alī-dost had plundered and driven away Ibrāhīm Sārū.
`Alī-dost had harassed and persecuted Babur and his well-wishers with Taṃbal's backing.
Babur's nature sorted ill with `Alī-dost.
`Alī-dost asked leave from Babur and Babur gave it.
`Alī-dost and his son Muḥammad-dost went to Taṃbal's presence.
`Alī-dost and Muḥammad-dost became Taṃbal's intimates.
`Alī-dost died from ulceration of the hand.
Muḥammad-dost went amongst the Aūzbegs.
Muḥammad-dost committed treachery to his salt and fled to the Andijān foot-hills.
Muḥammad-dost stirred up much revolt and trouble in the Andijān foot-hills.
Muḥammad-dost was blinded by Aūzbeg people.
Babur sent Ghūrī Barlās toward Bukhārā for news.
Shaibānī Khān had taken Bukhārā.
Shaibānī Khān was on his way to Samarkand.
Babur set out for Kesh.
Most of the Samarkand begs' families were in Kesh.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā gave Samarkand to Shaibānī Khān.
Zuhra Begī Āghā, Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's mother, secretly wrote to Shaibānī Khān.
Zuhra Begī Āghā promised Shaibānī Khān that her son would give him Samarkand if he would take her as wife.
Sayyid Yūsuf Arghūn knew of the plan to give Samarkand to Shaibānī Khān.
Sayyid Yūsuf Arghūn was the traitor who invented the plan.
Shaibānī Khān went to Samarkand and dismounted in the Garden of the Plain.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā went out to Shaibānī Khān without consulting his begs or braves.
Shaibānī Khān did not receive Sl. `Alī Mīrzā very favourably.
Shaibānī Khān seated Sl. `Alī Mīrzā on his less honourable hand.
Khwāja Yaḥya became very anxious when he heard of Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's departure.
Khwāja Yaḥya went out to Shaibānī Khān.
Shaibānī Khān showed Khwāja Yaḥya respect by rising when the Khwāja rose to leave.
Jān-`alī heard of Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's going to Shaibānī Khān and also went.
Shaibānī Khān did not care for Zuhra Begī Āghā.
Shaibānī Khān did not regard Zuhra Begī Āghā as the equal of a mistress or concubine.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's repentance was extreme.
Some of Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's close circle planned for him to escape but he would not agree.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā was killed in Plough-meadow three or four days after dismounting in Tīmūr Sulṯān's quarters.
Sl. `Alī Mīrzā died with a bad name.
Shaibānī Khān sent Jān-`alī after Sl. `Alī Mīrzā's Mīrzā.
Shaibānī Khān dismissed Khwāja Yaḥya and his two sons toward Khurāsān.
Khwāja Yaḥya and his two sons were martyred near Khwāja Kārdzan.
Shaibānī Khān claimed that Qaṃbar Bī and Kūpuk Bī killed Khwāja Yaḥya, not him.
Babur left Kesh and went in the direction of Ḥiṣār.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tārkhān and the Samarkand begs parted from Babur in the Chultū meadow of Chaghānīān.
Muḥ. Mazīd Tārkhān and the Samarkand begs became retainers of Khusrau Shāh.
Babur was cut off from his own abiding-town and country.
Babur was obliged to traverse Khusrau Shāh's districts despite the misery he had inflicted on Babur's dynasty.
Babur had planned to go to Alacha Khān by way of Qarā-tīgīn and the Alāī but this was not managed.
A servant of Khusrau Shāh brought Babur one set of nine horses and one of nine pieces of cloth.
Sher-`alī deserted to Khusrau Shāh's brother, Walī.
Qūch Beg parted from Babur and went to Ḥiṣār.
Many horses and camels were left on the steep and narrow roads of the Kām valley.
The Sara-tāq pass was steep and narrow with ravines and precipices.
Amongst the Fān mountains is a large lake called Iskandar.
The lake Iskandar is 2 miles in circumference.
Ibrāhīm Tarkhān had strengthened Fort Shīrāz and was seated in it.
Qaṃbar-`alī and Abū'l-qāsim Kohbur came into Yār-yīlāq and strengthened its lower forts.
The head-man of Fān had a reputation for hospitality, generosity, serviceableness and kindness.
The head-man of Fān had given tribute of 70 or 80 horses to Sl. Mas`ūd Mīrzā.
The head-man of Fān sent Babur one second-rate horse and did not wait on him himself.
Those renowned for liberality became misers when they had to do with Babur.
Khusrau Shāh was celebrated for liberality and kindness.
Khusrau Shāh showed great generosity to Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā.
Khusrau Shāh showed great generosity to Bāqī Tarkhān and other begs.
Babur passed through Khusrau Shāh's country twice.
Khusrau Shāh showed less regard for Babur than for Babur's lowest servants.
Babur made an excursion to Keshtūd under the impression that the Aūzbegs were there.
Keshtūd seemed to have gone to ruin with no one occupying it.
Babur sent a few begs under Qāsim Qūchīn to surprise Rabāṯ-i-khwāja.
The men within Rabāṯ-i-khwāja forced Babur's begs to retire.
Qaṃbar-`alī was holding Sangzār and came to see Babur.
Abū'l-qāsim Kohbur and Ibrāhīm Tarkhān showed loyalty and attachment by sending efficient men for Babur's service.
Babur went into Asfīdik, one of the Yār-yīlāq villages.
Shaibāq Khān lay near Khwāja Dīdār with 3 or 4000 Aūzbegs and as many more soldiers gathered locally.
Shaibāq Khān had given the Government of Samarkand to Jān-wafā.
Jān-wafā was in the fort with 500 or 600 men.
Ḥamza Sl. and Mahdī Sl. were lying near the fort in the Quail-reserve.
Babur's men, good and bad, numbered 240.
Babur discussed the position with all his begs and unmailed braves.
Babur believed the Samarkandīs would not be attached to Shaibānī Khān.
Babur believed the Samarkandīs would be for him if he made an effort at once.
Babur believed the Samarkandīs would not fight him for the Aūzbegs.
Babur rode out of Yār-yīlāq after the Mid-day Prayer to attack Samarkand.
The Samarkandīs knew of Babur's coming.
Babur made straight back from Khān-yūrtī.
Babur crossed the Kohik-water below Rabāṯ-i-khwāja and returned to Yār-yīlāq.
Dost-i-nāṣir, Nuyān Kūkūldāsh, Khān-qulī-i-Karīm-dād, Shaikh Darwesh, and Mīrīm-i-nāṣir were sitting in Babur's presence in Fort Asfīdik.
Babur asked when they would take Samarkand.
Some said Babur would take Samarkand in the heats.
It was late in autumn when this conversation took place.
Others said Babur would take Samarkand in a month, forty days, or twenty days.
Nuyān Kūkūldāsh said Babur would take Samarkand in 14 days.
Babur took Samarkand in exactly 14 days.
Babur had a wonderful dream in which Khwāja `Ubaid'l-lāh appeared.
In Babur's dream, Khwāja `Ubaid'l-lāh lifted Babur up by his arm.
In Babur's dream, Khwāja `Ubaid'l-lāh said 'Shaikh Maṣlaḥat has given Samarkand.'
Babur took Samarkand a few days after his dream.
Babur moved from Fort Asfīdik to Fort Wasmand.
Babur made another expedition to Samarkand despite his first approach revealing his plan.
Babur put his trust in God for the second expedition to Samarkand.
Babur rode out of Fort Wasmand after the Mid-day Prayer with Khwāja Abū'l-makāram.
Babur reached the Deep-fosse-bridge in the Avenue by mid-night.
Babur sent forward a detachment of 70 or 80 good men to set up ladders opposite the Lovers'-cave.
Babur was 19 years old when he took Samarkand.
Babur took Samarkand by surprise through a military expedition.
Babur entered Samarkand through the Turquoise Gate after his men opened it.
Babur's first wife was Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm, daughter of Sl. Aḥmad Mīrzā.
Babur's first child was a daughter named Fakhru'n-nisā' born to him by Āyisha-sulṯān Begīm.
Babur's first-born daughter died within a month or 40 days of her birth.
Samarkand had been the capital of Babur's dynasty for nearly 140 years.
Babur took Samarkand with 240 proved men.
Babur composed Turkī couplets and quatrains.
Babur exchanged letters with Āli-sher Beg.
Babur had Mullā Binā'ī fetched back after he came to Samarkand, despite Qāsim Beg's suspicions.
Babur fought Shaibāq Khān at the battle of Sar-i-pul.
Babur was eager to engage in battle at Sar-i-pul because of the position of the Eight stars.
Babur later understood that his haste to fight at Sar-i-pul was without reason.
Babur was defeated at the battle of Sar-i-pul.
Babur escaped from the battle of Sar-i-pul by crossing the Kohik-water with his horse in mail.
Babur cut off his horse-armour after crossing the Kohik-water.
Babur reached the citadel of Samarkand in the middle of the afternoon after his defeat.
Ibrāhīm Tarkhān, Ibrāhīm Sārū, and Ibrāhīm Jānī died in the battle of Sar-i-pul.
Babur valued truth and accuracy in his writing.
Babur trusted in God during his military campaigns.
Babur sought aid and reinforcement from khāns, sulṯāns, begs, and marchers after taking Samarkand.
Babur took precautions to fortify his camp with ditch and branch before the battle of Sar-i-pul.
Babur exerted himself greatly in preparations for the battle of Sar-i-pul.
Babur's Younger Khan dada bestowed on him arms, a horse, and a Mughal head-to-foot dress.
Babur traveled to Tashkent to meet his Elder Khan dada.
The Younger Khan performed nine knee bends before the Elder Khan as a sign of respect.
The Younger Khan brought between 1000 and 2000 men with him.
The Younger Khan was a man of singular manners and a mighty master of the sword.
The Younger Khan was brave.
The Younger Khan preferred to trust to the sword among all arms.
The Younger Khan believed the sword was superior to other weapons because it works from point to hilt.
The Younger Khan never parted with his keen-edged sword.
The Younger Khan was a little rustic and rough-of-speech from growing up in an out-of-the-way place.
Khwaja Abu'l-makaram did not recognize Babur when he was adorned in Mughal dress.
The Khan led out an army for Andijan against Ahmad Tambal.
The Khans numbered their men and reckoned them up to be 30,000.
The Khans decided to have Babur cross the Khujand-water and turn Tambal's rear.
Babur was joined by Ayub Begchik, Jan-hasan Barin, Muhammad Hisari Dughlat, Salim Husain Dughlat, Salim Ahmad Mirza Dughlat, and Qambar-ali Beg.
Sariagh-bash Mirza Itarchi was the commandant of the force joined to Babur.
Babur crossed the river on rafts near Sakan.
Babur reached Aush at dawn and those in it surrendered.
The hordes and highland and lowland tribes of southern and eastern Andijan came in to Babur.
The Auzikint people sent a man to Babur and came in.
Auzikint formerly must have been a capital of Farghana.
Auzikint has an excellent fort and is situated on the boundary of Farghana.
