The Marghimanis came in after two or three days, having beaten and chased their commandant.
Except Andijan, every fort south of the Khujand-water had come in to Babur.
Tambal sat down with an army of horse and foot, fortified with ditch and branch, to face The Khans between Karnan and Akhsi.
There was little fighting between Tambal's forces and The Khans' forces with no decided success to either side.
Most of the tribes and hordes and forts and all the districts in the Andijan country had come in to Babur.
Babur rode out from Aush at night with the idea of getting the townspeople of Andijan to let them in.
By midnight Babur's party was opposite Forty-daughters, 2 miles from Andijan.
Babur sent Qambar-ali Beg forward to discuss matters with the Khwaja of Andijan.
A war-cry and sound of drums suddenly arose while Babur's men were waiting.
Babur's men took flight without looking to one another.
Mir Shah Quchin, Baba Sher-zad, and Nasir's Dost sprang forward with Babur.
Babur shot at a man on a horse with a starred forehead and it rolled over and died.
Babur went three or four times to try to rally his men but all in vain.
Babur and his three companions kept the foe in check with arrows.
The foe pursued Babur's party two or three kuroh as far as the rising ground opposite Kharabuk and Pashamun.
Muhammad Ali Mubashir met Babur during the retreat.
Some of Ayub Begchik's Mughuls had slipped away from Aush to raid near Andijan.
There was confusion about the pass-word during the encounter near Andijan.
The pass-words settled on for use were Tashkent and Sairam.
Khwaja Muhammad Ali was somewhat in advance of Babur's party and got bewildered.
Khwaja Muhammad Ali was a Sart person.
The Mughuls took Khwaja Muhammad Ali for an enemy and raised their war-cry.
Pass-words are of two kinds: one for use in each tribe and one for the use of the whole army.
Tambal and his adherents lost heart and footing through the return of forts to Babur.
Tambal's army and people began to desert him in the next five or six days.
Salim Muhammad Galpuk was in Andijan as the younger of Tambal's cadet brothers.
Babur took the Mulberry-road and at the Mid-day Prayer came to the Khakan canal south of Andijan.
Salim Muhammad Galpuk came out with what men he had beyond the suburbs and gardens to the skirt of Aish-hill.
Salim Muhammad Galpuk may have had over 500 men.
Babur's men followed Galpuk's men almost to the Khakan Gate, unhorsing one after another.
The encounter with Galpuk's forces occurred at the Evening Prayer.
Nāṣir Beg and Qambar-ali Beg advised Babur not to go into the fort in the dark.
Babur yielded to the opinion of the experienced begs and withdrew to the outskirts of the suburbs.
If Babur had gone to the Gate, Andijan would have come into his hands.
Babur knew that Tambal had broken camp and was on his way to Andijan.
Babur dismounted near the Bed-time prayer at the side of the village of Rabat-i-zauraq.
Babur dismounted on level ground close to the village instead of where the defensive canal would have protected him.
Babur lay down carelessly without scouts or rear-ward.
It was Babur's habit to lie down in his tunic even in times of peace.
Qambar-ali Beg shouted to warn Babur that the enemy was near at the top of the morning.
Babur's standard-bearer had no time to adjust his standard and just mounted with it in his hand.
There were ten or fifteen men with Babur when he started toward the enemy.
Babur came up with Tambal's scouts, of which there may have been ten.
Babur overtook Tambal's scouts and poured in arrows on them.
Babur came up with Tambal's centre where Ahmad Tambal himself was with as many as 100 men.
Ahmad Tambal and another were standing in front of his array as if keeping a Gate.
Tambal's men were mostly sidling as if asking themselves whether to run away.
Three men were left with Babur: Nasir's Dost, Mirza Quli Kukulash, and Khudai-birdi Turkman's Karim-dad.
Babur shot an arrow aiming at Tambal's helm.
A new gosha-gir was given to Babur by his Younger Khan dada.
Babur and Tambal got upon the high-road and came face to face.
Tambal's right hand was towards Babur and Babur's right hand was towards Tambal.
Tambal was fully accoutred except for his horse's mail.
Babur was unprotected except for sword and quiver.
Babur shot an arrow adjusting for the attachment of Tambal's shield.
Babur's right leg was shot through during the encounter with Tambal.
Babur had on the cap of his helm.
Tambal chopped so violently at Babur's head that it lost all feeling under the blow.
A large wound was made on Babur's head though not a thread of the cap was cut.
Babur had not bared his sword during the encounter with Tambal.
Babur turned rein and retreated from Tambal.
Tambal used his sword on Nasir's Dost after using it on Babur.
The enemy followed Babur's party as far as an arrow flies to the butt.
The Khakan-canal is a great main-channel flowing in a deep cutting not everywhere to be crossed.
Babur's party came exactly opposite a low place where there was a passage over the Khakan-canal.
The horse Nasir's Dost was on fell down after crossing the canal.
Babur's party drew off for Aush over the rising-ground between Farghina and Khirabuk.
Mazid Taghai came up and joined Babur on the rise.
An arrow had pierced Mazid Taghai's right leg.
Nasir Beg, Muhammad Ali Mubashir, Khwaja Muhammad Ali, Khusrau Kukulash, and Na'man the page fell to Tambal's forces.
Many unmailed braves of Babur's fell to Tambal's forces.
The Khans dismounted near Andijan after closely following Tambal.
The Elder Khan dismounted at the side of the Reserve in the garden known as Birds'-mill belonging to Babur's grandmother Aisan-daulat Begim.
The Younger Khan dismounted near Baba Tawakkul's Alms-house.
Babur went from Aush two days later and saw the Elder Khan in Birds'-mill.
The Elder Khan gave over to the Younger Khan the places which had come in to Babur.
The Elder Khan said he brought the Younger Khan because Shaibaq Khan had taken Samarkand and was waxing greater.
The Elder Khan said the Younger Khan had no lands in Samarkand and his own were far away.
The Elder Khan promised Babur the country under Akhsi on the north of the Khujand-water.
The Elder Khan said after taking a firm grip of Farghana they would move and take Samarkand and give it to Babur.
The Elder Khan said the whole of the Farghana country was to be the Younger Khan's after taking Samarkand.
Babur believed the Elder Khan's words were meant to deceive him.
Babur agreed to the Elder Khan's terms willy-nilly.
Qambar-ali, known as the Skinner, joined Babur in a friendly way after he left the Elder Khan.
Qambar-ali told Babur that the Khans had taken the whole of the country just become his.
Qambar-ali said there was no opening for Babur through the Khans.
Babur had in his hands Aush, Marghinan, Auzikint and the cultivated land and the tribes and the hordes.
Shaibāq Khān had taken Samarkand and was growing more powerful.
The Younger Khān had no lands in Samarkand.
Babur was promised the country under Akhsī, on the north of the Khujand-water.
Babur believed the Khāns' words were meant to deceive him.
Babur agreed to the Khāns' proposal despite his doubts.
Babur had a wound in his leg.
Babur valued serving his blood relations the Khāns over ruling independently.
The Younger Khān had a reputation as a hero.
The Younger Khān's tent was disorganized and cluttered with various items.
Ātākā Bakhshī was a very skilled Mughūl surgeon.
Ātākā Bakhshī treated Babur's leg wound with a bandage and remedies.
Qaṃbar-`alī fled to Taṃbal in Andijān after fearing the consequences of his earlier words.
The Khāns sent Babur towards Akhsī with Ayūb Begchīk, Jān-ḥasan Bārīn, and Sārīgh-bāsh Mīrzā.
Shaikh Bāyazīd was Taṃbal's younger brother and was in Akhsī.
Shahbāz Qārlūq was in Kāsān.
Babur's forces crossed the Khujand-water opposite Bīkhrātā to attack Shahbāz.
Shahbāz Qārlūq fled into the fort when he learned of Babur's approach.
Babur believed that hesitation and caution can cause loss of opportunity for action.
Babur's army overran villages of both Akhsī and Kāsān.
Mīrīm, Long Ḥasan's adopted son, died in a fight with Babur's forces.
Pāp is a strong fort belonging to Akhsī.
Babur sent Sayyid Qāsim with braves to occupy Pāp.
Sayyid Qāsim slept carelessly without setting a watch at Pāp.
Shaikh Bāyazīd sent 200 serviceable braves to surprise Pāp at night.
Sayyid Qāsim drove off the attackers with only five or six men despite being outnumbered.
Babur considered Sayyid Qāsim's careless sleeping bad leadership but his defense very brave.
The Khāns were besieging Andijān but the garrison would not let them approach.
Shaikh Bāyazīd sent persons to invite Babur to Akhsī.
Shaikh Bāyazīd's objective was to separate Babur from The Khāns.
It was not Babur's habit to cheat and play false.
Babur hoped that by entering Akhsī he might detach Shaikh Bāyazīd from Taṃbal.
Shaikh Bāyazīd brought Babur's younger brother Nāṣir Mīrzā when meeting him.
Shaikh Bāyazīd gave Babur one of his father's houses in the outer fort of Akhsī.
Taṃbal sent Beg Tīlba to Shaibāq Khān with an invitation to enter Farghāna.
Shaibāq Khān agreed to come to Farghāna.
The Younger Khān had a reputation for justice and orthodoxy.
The Khāns' Mughūls behaved ill and oppressively in Aūsh, Marghīnān and other places.
The Aūshīs and Marghīnānīs rose in tumult and drove out the Mughūls.
The Khāns left Farghāna by way of Marghīnān and Kand-i-badām.
Taṃbal pursued The Khāns as far as Marghīnān.
Babur had much uncertainty about whether The Khāns would make a stand.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā came into Akhsī as a fugitive from Taṃbal.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā and Ibrāhīm Beg proposed making Shaikh Bāyazīd a prisoner.
Babur refused to break his promise to Shaikh Bāyazīd.
Babur's men failed to post guards on the bridge into the citadel.
Babur attributed these failures to inexperience.
Taṃbal arrived with 2 or 3000 men in mail and entered the citadel.
Babur had only about 100 men left in Akhsī.
Shaikh Bāyazīd and Qaṃbar-`alī came from Taṃbal to discuss peace.
Babur advised against making prisoners, believing negotiation was better.
Ibrāhīm Beg seized Shaikh Bāyazīd against Babur's wishes.
Babur posted men throughout Akhsī to defend against Taṃbal's forces.
Babur's horse was shot in the leg during fighting in Akhsī.
Babur was thrown from his horse among the enemy.
Babur's squire Kahil gave him a weakly pony.
Sl. Muḥ. Wais gave Babur his own horse.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā was attacked and driven off in panic.
Babur considered getting across the bridge and destroying it to make for Andijān.
Bābā Sher-zād suggested storming out at the gate.
Khwāja Mīr Mīrān spoke boldly during the crisis.
Babur shot an arrow at Shaikh Bāyazīd that grazed his neck.
An enemy shot Babur in the arm-pit, cutting two plates of his Qālmāq mail.
Babur shot at a man running along the ramparts, nailing his cap to the wall.
Babur pricked a fleeing man's head with his sword.
Babur and his men were outnumbered 10 to 1 or more in the outer fort.
Babur sent a message to Jahāngīr Mīrzā asking him to attack again.
Sulaimān, a servant of Muḥ. `Alī's Mubashir, gave his horse to Ibrāhīm Beg.
Kīchīk `Alī showed courage at the Gate and later in Aūsh.
Babur delayed too long in the Gate before escaping.
About twenty or thirty men were with Babur when he left Akhsī.
Banda-`alī called for Ibrāhīm Beg to make a stand with swords.
Babur considered such a stand ill-timed and inappropriate to the situation.
Babur was waiting in the Gate when he dismounted and gave his horse to Ibrahim Beg.
Kichik Ali was the Governor of Koel.
Kichik Ali was a retainer of Sl. Muh. Wais.
Twenty or thirty men were with Babur.
Armed men came down upon Babur's party as they exited the Gate.
Banda-ali was the maternal grandfather of Qasim Beg's son, Hamza.
Ibrahim Beg was beside Babur during the escape.
Babur considered the zeal displayed by Banda-ali and Ibrahim Beg to be ill-timed.
Babur and his party were pursued by the enemy after leaving Akhsi.
Many of Babur's men were unhorsed before reaching Sang.

Eight men were with Babur in his position of affairs: Nasir's Dost, Qasim Beg's Qambar-ali, Bayan-quli's Khan-quli, Mirza Quli Kukuldash, Nasir's Shaham, Sayyidi Qara's Abdu'l-qadus, Khwaja Husaini, and Babur himself.
Babur's party found a good little road in the broad valley far from the beaten track.
Babur made his party take cover when he saw blackness on the plain.
There were 20 or 25 pursuers while Babur's party numbered eight.
Babur believed that a fleeing foe, even if many, cannot face a few pursuers.
Khan-quli suggested that Babur take two good horses and go quickly on with Mirza Quli Kukuldash.
Babur considered it would not have looked well to leave any man alone without a horse amongst his foes.
Khan-quli dismounted and gave Babur his horse when Babur's horse was tired.
Sayyidi Qara's Abdu'l-qadus and Nasir's Shaham were unhorsed and fell behind.
Khan-quli was left behind during the escape.
Dost Beg's horse failed and stopped.
Qambar-ali dismounted and gave Babur his horse when Babur's horse began to tire.
Khwaja Husaini was a lame man who turned aside to higher ground.
Babur was left alone with Mirza Quli Kukuldash.
Babur said to Mirza Quli Kukuldash, 'What will become of me, if you fall behind? Come along! let's live or die together.'
Mirza Quli Kukuldash remained behind when his horse could not go on.
Two of the enemy were in sight: one Baba of Sairam and the other Banda-ali.
Babur had at least 20 arrows in his quiver.
Babur had confidence in his feet.
Babur spared his arrows for his own sake while the two men came within arrow's reach.
The two pursuers came no nearer out of caution.
By sunset Babur was near the hills.
The two pursuers called out that Jahangir Mirza had been brought in as a prisoner and Nasir Mirza was in their hands.
Babur made no reply to the pursuers' first call.
The two pursuers dismounted and spoke more respectfully to Babur.
The two pursuers told Babur that Sl. Ahmad Tambal would make him padshah.
Babur said his mind was not easy about going to Tambal.
Babur asked the two pursuers to guide him to a road to reach The Khan's presence.
Babur promised the two pursuers favour and kindness greater than their heart's desire if they would guide him.
Babur told the two pursuers to go back the way they came if they would not help him.
The two pursuers swore solemn oath upon the Holy Book.
Babur confided in the two pursuers after they swore their oath.
Babur's trust in the two pursuers was not completely secure despite their oath.
Babur gave the two pursuers the lead and followed them.
After 2 to 4 miles, Babur and the pursuers came to the bed of a torrent.
The two pursuers said the road through the broad valley was a long way ahead.
About half through the night, Babur and the pursuers reached another stream.
The two pursuers said the road through the broad valley was behind them.
The two pursuers guided Babur toward the Ghawa road.
In the third watch of the night, Babur reached the Karnan gully which comes down from Ghawa.
Baba Sairami said he would look along the Ghawa road.
Baba Sairami reported that some men had gone along the Ghawa road led by one wearing a Mughul cap.
Babur took alarm at Baba Sairami's report.
At dawn, Babur was in the middle of cultivated land far from the road he wanted to take.
Babur asked to be guided to a hiding place for the day.
Babur asked to be led to cross the Khujand-water and along its further bank at night.
Banda-ali was Commandant in Karnan.
Banda-ali said there was no doing without food for themselves or their horses.
Banda-ali went into Karnan to bring what he could find.
Babur's party stopped 2 miles out of Karnan.
Banda-ali was a long time away and near dawn there was no sign of him.
Banda-ali hurried up bringing three loaves of bread but no corn for the horses.
Each of Babur's men put a loaf into the breast of his tunic.
Babur's party tethered their horses in the open valley and went to higher ground to keep watch.
Ahmad the Falconer went along the Ghawa road for Akhsi near mid-day.
Babur thought of calling to Ahmad the Falconer and offering him the horses.
Babur's horses had had a day and a night's strain and struggle without corn and were utterly done.
Babur and his men decided to fetch one horse for each of them from the men Baba Sairami had seen on the road.
At mid-day, something glittering was seen on a horse as far away as eye could reach.
Babur believed it must have been Muh. Baqir Beg himself.
Muh. Baqir Beg had been with Babur in Akhsi.
Banda-ali and Baba Sairami said the horses had had no corn for two days and two nights.
Banda-ali and Baba Sairami suggested putting the horses to graze in the dale.
At the Afternoon Prayer, a horseman passed along the rising-ground where Babur's party had been.
Babur recognized the horseman as Qadir-birdi, the head-man of Ghawa.
Babur sent Qadir-birdi to bring rope, a grass-hook, an axe, material for crossing water, corn for the horses, food, and if possible, other horses.
Babur made a tryst with Qadir-birdi for that same spot at the Bed-time Prayer.
Near the Evening Prayer, a horseman passed from the direction of Karnan for Ghawa.
The horseman must have been Muh. Baqir Beg himself.
Muh. Baqir Beg had changed his voice so much that Babur did not recognize him despite years of service.
Babur said it would have been well if he had recognized Muh. Baqir Beg and he had joined him.
The passing of the horseman caused much anxiety and alarm.
The tryst with Qadir-birdi of Ghawa could not be kept.
Banda-ali suggested going to retired gardens in the suburbs of Karnan.
It was winter and very cold.
Someone found a worn, coarse sheepskin coat and brought it to Babur.
Babur put on the sheepskin coat.
Someone brought Babur a bowl of millet-porridge.
Babur ate the millet-porridge and was wonderfully refreshed.
Babur asked Banda-ali if he had sent off the man to Qadir-birdi.
Banda-ali said he had sent the man.
The men sent the messenger to Tambal in Akhsi instead of to Qadir-birdi.
Babur's eyes closed in sleep in a house.
The men artfully told Babur not to bestir himself to leave Karnan till there was news of Qadir-birdi.
Babur and his party mounted at mid-night and went to a distant orchard.
Baba Sairami kept watch from the roof of a house.
Near mid-day, Baba Sairami came down and said Commandant Yusuf was coming.
Great fear fell upon Babur.
Babur asked Baba Sairami to find out whether Yusuf came because he knew about Babur.
Yusuf said he met a foot-soldier in the Gate of Akhsi who said the padshah was in such a place.
Yusuf told no-one and put the foot-soldier with Wali the Treasurer whom he had made prisoner in the fight.
Yusuf gallopped off to find Babur.
Baba Sairami said they were all Babur's servants and Babur must go.
Babur said after such rebellion and fighting, he could not go with confidence.
Yusuf knelt before Babur.
Yusuf said Sl. Ahmad Tambal had no news of Babur, but Shaikh Bayazid had and sent Yusuf.
On hearing this, Babur's state of mind was miserable.
Babur said nothing in the world is worse than fear for one's life.
Babur told Yusuf to tell the truth.
Babur said if the affair was likely to go on to worse, he would make ablution.
Yusuf swore oaths but Babur did not trust them.
Babur knew the helplessness of his position.
Babur rose and went to a corner of the garden.
Babur was not yet 22 years old when he experienced failure, privation, and dangers.
Babur had been tempered by failure, privation, and dangers.
Babur possessed quenchless courage and abounding vitality.
Babur had a resolve to win upward.
Babur initially intended to go to Khurasan but changed his plan to go to Kabul.
Babur claimed a right to Kabul as a Timurid to dispossess the Arghuns.
Babur's uncle Aulugh Beg Mirza Kabuli died in 907 AH.
Babur left Farghana in mid-June 1504.
Babur entered his 23rd year at Ailak-yilaq and applied the razor to his face.
Between 2 and 300 people, mostly on foot, went with Babur into exile.
Babur's company had only two tents among them.
Babur's own tent was pitched for his mother.
Babur's mother was with him during his time in the hill-country of Sukh and Hushar.
Babur spent about a year in great misery in the hill-country of Sukh and Hushar.
Babur was homeless and poor and shut in by enemies.
The loyalty and kindness of hill-tribes saved Babur and his followers.
Babur's families and the families of his men were with him.
Qutluq-nigar joined Babur from Tashkent.
Qutluq-nigar's daughter Khanzada was Shaibaq's wife.
Qutluq-nigar spent hard months with Babur when his fortunes were at their lowest ebb.
Babur's escape led to the establishment of a Timurid dynasty in Hindustan.
Babur was the sport of older men's intrigues.
Babur had been deprived of rule and retainers with Jahangir Mirza.
Babur's nature was against doing injury to his brethren or kinsmen.
Babur kept letters from Sulaiman Husain Mirza in his possession.
Babur deposited households and families in Fort Ajar of the Kahmard-valley.
Babur moved out with his men after depositing families in Ajar.
Three or four thousand heads-of-houses from the Mughul horde joined Babur.
Babur accepted Khusrau Shah's offer of submission and service.
Babur made a compact with Khusrau Shah that his life would be safe.
Babur agreed that Khusrau Shah could select whatever amount of his goods he wished.
Babur sat under a large plane-tree near Dushi to meet Khusrau Shah.
Khusrau Shah knelt 25 or 26 times in succession before Babur.
Khusrau Shah's conversation was vapid and empty.
Khusrau Shah was a coward and false to his salt.
Khusrau Shah had once been master of 20 or 30,000 retainers.
Khusrau Shah once owned Sulaiman Mahmud's dominions from Qahlugha to the Hindukush range.
Babur's company consisted of defeated and destitute men numbering 200 or 300.
Sherím Taghái was the greatest man of Babur's followers.
Sherím Taghái thought of leaving Babur because he was not keen to go into Khurasan.
Sherím Taghái was a bit of a coward.
Baqi Chaghaniáni was a younger brother of Khusrau Shah.
Baqi Chaghaniáni's holdings were Chaghaniáni, Shahr-i-safa, and Tirmiz.
Baqi Chaghaniáni urged Babur to dismiss Jahangir Mirza.
Baqi Chaghaniáni believed two rulers in one country cause faction and disorder.
Baqi Chaghaniáni was a man of weight.
Yar-ali Balal joined Babur at Aibak with several braves.
Yar-ali Balal had been with Babur before and made good use of his sword.
Yar-ali Balal had gone to Khusrau Shah during Babur's throneless times.
Qambar-ali Beg, also known as Qambar-ali Silakh, fled to Babur in the Zindan-valley.
Jahangir Mirza married the daughter of Sulaiman Mahmud Mirza and Khanzada Begim.
Jahanzada Begim had been set aside for Jahangir Mirza during the lifetime of the Mizras.
Ayub's Yusuf and Ayub's Bihlul left Babur for Jahangir Mirza.
Ayub's Yusuf and Ayub's Bihlul were tempters to disloyalty.
Ayub's Yusuf and Ayub's Bihlul conveyed Jahangir Mirza into Khurasan.
Mulla Baba of Pashaghar was Babur's envoy to Khusrau Shah.
Muhib-ali, the Armourer, came to Babur from Khusrau Shah.
Khusrau Shah was renowned for kindness and liberality.
Khusrau Shah did not show Babur the humanity he showed to the meanest of men.
Mulla Muhammad Turkistani was Khusrau Shah's old servant and able and trusted.
Mulla Muhammad Turkistani made Qunduz fast for Shaibaq Khan.
Qambar-ali Beg's talk was foolish.
Qambar-ali Beg's manners were displeasing to Baqi Beg.
Qambar-ali Beg was dismissed to gratify Baqi Beg.
Qambar-ali Beg's son Abdu'l-shukur was in Jahangir Mirza's service.
Khusrau Shah's son-in-law was Ayub's Yaqub.
Sulaiman Husain Mirza was the greatest ruler in Timur Beg's territory by years, armed strength, and dominions.
Sulaiman Husain Mirza defended the bank of the Murghab against three brothers.
Sulaiman Husain Mirza spoke only of defence against the Auzbegs, not of marching forth to meet them.
Hasan Barlas was Khusrau Shah's old tax-gatherer.
Hasan Barlas made Babur march and halt with roughness from Ailak to Aubaj.
Mirza Khan demanded vengeance on Khusrau Shah for the blood of his brothers.
Many of Babur's followers agreed that Khusrau Shah should get his desserts.
Babur was once master of 20 or 30,000 retainers.
Babur owned Mahmud's dominions from Qahlugha to the range of Hindu-kush.
Babur was made to march and halt by a tax-gatherer named Hasan Barlas.
Babur allowed Khusrau Shah to take his goods and leave despite calls for his execution.
Hamza Bi Mangfit led Auzberg raiders near Dushi.
Babur's forces defeated Hamza Bi Mangfit's Auzbegs and brought back several heads.
Khusrau Shah's armoury contained approximately 7 or 800 coats-of-mail and horse accoutrements.
Babur had never seen the star Suhail before crossing the Hupian-pass.
Babur's forces defeated Sherak Arghun and took 70-80 serviceable braves.
Sherak Arghun accepted service with Babur after his defeat.
Walii, Khusrau Shah's younger brother, was defeated at Sar-i-ab and fled to the Auzbegs.
Shaibaq Khan had Walii's head struck off in the Square of Samarkand.
Babur had one of his soldiers beaten to death for forcibly taking a jar of oil.
Babur's mother and belongings rejoined him at Aba-quruq.
Babur decided to lay siege to Kabul after taking counsel with his advisors.
Babur dismounted between Haidar Taqi's garden and the tomb of Qul-i-bayazid during the siege of Kabul.
Jahangir Mirza dismounted in Babur's great Four-gardens during the siege.
Nasir Mirza dismounted in the meadow of Qutluq-qadam's tomb during the siege.
Babur obtained Kabul without a fight in the last ten days of the Second Rabi (October 1504 AD).
Babur obtained Ghaznī and their dependent districts at the same time as Kabul.
Kabul is situated in the Fourth climate.
Kabul has the Lamghanat, Parashawar, and Hash-nagar to its east.
Kabul has mountain regions with Karnud and Ghur to its west.
Kabul has the Qunduz and Andar-ab countries to its north, separated by the Hindu-kush range.
Kabul has Farmul, Naghr, Bannu and Afghanistan to its south.
The Kabul walled-town connects with a mountain known as Shah-of-Kabul.
A canal was brought to Shah-of-Kabul during the time of Babur's paternal uncle, Aulugh Beg Mirza.
Wais Ataka was the guardian of Aulugh Beg Mirza.
A large pool lies east of Shah-of-Kabul and south of the walled-town.
The citadel of Kabul stands on a detached rock known as Uqabain.
The citadel of Kabul overlooks three meadows: Siyah-sang, Sung-qurghan, and Chalak.
The Parwan-wind blows through Kabul in the heats.
Caravans come to Kabul from Kashghar, Farghana, Turkistan, Samarkand, Bukhara, Balkh, Hisar and Badakhshan.
Caravans come to Qandahar from Khurasan.
7, 8, or 10,000 horses come to Kabul every year.
10, 15 or 20,000 heads-of-houses come to Kabul from Hindistan every year.
Merchants bring slaves, white cloth, sugar-candy, refined and common sugars, and aromatic roots to Kabul.
Many traders in Kabul are not content with a profit of 30 or 40 on 10.
Products of Khurasan, Rum, Iraq and China can be had in Kabul.
Kabul has hot and cold districts close to one another.
A man may go from Kabul town to where snow never falls in one day.
A man may go to where snow never thaws in two sidereal hours from Kabul.
Grapes, pomegranates, apricots, apples, quinces, pears, peaches, plums, sinjid, almonds and walnuts grow in Kabul.
Babur had cuttings of the alu-balu brought to Kabul and planted.
Babur had sugar-cane brought to Kabul and planted.
Oranges, citrons, amluk and sugar-cane are brought to Kabul from the Lamghanat.
Honey is brought to Kabul from the hill-tracts.
Bee-hives are in use in Kabul.
No honey comes from towards Ghaznī.
Kabul rhubarb is good.
Kabul quinces and plums are very good.
Kabul badrang is very good.
Kabul grows an excellent grape known as the water-grape.
Kabul wines are heady.
Wines from the Khwaja Khawand Said hill-skirt are famous for their strength.
Kabul is not fertile in grain, with a four or five-fold return reckoned as good.
Kabul melons are not first-rate but not altogether bad when grown from Khurasan seed.
Kabul has a very pleasant climate.
Even in the heats of Kabul, one cannot sleep at night without a fur-coat.
Snow lies deep in most places in Kabul in winter, but the cold is not excessive.
The cold in Samarkand and Tabriz is more extreme than in Kabul.
Sung-qurghan meadow is some 4 miles to the north-east of Kabul.
Sung-qurghan meadow has grass fit for horses and few mosquitos.
Chalak meadow is some 2 miles to the north-west of Kabul.
Chalak meadow is large but mosquitos greatly trouble the horses there.
Durrin meadows (Tipa and Qush-nadir) are on the west of Kabul.
Durrin meadows are each about 2 miles from Kabul town.
Durrin meadows are small, have grass good for horses, and no mosquitos.
Siyah-sang meadow is on the east of Kabul.
Siyah-sang meadow swarms with mosquitos in the heats.
Qutluq-qadam's tomb lies between Siyah-sang meadow and the Currier's-gate.
The Hindu-kush mountains separate Kabul from Balkh, Qunduz and Badakhshan.
Seven roads cross the Hindu-kush mountains into Kabul.
Three passes lead out of Panjhir: Khawak, Tul, and Bazarak.
The pass on the Tul road is the best of the Panjhir passes.
Babur crossed the Sind river by fords in most of his expeditions into Hindustan.
In 932 AH (1525 AD), Babur defeated Sultan Ibrahim and conquered Hindustan, crossing by boat at Nil-ab.
Eleven or twelve tongues are spoken in Kabul.
The Kabul country has fourteen tumans.
In 914 AH (1508-9 AD), Babur laid out the Four-gardens known as the Bagh-i-wafa in Ningnahar.
Babur had plantains brought and planted in the Bagh-i-wafa garden in the year he defeated Pahar Khan and took Lahor and Dipalpur.
Babur had sugar-cane planted in the Bagh-i-wafa garden the year before planting plantains.
The Bagh-i-wafa garden has a one-mill stream flowing constantly through its middle.
Babur considers the Bagh-i-wafa garden admirably situated.
Babur made the circuit of Mir Sayyid Ali Hamadani's shrine when he took Chaghan-sarai in 920 AH.
Babur found flying-squirrels in the Nijr-au mountains.
Babur has never seen a musk-rat despite hearing about them in Nijr-au.
Babur once counted tulips on the foot-hills and found 32 or 33 different sorts.
Babur named one tulip the Rose-scented because its perfume resembled that of the red rose.
Babur observed that the Hundred-leaved tulip grows at the outlet of the Ghur-bund narrows.
Babur considers the snow mountains south-west of Kabul to be fastnesses.
Babur values the Bagh-i-wafa garden highly for its beauty and situation.
Babur had the road from Kabul to Ningnahar made safe by having it peopled at Qara-tu.
Babur believes that if any country has so many differing tribes and diversity of tongues as Kabul, it is not known.
Babur valued the aesthetic qualities of gardens and natural landscapes.
Babur took possession of the Great garden of Istālīf after paying its owners.
Babur had the stream in the Istālīf garden made straight and orderly.
Babur ordered the spring at Khwāja Sih-yārān to be enclosed in mortared stone-work.
Babur built a large round seat on the hillside and planted it with willows.
Babur had a channel dug across the slope to bring water to the round seat.
Babur discovered that a supposedly miraculous moving tomb in Ghaznī was a trick.
Babur ordered the scaffold at the tomb destroyed and a dome built over it.
Babur forbade the tomb servants from creating the movement again.
Babur conquered Kābul and Ghaznī.
Babur over-ran Kohāt, the plain of Bannū, and lands of the Afghāns.
Babur sent money through Khwāja Kalān to repair the Sultan's dam in Ghaznī.
Babur conquered Hindūstān.
Babur conquered Qandahār, Balkh, and Badakhshān.
Babur intended to bring the Bangash people to obedience.
Babur valued detailed geographical and agricultural knowledge.
Babur appreciated fine fruits, grapes, and wines.
Babur was interested in the flora and fauna of his territories.
Babur was knowledgeable about irrigation and water management.
Babur was skeptical of miraculous claims and investigated them.
Babur shot a cord at birds on the Bārān river at night.
The cord broke while Babur was drawing in a bird.
Babur devised the Bāburī script during the time of levying grain on Kābul.
Babur rode out of Kābul for Hindūstān in the month of Sha`bān (910 AH., January 1505 AD.).
Babur had never seen a hot country or the Hindūstān border-land before this journey.
Babur was amazed by the different grasses, trees, animals, birds, and customs in Nīngnahār.
Babur rode out from Jām to visit Bīgrām.
Babur took the advice of Bāqī Chaghānīānī to go to Kohāt instead of crossing the Sind river.
Babur captured many Afghāns at Kohāt but set them all free.
Babur ordered prisoners to be beheaded and had pillars of heads set up in his camp.
Babur pardoned all the prisoners after the Kīwī sangur was taken.
Babur made the rounds of the camp once each night.
Babur kept his right, left, centre and van positioned as in battle formation each night.
Babur had men on foot stationed at an arrow's distance from the tents each night.
Babur favored guest-begs and stranger-begs over old servants and Andijānīs.
Babur had been blamed for favoring only old servants and Andijānīs despite his actual preferences.
Babur levied 30,000 ass-loads of grain on Kābul, Ghaznī and their dependencies.
The grain levy was excessive and caused the country to suffer greatly.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā was given Ghaznī with its dependencies and appurtenances.
Nāṣir Mīrzā was given the Nīngnahār tūmān, Mandrāwar, Nūr-valley, Kūnār, Nūr-gal and Chīghān-sarāī.
Babur decided to overrun the Plain and return to Kābul afterwards by way of Farmūl.
Babur's army maintained strict nighttime security with soldiers positioned as they would be in battle formation.
Babur personally made the rounds of the camp once each night.
Soldiers who were not at their posts had their noses slit and were led through the army.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā commanded the right wing of Babur's army.
Mīrzā Khān commanded the left wing of Babur's army.
Sayyid Qāsim Lord-of-the-gate commanded the van of Babur's army.
Babur's army was organized in six divisions, each with its day and night on guard.
Babur observed that water could be found in dry riverbeds of Hindūstān by digging down one to one-and-a-half yards.
Hindī Mughūl killed the Afghan merchant Khwāja Khiẓr Lūhānī.
Sherīm T̤aghāī had his index-finger cut off by an Afghan.
Babur expected the choice of roads to be settled the next day after departure.
It was the `Īd-i-fitr (March 7th 1505 AD) when Babur's army chose to take the road turning the bill of the Mehtar Sulaimān range.
Babur's army took a road that no one in the army had ever seen before.
Babur composed a Turkī ode when New-year's Day fell close to the `Id-i-fitr.
Sl. Qulī Chūnāq killed an Afghan on a single rock, which led to his favor and promotion.
Qūtlūq-qadam killed an Afghan after grappling with him and falling 10 to 12 yards.
Kūpūk Beg killed an Afghan after rolling down a hill with him.
All Afghāns taken prisoner were set free.
Babur's army crossed the Sind-water at Bīlah, with some soldiers being carried down by the current.
Qul-i-bāyazīd swam the Sind river alone on a bare-backed horse and chased off enemies.
Babur raised Qul-i-bāyazīd from the cook's office to the royal taster's position.
Babur had soldiers who injured attendants at Pīr Kānū's tomb cut to pieces.
Babur's felt-tent had to be left behind due to lack of baggage-beasts.
Water stood knee-deep in Babur's tent during heavy rain one night.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā informed Babur of Bāqī Chaghānīānī's treacherous plot to make him Pādshāh.
Babur suspected Sayyid Ḥusain Akbar, Sl. `Alī the page, and Khusrau Shāh's begs were involved in the treachery.
Babur considered Jahāngīr Mīrzā's behavior in revealing the plot to be like that of a blood-relation.
Babur sent Jahāngīr Mīrzā to raid the Afghāns of the neighborhood after learning of the treachery.
As many as 200 to 300 horses were left behind in each camping-ground due to lack of provisions.
Sayyid Maḥmūd Aūghlāqchī left his horses behind and walked.
The Āb-i-istāda was a wonderfully large sheet of water that seemed to join the sky.
Flocks of geese at the Āb-i-istāda were innumerable, sometimes showing red feathers when they flew.
Eggs lay in masses on the shore of the Āb-i-istāda.
The torrent coming down to the Āb-i-istāda from the plain of Kattawāz had water deep enough to require horses and camels to swim it.
Babur's army reached Ghaznī after passing through Old Nānī and Sar-i-dih.
Jahāngīr Mīrzā was Babur's host at Ghaznī, setting food before him and offering tribute.
That year most waters came down in flood.
Babur had a boat fashioned in a pool and set on the Dih-i-yaq`ūb-water to transport his people.
Babur reached Kābul in the month of Ẕū'l-ḥijja (May 1505 AD).
Sayyid Yūsuf Aūghlāqchī died from colic before Babur reached Kābul.
Nāṣir Mīrzā asked leave to stay behind at Qūsh-guṃbaz to take something from his district.
Nāṣir Mīrzā sent a force against the people of Nūr-valley.
Faẓlī, Nāṣir Mīrzā's commander, scattered his men to forage in impracticable ground.
The valesmen of Nūr-valley drove off Faẓlī's foragers and captured many soldiers and horses.
Nāṣir Mīrzā did not follow Babur and stayed behind.
Yūsuf and Bahlūl, sons of Ayūb, were seditious, silly and arrogant persons.
Yūsuf and Bahlūl did not come with Babur because Nāṣir Mīrzā did not come.
Yūsuf and Bahlūl spent the winter as companions of Nāṣir Mīrzā's cups and social pleasures.
Nāṣir Mīrzā and his companions over-ran the Tarkalānī Afghāns.
Nāṣir Mīrzā drove the families of clans, outside-tribes and hordes from Nīngnahār and the Lamghānāt to the Bārān-water.
The Badakhshīs were united against the Aūzbegs and killed some of them.
Shaibāq Khān gave Qūndūz to Qaṃbar Bī and went himself to Khwārizm.
Qaṃbar Bī sent a son of Muḥammad-i-makhdūmī named Maḥmūd to the Badakhshīs to conciliate them.
Mubārak Shāh cut off Maḥmūd's head and those of some Aūzbegs.
Mubārak Shāh made himself fast in a fort he renamed Qila`-i-ẕafar.
Babur's mother had fever in the month of Muharram.
Babur's mother died after six days of illness.
Babur's mother was buried in the New-year's Garden on the mountain-skirt.
Babur learned of the deaths of his younger Khan dada Alacha Khan and his grandmother Aisan-daulat Begim while mourning his mother.
Shah Begim, the mother of the Khans, arrived from Khurasan after Babur's mother's Fortieth.
Babur got fever at Qush-nadir when starting for Qandahar.
A great earthquake occurred that leveled houses and killed many people.
The earthquake caused the Earth to quake 33 times on the first day.
The earthquake caused the Earth to quake two or three times daily for a month afterward.
Babur ordered begs and soldiers to repair breaches in the fort of Kabul.
The fort repairs were completed in 20 days or a month.
Babur decided to move on Qalat instead of going to Qandahar.
Jahangir Mirza and Baqi Chaghaniāni insisted strongly on the move to Qalat.
Sher-i-`ali the page showed signs of disloyalty and was put to death.
Kichik Khwaja was a daring brave who had used his sword in Babur's presence.
Kichik Khwaja died from a spear wound to the eye while attacking Qalat.
Babur forgave the offences of Muqim's retainers when they surrendered Qalat.
Babur did not wish to reduce the high family of Muqim to great straits.
Babur returned to Kabul after overrunning the Afghans of Sawa-sang and Ala-tagh.
A Khirilchi thief stole a bay horse and khachar from Babur's tent in the Char-bagh.
Baqi Chaghaniāni joined Babur on the Amu-bank and had more trust and authority than any other man of Babur's.
Baqi Chaghaniāni was mean, miserly, malicious, ill-tongued, envious and cross-natured.
Baqi Chaghaniāni owned 30 to 40,000 sheep when he left Tirmiz.
Baqi Chaghaniāni gave only 50 sheep to Babur's hungry braves despite his wealth.
Baqi Chaghaniāni had nagarets beaten at his own Gate despite acknowledging Babur as his chief.
Baqi Chaghaniāni controlled the revenue from Kabul town, the darogha-ship in Kabul and Panjhir, the Gadai Hazara, kushluk, and the Gate.
Babur gave Baqi Chaghaniāni leave to go towards Hindustan.
Yar-i-husain, the son of Darya Khan, was in Kacha-kot and blocked roads taking toll.
Yar-i-husain killed Baqi Chaghaniāni and took his wife.
The Turman Hazaras had robbed on the roads since Babur came into Kabul.
Babur raided Hazaras at Janlik in the month of Shaban.
Shaikh Darwish Kukulddash was shot in the nipple by a Hazara and died.
Shaikh Darwish Kukulddash had been with Babur in the guerilla-times and was Master-armourer.
Babur and his men found a fat camel belonging to the Hazaras and cooked its flesh.
Babur composed good musical pieces including excellent airs and preludes.
Babur was an exceptional patron and protector of men of talent and accomplishments.
Babur's instruction and support enabled Master Qul-i-muhammad the lutanist, Shaikhi the flautist, and Husain the lutanist to rise to eminence.
Babur's effort and supervision helped Master Bih-zad and Shah Muzaffar become distinguished in painting.
Ali-sher Beg had no son, daughter, wife, or family.
Ali-sher Beg let the world pass by alone and unencumbered.
Ali-sher Beg was first Keeper of the Seal, then became a beg, and served as Commandant in Astarabadi before forsaking soldiering.
Ali-sher Beg took nothing from the Mirza but instead offered considerable gifts each year.
Ali-sher Beg collapsed and died the day after meeting Babur returning from the Astarabadi campaign.
Wali Beg was orthodox and made the prayers.
Wali Beg was rough and sincere.
Wali Beg died shortly after the Mirza took the throne in 973 AH.
Nuyan Beg was a Sayyid of Tirmiz on his father's side.
Nuyan Beg was related to Sultan Abu-said Mirza and Sultan Husain Mirza on his mother's side.
Nuyan Beg was a bragging, easy-going, wine-bibbing, jolly person.
Jahangir Barlas shared the Kabul command with Muhammad Baranduq Barlas.
Jahangir Barlas had graceful and charming movements and poses.
Jahangir Barlas was a man of pleasant temper.
Jahangir Barlas knew the rules of hunting and hawking.
Jahangir Barlas was a favorite of Badiu'z-zaman Mirza.
Mirza Ahmad of Ali Farsi Barlas did not write verse but knew what was poetry.
Mirza Ahmad of Ali Farsi Barlas was a gay-hearted, elegant person.
Abdulkhaliq Beg's grandfather was Firuz Shah, a greatly favored beg of Shahrokh Mirza.
Abdulkhaliq Beg held Khwarizm for a time.
Ibrahim Duldai had good knowledge of revenue matters and the conduct of public business.
Zun-nun Arghun was a brave man who used his sword well.
Zun-nun Arghun was somewhat of a fool despite his courage.
Zun-nun Arghun did excellent work in Ghur and the Nikdiris with only 70 to 80 men against masses of Hazaras and Nikdiris.
Zun-nun Arghun had no match for keeping the tribes in order.
Zun-nun Arghun was given Zamin-dawar after his success in Ghur and the Nikdiris.
Zun-nun Arghun's son Shah-i-shuja Arghun used to move about with him and chopped away with his sword even in childhood.
Zun-nun Arghun was made Lord of Badiu'z-zaman Mirza's Gate.
Zun-nun Arghun was orthodox and did not neglect the prayers, even making the extra ones.
Zun-nun Arghun was mad for chess and played it with both hands.
Avarice and stinginess ruled in Zun-nun Arghun's character.
Zun-nun Arghun was killed by Shaibaq Khan near Qara-rabat while standing against him with only 100 to 150 men.
Darwish-i-ali Beg was the younger full-brother of Ali-sher Beg.
Darwish-i-ali Beg had the Balkh Command for a time.
Darwish-i-ali Beg was a muddle-head and somewhat wanting in merit.
Darwish-i-ali Beg was dismissed from the Balkh Command because his muddle-headedness hampered the Mirza in his first campaign against Qunduz and Hisar.
Mughul Beg was Governor of Heri for a time.
Mughul Beg was later given Astarabadi and fled from there to Yaqub Beg in Iraq.
Mughul Beg was of amorous disposition and an incessant dicer.
Sayyid Badr was a very strong man.
Sayyid Badr was graceful in his movements and singularly well-mannered.
Sayyid Badr danced wonderfully well with one unique dance of his own invention.
Sayyid Badr was the Mirza's comrade in wine and social pleasure.
Islam Barlas was a plain person who understood hawking well.
Islam Barlas could draw a bow of 30 to 40 batmans strength and make his shaft pass through the target.
Islam Barlas could loosen his bow while galloping, string it again, and hit the gourd.
Islam Barlas could shoot through a revolving string-grip.
Islam Barlas served the Mirza continuously and was at every social gathering.

Shaikh Abu-said Khan Dar-miyan's name origin is uncertain, possibly from taking a horse to the Mirza in the middle of a fight or from putting himself between the Mirza and someone designing on his life.
Bih-bud Beg served in the pages' circle during guerilla times.
Bih-bud Beg gave such satisfaction by his service that the Mirza put his name on the stamp and the coin.
Shaikhim Beg was called Shaikhim Suhail because Suhail was his pen-name.
Shaikhim Beg wrote all sorts of verse with terrifying words and mental images.
Shaikhim Beg recited verse in Maulana Abdur-rahman Jami's presence.
Shaikhim Beg put together a divan and wrote masnavis.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg was the son of Wali Beg.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg became one of the Mirza's great begs.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg never neglected his service and used to recline day and night in the Gate.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg's free meals and open table were always set just outside the Gate.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg kept his servants neat and well-dressed.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg gave ample portion to the poor and destitute with his own hands.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg was foul-mouthed and evil-spoken.
Muhammad-i-wali Beg was palsied when Babur took Samarkand in 917 AH.
Baba Ali the Lord of the Gate was first shown favor by Ali-sher Beg.
Baba Ali was taken into service by the Mirza because of his courage.
Baba Ali was made Lord of the Gate and promoted to be a beg.
Baba Ali's son Yunas of Ali is serving Babur as a beg, confidant, and member of his household.
Badru'd-din had been in the service of Sultan Abu-said Mirza's Chief Justice Mirak Abdur-rahim.
Badru'd-din was very nimble and sure-footed and could leap over seven horses at once.
Badru'd-din and Baba Ali were close companions.
Hasan of Ali Jalai's original name was Husain Jalai.
Hasan of Ali Jalai's father Ali Jalai was favored and made a beg by Babur Mirza.
Hasan of Ali Jalai was Sultan Husain Mirza's Qush-begi.
Hasan of Ali Jalai made Tufaili his pen-name.
Hasan of Ali Jalai wrote good odes and was the Master of this art in his day.
Hasan of Ali Jalai wrote odes on Babur's name when he came to his presence at the time Babur took Samarkand in 917 AH.
Hasan of Ali Jalai was impudent and prodigal.
Hasan of Ali Jalai was a keeper of catamites and a constant dicer and draught-player.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid was at first Chief Justice.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid later became one of the Mirza's favorite household-begs.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid was full of accomplishments.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid had no equal on the dulcimer and invented the shake on the dulcimer.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid wrote in several scripts, most beautifully in the taalik.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid composed admirable letters and wrote good verse with Bayani as his pen-name.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid was a pleasant companion.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid's verse ranks low compared with his other accomplishments.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid knew what was poetry.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid was vicious and shameless.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid became the captive of a sinful disease through his vicious excesses.
Khwaja Abdulah Marwarid outlived his hands and feet and tasted agonies of varied torture for several years.
Sayyid Muhammad-i-aorus was the son of Aorus Arghun.
Aorus Arghun was the beg in chief authority when Sultan Abu-said Mirza took the throne.
Sayyid Muhammad-i-aorus was one of the most distinguished archer-braves of his time.
Sayyid Muhammad-i-aorus had a strong bow and long shaft.
Sayyid Muhammad-i-aorus was a bold and good shot.
Sayyid Muhammad-i-aorus was Commandant in Andikhud for some time.
Mir Ali the Master of the Horse brought Sultan Husain Mirza down on the defenceless Yadgar-i-muhammad Mirza by sending a man to him.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi was the son of Sayyid Aughlaqchi.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi was the younger brother of Sayyid Yusuf Beg.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi was the father of Mirza Farrukh, a capable and accomplished son.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi came to Babur's presence before Babur took Samarkand in 917 AH.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi wrote fairly though he had written little verse.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi understood the astrolabe and astronomy well.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi was excellent company with good talk.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi was rather a bad drinker.
Sayyid Hasan Aughlaqchi died in the fight at Ghaj-dawan.
Tingri-birdi the storekeeper was a plain, bold, sword-slashing brave.
Tingri-birdi charged out of the Gate of Balkh on Khusrau Shah's great retainer Nazar Bahadur and overcame him in 903 AH.
Ali Khan Bayandar was one of the first Turkman braves to come to the Mirza's presence.
Asad Beg and Taham-tan Beg were an elder and younger brother.
Badiu'z-zaman Mirza took Taham-tan Beg's daughter and by her had Muhammad-i-zaman Mirza.
Mir Umar Beg was a brave, plain, excellent person.
Mir Umar Beg was later in Badiu'z-zaman Mirza's service.
Mir Umar Beg's son Abu'l-fath came from Iraq to Babur's presence.
Abu'l-fath was a very soft, unsteady and feeble person.
Abdulbaqi Mirza of Timur Beg's line came into Khurasan after Shah Ismail took Iraq and Azarbaijan around 906 AH.
Abdulbaqi Mirza was a Miran-shahi.
Timur Usman was the great, trusted beg of Yaqub Beg and was Abdulbaqi Mirza's paternal-uncle.
Sultan Husain Mirza took Abdulbaqi Mirza into favor and made him a son-in-law by giving him Sultanim Begim.
Mir Sar-i-barahna was from a village in Andijan.
Mir Sar-i-barahna appears to have made claim to be a sayyid.
Mir Sar-i-barahna was a very agreeable companion with pleasant temper and speech.
Mir Sar-i-barahna's judgments and rulings carried weight amongst men of letters and poets of Khurasan.
Mir Sar-i-barahna wasted his time composing a work in imitation of the story of Amir Hamza.
Mir Sar-i-barahna's composition is one long, far-fetched lie opposed to sense and nature.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi was not a Sufi but was mystical.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi's promotion was due to his good birth rather than merit.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi wrote a production called Majalis-ul-ushshaq.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi ascribes the authorship of Majalis-ul-ushshaq to Sultan Husain Mirza in its preface.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi's Majalis-ul-ushshaq is mostly a lie and a tasteless lie.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi wrote irreverent things in Majalis-ul-ushshaq that cast doubt upon his orthodoxy.
Kamalud-din Husain Gazur-gahi represents the Prophets and Saints as subject to earthly passion in Majalis-ul-ushshaq.
Majdu'd-din Muhammad was the son of Khwaja Pir Ahmad of Khwaf.
Majdu'd-din Muhammad was the one man of Shahrokh Mirza's Finance-office.
In Sultan Husain Mirza's Finance-office there was not at first proper order or method.
Waste and extravagance resulted in Sultan Husain Mirza's Finance-office.
The peasant did not prosper and the soldier was not satisfied in Sultan Husain Mirza's Finance-office.
Majdu'd-din Muhammad was styled Mirak when he was still parwanchi.
Babur traveled to Herat during winter.
The mountain road to Kabul took a month's journey even without delays from snow.
The low-country road to Kabul took 40 or 50 days.
Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā, Abū'l-muḥsin Mīrzā, and Muz̤affar Mīrzā personally visited Babur's tent to urge him to stay the winter.
Babur greatly wished to stay in Herat and consented to do so.
Herat had become an exceptionally splendid town under Sl. Ḥusain Mīrzā.
Babur visited his paternal aunts Pāyanda-sulṯān Begīm, Khadīja Begīm, and Apāq Begīm at Sl. Ḥusain Mīrzā's College at his Mausoleum.
Babur bent the knee with Pāyanda-sulṯān Begīm and Khadīja Begīm but not with Apāq Begīm.
Babur was assigned 'Alī-sher Beg's residence as his lodging in Herat.
Muz̤affar Mīrzā invited Babur to a wine party in the T̤arab-khāna.
Babur had not committed the sin of wine-drinking before this party.
Babur had no inclination for wine in his childhood.
Babur's father pressed wine on him, but he excused himself.
After his father's death, Khwāja Qāẓī's guidance kept Babur from wine.
Babur later developed a desire for wine due to young man's lusts and sensual passion.
Babur resolved to drink wine at the party in Herat.
Babur hesitated to drink from Muz̤affar Mīrzā's hand because Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā was like an elder brother to him.
The Mīrzās agreed to press wine on Babur only when both were present together.
Ḥāfiẓ Ḥājī sang well in the refined manner of Herat people.
Mīr Jān, a Samarkandī singer, sang loudly, harshly, and out-of-tune.
Yūsuf-i-'alī danced well as a master in music.
Muz̤affar Mīrzā gave Babur a sword-belt, a lambskin surtout, and a grey horse.
Qāsim Beg sent advice to Ẕū'n-nūn Beg and Muz̤affar Mīrzā about Babur drinking wine.
After Qāsim Beg's intervention, the Mīrzās entirely ceased to press wine upon Babur.
Babur's close circle drank rarely and with great precaution.
Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā stood to Babur as a father or elder brother.
Babur was not a skilled carver or disjointer of birds.
Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā disjointed a roast goose for Babur and had no match in such matters.
Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā gave Babur an enamelled waist-dagger, a chār-qāb, and a horse at the end of the party.
Babur rode out every day during his stay in Herat to see new sights.
Yūsuf-i-'alī Kūkūldāsh was Babur's guide during his excursions in Herat.
Babur visited approximately 40 famous sites in Herat during his stay.
Babur did not visit Sl. Ḥusain Mīrzā's Almshouse during his time in Herat.
Ma'ṣūma-sulṯān Begīm felt a great inclination towards Babur when they met.
Ḥabība-sulṯān Begīm, Ma'ṣūma-sulṯān Begīm's mother, arranged for her daughter to be brought to Kabul after Babur.
Babur left Herat on the 7th day of Sha'bān (December 24, 1506 AD).
Babur left Herat on the pretext of finding winter-quarters.
Sayyidīm 'Alī the gate-ward was one of Babur's servants who stayed in Herat and took service with the Mīrzās.
Babur showed more favor to Sayyidīm 'Alī than to other servants of Khusrau Shāh.
Sayyidīm 'Alī was given Ghaznī by Babur.
Sayyidīm 'Alī was of excellent nature and manners, a bold swordsman, and a competent and methodical man.
Sayyidīm 'Alī was greatly generous, witty, charming, and fun-loving.
Sayyidīm 'Alī practiced vice and pederasty.
Badī'u'z-zamān Mīrzā had Sayyidīm 'Alī thrown into the Harmand because of double-dealing words spoken between the Mīrzā and Shāh Beg.
Babur and his party traveled from the Langar of Mīr Ghiyās to Chach-charān.
From the almshouse to Gharjistān was an unbroken sheet of snow.
Near Chach-charān, snow was above the horses' knees.
Most of Babur's party recommended taking the Qandahār road instead of the mountain road.
Qāsim Beg insisted on taking the mountain road.
Pīr Sulṯān, a Pashāī guide, lost the road due to old age, lack of heart, or deep snow.
Qāsim Beg and his sons dismounted and trampled the snow to find the road again.
Babur sent 60 or 70 men to fetch a Hazāra guide from the valley.
The party was unable to proceed for three or four days while waiting for guides.
Babur composed a couplet during the difficult mountain journey.
Babur was one of the snow-stampers during the journey.
Babur's household of 10 or 15 men participated in stamping down the snow.
Qāsim Beg and his sons Tīngrī-bīrdī and Qaṃbar-i-'alī participated in stamping down the snow.
The snow-stampers sank to the waist or breast with each step.
After 10, 15, or 20 men stamped the snow, horses could be led over it.
Horses sank to the stirrups and could only advance 10 or 12 steps before being drawn aside.
The party reached the Khawāl-i-qūtī cave below the Zirrīn-pass after three or four days of snow-stamping.
A severe blizzard with cutting wind occurred at the Khawāl-i-qūtī cave.
The first arrivals reached the cave by daylight; others arrived until bedtime.
Some people were still in the saddle at dawn.
Babur shoveled out a shelter near the cave's mouth the size of a sitting-mat.
Babur dug his shelter breast-high but did not reach the ground.
Babur refused to sleep inside the cave while his men were outside in the snow and storm.
Babur believed it would be unmanly to sleep in comfort while his horde suffered outside.
Babur and his men encountered an extremely violent storm while traveling through mountains.
Babur reached a mountain cave called a khawāl during the storm.
Deep snow made travel extremely difficult, with only a one-man road passable.
Babur dug out a small shelter near the cave's mouth rather than entering the cave itself.
Babur refused to enter the warm cave while his men suffered outside in the snow and storm.
Babur believed it would be dishonorable to seek comfort while his soldiers endured hardship.
Babur sat through the blizzard in his dug-out shelter until the Bed-time Prayer.
Snow accumulated on Babur's head, back, and ears to a depth of four hands thick.
The cold night affected Babur's ears.
Upon learning the cave was actually spacious, Babur entered it with his men.
The cave could accommodate 50 or 60 people.
Babur's men shared their rations of cold meat and parched grain.
Babur and his army crossed a mountain pass by stamping down a road through deep snow.
They chose to go straight up the valley-bottom instead of taking the proper Zirrīn-pass route.
Many of Babur's men suffered frostbite during the crossing, including Kīpa, Sīūndūk Turkmān, and Āhī.
No one in living memory was known to have crossed that pass with such deep snow.
The deep snow ultimately enabled Babur's army to traverse pathless slopes and sudden falls.
Babur believes that all ill and good should be counted as gain if viewed correctly.
Babur's army reached the village of Yaka-aūlāng and found warm houses, sheep, grass, and supplies.
Babur and his men tarried one day in Yaka-aūlāng in good spirits.
Babur's army encountered Turkmān Hazāras with their families and cattle on their route.
Babur's men raided and plundered some Hazāra cattle-pens and tents.
Babur charged forward alone to encourage his men to engage the Hazāras on the hill.
Babur's men initially refused to advance and stood still despite his orders.
Babur had no armor, horse-mail, or arms except arrows and a quiver during the Hazāra engagement.
Babur charged forward himself, which prompted his men to follow and engage the enemy.
Babur's forces drove the Hazāras across valleys and ridges.
Babur's army captured Hazāra people, including their wives, children, and men of renown.
Babur collected some Hazāra sheep and gave them to Yārak T̤aghāī's charge.
Fourteen or fifteen Hazāra thieves were captured by Babur's forces.
Babur intended to execute the captured Hazāra thieves with torture as a warning to highwaymen.
Qāsim Beg released the Hazāra prisoners out of compassion before Babur could execute them.
Babur disapproves of showing compassion to the wicked, comparing it to doing harm to the good.
While raiding the Hazāras, Babur learned of a rebellion in Kābul.
Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā Dūghlāt and Sanjar Barlās led a rebellion in Kābul.
The rebels declared Mīrzā Khān (Wais) as supreme ruler.
The rebels laid siege to Kābul fort.
The rebels spread a false report that Babur was a prisoner.
Mullā Bābā of Pashāghar, Khalīfa, Muḥibb-i-`alī, Aḥmad-i-yūsuf, and Aḥmad-i-qāsim commanded Kābul fort.
The fort commanders strengthened the fort and kept watch against the rebels.
Babur sent a messenger with written instructions for a coordinated attack on the rebels.
Babur planned to signal his arrival by lighting fires on Minār-hill.
The fort commanders were to signal back by lighting fires on the citadel.
Babur's army traveled through extreme cold between Ẕamma-yakhshī and Minār.
Babur's forces reached Kābul undetected by the rebels.
The fort commanders saw Babur's signal fires and prepared for the coordinated attack.
Babur divided his forces, sending Sherīm T̤aghāī toward Mullā Bābā's bridge.
Babur led the left and center forces along the Bābā Lūlī road.
Mīrzā Khān was positioned in a small garden made by Aūlūgh Beg Mīrzā.
Muḥ. Ḥusain Mīrzā was in the Bāgh-i-bihisht garden.
Four men including Sayyid Qāsim, Qaṃbar-i-`alī, Sher-qulī, and Aḥmad Mughūl attacked Mīrzā Khān's quarters.
Mīrzā Khān heard the commotion and escaped on horseback.
Muḥ. Ḥusain, the armourer's younger brother, fought against the attackers.
Babur's horsemen were jammed in a narrow lane and unable to advance.
Babur ordered men to dismount and clear the road.
Nāṣir's Dost, Khwāja Muḥammad `Alī, Bābā Sher-zād, and Shāh Maḥmūd cleared the way forward.
The enemy fled after the road was cleared.
The fort commanders arrived too late to participate in the main fighting.
Muḥ. Ḥusain Mīrzā fled from the Bāgh-i-bihisht.
Dost of Sar-i-pul, whom Babur had promoted to Kotwāl, attacked Babur with a sword.
Babur was wearing a cuirass but had not fastened the gharīcha or put on his helm.
Dost struck Babur's unprotected arm with his sword.
Babur was not harmed by Dost's sword strike, which he attributes to God's grace.
Babur had recited a protective prayer before the battle.
Babur's prayer asked God to protect him from all evil from within and without.
Babur pursued enemies at the Bāgh-i-bihisht and cut at a man he believed was Mīrzā Khān's foster-brother.
The man Babur struck was Tūlik Kūkūldāsh, Mīrzā Khān's foster-brother.
A Mughūl servant of Babur's aimed an arrow at him from a roof but changed his aim upon recognizing him.
Sanjar Barlās was captured and brought before Babur with a rope around his neck.
Babur had previously given Sanjar Barlās the Nīngnahār tūmān.
Sanjar Barlās was the nephew of Shāh Begīm, Babur's Khān dādā's mother.
Babur ordered that Sanjar Barlās not be executed despite his disloyalty.
Babur visited Shāh Begīm and Khānīm after the battle.
Shāh Begīm and Khānīm were extremely agitated, upset, and ashamed.
Babur had not expected disloyalty from Shāh Begīm and Khānīm.
Mīrzā Khān was Shāh Begīm's grandson and was with her day and night.
Babur believes Shāh Begīm could have prevented the rebellion if she had opposed it.
Twice before, Babur and his mother had taken refuge with Shāh Begīm and Khānīm when separated from throne and country.
Shāh Begīm and Khānīm showed no kindness to Babur and his mother during their previous refuge.
Babur's younger cousin Mīrzā Khān and his mother Sulṯān-nigār Khānīm held valuable cultivated districts.
Babur and his mother were not given even a single village or plough-oxen by Shāh Begīm and Khānīm.
Babur's mother was Yūnas Khān's daughter.
Babur was Yūnas Khān's grandson.
Babur has given generously to members of the Chaghatāī dynasty according to their blood-relationship and position.
Babur expected loyalty from certain family members but did not receive it.
Babur and his mother took refuge with relatives twice when separated from throne and country.
Babur's relatives did not provide him and his mother with villages or plough-oxen during times of hardship.
Babur's mother was the daughter of Yūnas Khān.
Babur was the grandson of Yūnas Khān.
Babur gave Pamghān in Kābul to Shāh Begīm.
Babur gave Mandrāwar of the Lamghān to Sl. Sa`īd Khān.
Babur made Sl. Sa`īd Khān a Khān and gave him a force.
Babur treated members of his family who came to him as blood-relations.
Babur showed more favor to Chīn-tīmūr Sulṯān, Aīsān-tīmūr Sulṯān, Tūkhtā-būghā Sulṯān, and Bābā Sulṯān than to his own blood-relations.
Babur committed to recording the truth in his History.
Babur recorded both virtues and defects of fathers, elder brothers, kinsmen and strangers.
Babur showed deference to Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā despite his treason.
Babur did not execute Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā despite his deserving death because of family connections.
Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā's children were born to Babur's mother's sister Khūb-nigār Khānīm.
Babur permitted Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā to depart towards Khurāsān.
Muḥammad Ḥusain Mīrzā later slandered Babur to Shaibāq Khān.
Babur showed kindness to Mīrzā Khān by drinking sherbet first to remove his fears.
Babur found the open-lands of Bārān, the plain of Chāsh-tūpa, and the skirt of Gul-i-bahār more beautiful in Spring than other parts of Kābul.
Babur counted 34 different kinds of tulips in the Bārān area.
Babur composed an ode during his excursion to Bārān, Chāsh-tūpa, and Gul-i-bahār.
Babur enjoyed spring-excursions, hawking, and bird-shooting in the Kābul region.
Babur showed no displeasure towards Nāṣir Mīrzā when he returned humbled to Kābul.
Babur made kind enquiry about Nāṣir Mīrzā and brought him out of his confusion.
Babur refused to raid the Mahmands instead of pursuing his intended target of the Ghiljī.
Babur navigated across the plain of Kattawāz at night using the Pole-star as a guide.
Babur had been in the Kattawāz region several times before.
Babur checked his army from galloping too far ahead during the raid on the Ghiljī.
Babur's raid on the Ghiljī yielded more sheep than any other raid.
Babur did not take the Fifth from Qāsim Beg and some others as a favor.
The Fifth from the Ghiljī raid came out at 16,000 sheep.
Babur estimated that approximately 100,000 sheep were taken in the Ghiljī raid.
Babur hunted on the plain of Kattawāz where deer and wild-ass were plentiful.
Babur shot a wild-ass with arrows and then cut through its wind-pipe with his sword.
Babur's sword cut well.
The wild-ass Babur killed had a rib nearly one yard in length.
Babur shot another wild-ass during the hunt.
Babur observed that the wild-ass he killed was surprisingly fat.
Shaibāq Khān got an army to horse to march against Khurāsān.
Shāh Manṣūr the Paymaster was a traitor to his salt.
Shaibāq Khān's Aūzbegs attacked Shāh Manṣūr when he went out to meet them.
Badī`u´z-zamān Mīrzā, Muz̤affar Mīrzā, Muḥ. Barandūq Barlās and Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn were lying with their army in Bābā Khākī.
The Khurāsān Mīrzās were not decided to fight or to make Herī fort fast.
Muḥammad Barandūq Barlās was a knowledgeable man.
Muḥammad Barandūq Barlās proposed a practical plan to defend Herī.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn was brave but mean and a lover of goods.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn was not businesslike, judicious, or wise.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn had chief authority in Badī`u´z-zamān Mīrzā's presence.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn did not want Muḥ. Barandūq Beg to remain inside Herī town.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn was styled Hizabru´l-lāh (Lion of God) by Shaikhs and Mullās.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn believed flattery that he would overcome the Aūzbeg.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn put his bathing-cloth round his neck and gave thanks based on false prophecies.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn did not accept Muḥammad Barandūq Beg's sensible counsel.
Ẕū´n-nūn Arghūn did not strengthen the works of the fort or get ready fighting equipment.
Shaibāq Khān passed through Murgh-āb to near Sīr-kāī in Muḥarram 913 AH.
The Mīrzās were altogether upset when they heard of Shaibāq Khān's approach.
Ẕū'n-nūn Arghūn went out to Qarā-rabāṯ with 100 to 150 men to face 40,000 to 50,000 Aūzbegs.
Ẕū'n-nūn Arghūn was killed and his head was cut off by the Aūzbegs.
The Mīrzās reached the town at night and slept until dawn.
The Mīrzās ran away at dawn without strengthening the fort.
The Mīrzās left their mothers, sisters, wives and children to Aūzbeg captivity.
Sl. Ḥusain Mīrzā's haram was inside Ālā-qūrghān.
Pāyanda-sulṯān Begīm and Khadīja Begīm were at the head of Sl. Ḥusain Mīrzā's haram.
The harems of Badī`u'z-zamān Mīrzā and Muz̤affar Mīrzā with their children and treasure were inside Ālā-qūrghān.
The fort Ālā-qūrghān was not properly prepared for defense.
Shaibāq Khān arrived at Herī two or three days after the Mīrzās fled.
The Shaikhu'l-islām and notables went out to Shaibāq Khān with the keys of the outer-fort.
Babur's mother, sister, wife and little child were left in Aūzbeg captivity when the Mīrzās fled.
The Mīrzās did not attempt to strengthen the fort before fleeing.
Shaibāq Khān took the fort of Herī after a mine was fired that brought down a tower.
Shaibāq Khān behaved badly to the wives, children, and people of Herī.
Shaibāq Khān married Muz̤affar Mīrzā's wife Khān-zāda Khānīm without regard to the legal term.
Shaibāq Khān instructed in the exposition of the Qoran despite his own illiteracy.
Shaibāq Khān corrected the handwriting of Mullā Sl. `Alī of Mashhad and the drawing of Bih-zād.
Shaibāq Khān had his tasteless couplets read from the pulpit and hung in the Chār-sū.
Shaibāq Khān practiced early-rising and did not neglect the Five Prayers.
Shaibāq Khān had fair knowledge of the art of reciting the Qorān.
Abū'l-muḥsin Mīrzā and Kūpuk Mīrzā were killed in battle with Shaibāq Khān's forces.
Shāh Beg and Muḥammad Muqīm sent envoys to Babur with dutiful letters giving sign of amity.
Babur consulted all begs and counsellors before deciding to march.
Babur decided to get an army to horse and join the Arghūn begs.
Ḥabība-sulṯān Begīm was Babur's aunt.
Babur met Ḥabība-sulṯān Begīm in Ghaznī to receive her daughter Maṣ`ūma-sulṯān Begīm.
Babur disagreed with his army about plundering Hindu traders at Qalāt.
Babur believed that leaving aside gain for God's pleasure would bring reward from the Most High God.
Babur had previously refused to raid the Mahmand Afghāns despite his army's wishes.
Babur took only a small offering from each trader at Qalāt.
Mīrzā Khān (Wais) and `Abdu'r-razzāq Mīrzā joined Babur after fleeing from Qandahār.
Shāh Beg and Muqīm returned a rude and ill-mannered answer to Babur's invitation.
Shāh Beg stamped his letter to Babur in a manner disrespectful to Babur's rank.
Babur believed that strife-stirring words accomplish the downfall of ancient lines.
Babur was taking a bath when a false alarm occurred in his camp.
Babur mounted when ready despite the alarm and the flurried state of the begs.
Babur organized his force of approximately 2000 men (perhaps only 1000 in actual battle) on a first-rate plan.
Babur had never arrayed his men before by such a good method as at the Battle of Qandahār.
Babur selected braves for his immediate command and inscribed them by tens and fifties.
Babur's right wing was led by Mīrzā Khān (Wais), Sherīm T̤aghāī, and Yārak T̤aghāī.
Babur's left wing was led by `Abdu'r-razzāq Mīrzā and Qāsim Beg.
Babur's advance was led by Nāṣir Mīrzā and Sayyid Qāsim.
Babur's centre consisted entirely of his household with no great begs.
Pīrī Beg T̤ūrkman and 4 or 5 of his brethren defected to Babur during the battle.
Pīrī Beg was a Turkmān who came to Herī after Shāh Ismā`īl vanquished the Bāyandar sulṯāns.
Babur's right wing overcame the foe and pursued them.
Babur's left wing, though small, held the crossing-place against Muqīm's larger force.
Qaṃbar-i-`alī was wounded in the battle.
An arrow stuck in Qāsim Beg's forehead during the battle.
An arrow struck Ghūrī Barlās above the eyebrow and came out above his cheek.
Babur had only eleven men remaining with him when he advanced toward Muqīm.
Babur sounded the nagarets and advanced toward Muqīm with trust in God.
Muqīm took to flight upon hearing the nagarets.
Babur entered Qandahār after defeating Shāh Beg and Muqīm.
Shāh Beg fled toward Shāl and Mastūng after his defeat.
Muqīm fled toward Zamīn-dāwar after his defeat.
Aḥmad `Alī Tarkhān was in Qandahār fort with other brethren of Qulī Beg Arghūn.
Aḥmad `Alī Tarkhān and his companions had known attachment and good-feeling for Babur.
Babur had eleven men remaining with him during a pursuit.
Abdulla the librarian was among Babur's remaining men.
Muqim was fighting and holding his ground.
Babur trusted in God when facing a numerically inferior force.
Babur had nagarets sounded to signal his approach.
Muqim fled upon learning of Babur's approach.
Babur moved to Qandahar after putting his foe to flight.
Babur dismounted in Farrukh-zad Beg's Char-bagh near Qandahar.
Shah Beg fled towards Shal and Mastung.
Muqim fled towards Zamin-dawar.
Ahmad Ali Tarkhan was in Qandahar's fort.
Babur granted protection to the families of Quli Beg Arghun's brethren.
Babur posted Sherim Taghai and Yarim Beg at the Mashur-gate.
Babur had two or three leaderless men put to death as an example.
Babur found Muqim's treasury in the outer-fort.
Abdur-razzaq Mirza arrived at Muqim's treasury before Babur.
Babur appointed Dost-i-nasir Beg, Qul-i-bayazid, and Muhammad bakhshi to manage Muqim's treasury.
Babur appointed Khwaja Muhammad Ali, Shah Mahmud, and Taghai Shah bakhshi to manage Shah Beg's treasury.
Babur had never seen such large quantities of white money before.
Shah Beg's slave Sambal was captured and later escaped.
Babur gave the Qandahar country to Nasir Mirza.
Babur gave Nasir Mirza seven camels laden with white tankas without demanding them back.
Babur's camp contained excellent tipuchqs, camels, mules, and silken goods after the conquest.
Babur made over Muqim's retainers in Qalat to Qasim Beg.
Qasim Beg advised Babur to march away from Qandahar.
Babur bestowed Qandahar on Nasir Mirza.
Babur and his forces started for Kabul.
Spoils were apportioned at Qara-bagh where the army delayed two or three days.
Coins were apportioned by weighing in scales due to the difficulty of counting.
Babur returned to Kabul with masses of goods and treasure.
Babur concluded an alliance with Masuma-sultan Begim, daughter of Ahmad Mirza.
Babur had asked Masuma-sultan Begim in marriage at Khurasan.
Shaibaq Khan laid siege to Qandahar.
Muqim fled to Zamin-dawar and then went to Shaibaq Khan.
Shaibaq Khan came to Qandahar at the instigation of Muqim and Shah Beg.
Qasim Beg had anticipated Shaibaq Khan's arrival and urged Babur to leave Qandahar.
Babur summoned begs for counsel when news of Shaibaq Khan's siege arrived.
Shaibaq Khan and the Auzbegs possessed all countries once held by Timur Beg's descendants.
Babur considered himself weak compared to Shaibaq Khan's power.
Babur and several household-begs preferred going towards Hindustan over Badakhshan.
Babur had bestowed Qalat and the Turnuk country on Abdur-razzaq Mirza.
Abdur-razzaq Mirza left Qalat and came to Kabul when the Auzbegs besieged Qandahar.
Mirza Khan went to Badakhshan due to his relationship to Shah Begim.
Babur's maternal aunt Mihr-nigar Khanim wished to go to Badakhshan.
Babur marched out of Kabul in the month of first Jumada (September 1507 AD).
Babur took the road through Little Kabul and went down by Surkh-rabat to Quruq-sai.
Afghans between Kabul and Lamghan are thieves and abettors of thieves.
Afghans multiplied their misdeeds when they learned Babur had abandoned Kabul.
Khizr-khail, Shimu-khail, Khirilchi, and Khugiani Afghans attempted to block the pass.
Babur ordered his men to move along the mountain-side when Afghans blocked the pass.
Babur shot an Afghan in the hand during the pursuit.
Some captured Afghans were put to death by impalement as an example.
Babur dismounted near the Adinapur-fort in the Ningnahar tuman.
Babur had not planned where to camp or stay initially.
It was late in autumn when Babur's forces were near Ningnahar.
Mil Kafirs grow great quantities of rice in the Alishang tuman.
Babur's soldiers collected a mass of rice from the Kafirs.
Some Kafirs were killed during the rice collection raid.
Puran, Qasim Beg's son-in-law, was attacked by Kafirs with an axe.
Babur's braves rescued Puran from the Kafirs.
Babur's forces returned to camp with masses of provisions after one night in the Kafirs' rice-fields.
An alliance was concluded between Muqim's daughter Mah-chuchuk and Qasim Kukuldash.
Mah-chuchuk was married to Shah Hasan Arghun.
Babur sent Mulla Baba of Pashaghur back to Kabul with a few braves.
Babur marched from near Mandravar to Atar and Shiwa.
Babur visited Kunar and Nur-gal from Atar.
Babur sat on a raft for the first time when returning from Kunar to camp.
Babur was pleased with the raft experience.
Rafts came into common use after Babur's experience.
Shaibaq Khan took the outer-fort of Qandahar but could not take the citadel.
Shaibaq Khan retired from Qandahar.
Nasir Mirza left Qandahar and went to Ghazni.
Shaibaq Khan ran mines around the citadel and made several assaults.
Khwaja Muhammad Amin, Khwaja Dost Khawand, Muhammad Ali, and Shami let themselves down from the citadel walls.
Shaibaq Khan had sent his haram to Nirah-tu before besieging Qandahar.
Someone in Nirah-tu lifted up his head and got command in the fort.
Shaibaq Khan made peace and retired from Qandahar due to troubles in Nirah-tu.
Babur returned to Kabul by the Bad-i-pich road in mid-winter.
Babur ordered the date of his transit through Bad-i-pich to be cut on a stone.
Hafiz Mirak wrote the inscription for the Bad-i-pich stone.
Ustad Shah Muhammad cut the inscription on the Bad-i-pich stone.
Babur bestowed Ghazni on Nasir Mirza.
Babur gave Abdur-razzaq Mirza the Ningnahar tuman with Mandravar, Nur-valley, Kunar, and Nur-gal.
Babur ordered people to style him Padshah instead of Mirza.
Timur Beg's descendants had been styled Mirza even when ruling.
Humayun was born on Tuesday the 4th day of Zul-qada (March 6th 1506 AD) in the citadel of Kabul.
The Sun was in Pisces when Humayun was born.
Maulana Masnadī found the date of Humayun's birth in the words Sultan Humayun Khan.
A minor poet of Kabul found Humayun's birth date in Shah-i-firus-qadr.
Humayun received his name a few days after birth.
Babur went to the Char-bagh for Humayun's nativity feast when the child was five or six days old.
All begs brought gifts to Humayun's nativity feast.
A mass of white tankas was heaped up at Humayun's nativity feast.
A body of Mahmand Afghans was over-run near Muqur.
Quj Beg, Faqir-i-ali, Karim-dad, and Baba chuhra were thinking about deserting.
Babur ordered the deserters to be put to death at the top of the bazar.
Qasim Beg sent Khalifa to entreat Babur to pardon the deserters' offences.
Babur pardoned the deserters to please Qasim Beg but kept them in custody.
Khusrau Shah's retainers from Hisar and Qunduz took up a bad position towards Babur.
Mughul head-men Chilma, Ali Sayyid, Sakma, Sher-quli, and Aiku-salam rebelled.
Chaghatai retainers under Ali chuhra and Khudabakhsh rebelled.
Two or three thousand Turkman braves led by Siunduk and Shah Nazar rebelled.
The rebels were seated from the Sung-qurghan meadow to the Chalak.
Abdur-razzaq Mirza was in Dih-i-afghān during the rebellion.
Muhib-i-ali the armourer informed Khalifa and Mulla Baba of the rebel assemblies.
Musa Khwaja informed Babur of the Mughul rebellion.
Babur feigned disregard when informed of the rebellion.
Babur went towards the harems in the Yurunchqa-garden and Bagh-i-khilwat.
Babur went with the chief-slave towards the town and along the ditch.
Khwaja Muhammad Ali met Babur at the Iron-gate.
Babur composed the annals of 914 AH not long before his last illness and death.
There is a gap in Babur's autobiography of nearly eleven years between 914 AH and 925 AH.
Babur intended to describe events of the unchronicled years.
A mutiny against Babur was crushed by the victory of 500 loyalists over 3,000 rebels.
Babur defeated five champions of his adversaries in single combat during the mutiny.
Kabul was tranquil in November when Said Khan arrived seeking Babur's protection.
Said Khan was 21 years old when he arrived in Kabul.
Said Khan had been defeated by his brother Mansur at Almatu.
Shaibani murdered Mahmud Khan and his six sons in 914 AH.
Shaibani murdered Muhammad Husain Mirza and other Dughlat sultans in 914 AH.
Hostilities began between Shah Ismail Safawi and Muhammad Shaibani Khan in 915 AH.
Haider Mirza Dughlat was eleven years old when he came to Babur's protection in Kabul.
Haider Mirza lived under Babur's protection from his eleventh to his thirteenth year.
Haider Mirza served under Said Khan for 19 years in Kashghar.
