Haider Mirza later went to Babur's sons Kamran and Humayun in Hindustan.
Ismail defeated Shaibani near Merv in 916 AH.
Babur traveled through the winter-route of Ab-dara to reach Qunduz.
Babur kept the Ramzan Feast in Bamian on his way to Qunduz.
Babur reached Qunduz in Shawwal (January 1511 AD).
About 20,000 Mughuls came from Merv to Qunduz after Shaibani's defeat.
There were approximately 5,000 Chaghatais compared to 20,000 Mughuls.
Sherim Taghai and Ayub Begchik were chiefs of the Mughul bodies.
The Mughul chiefs offered the Mughul Khanship to Said Khan.
Said Khan rejected the offer to injure Babur on grounds of right and gratitude.
Babur sent Said Khan to Andijan in response to an urgent petition for help.
Said Khan was made Khan and departed on Safar 14th 917 AH (May 13th 1511 AD).
Most of the Mughuls departed with Said Khan to Andijan.
Babur's sister Khan-zada arrived in Qunduz safe-returned under escort of the Shah's troops.
Khan-zada's successive husbands Shaibani and Sayyid Hadi died in the battle of Merv.
An envoy from Ismail arrived in Qunduz offering friendship to Babur.
Babur sent Mirza Khan with thanks and gifts to acknowledge Ismail's courtesies.
Mirza Khan conveyed protestations of good faith and a request for military assistance to Ismail.
Ismail's request for help was granted under hard conditions.
Babur took post at the Pul-i-sangin on the Surkh-ab during his second attempt on Hisar.
Babur lay at Pul-i-sangin for a month awaiting reinforcement.
Timur had previously wrought out success at Pul-i-sangin against great odds.
The Auzbegs swam the Surkh-ab below the bridge and attacked Babur.
Babur retired to better ground amongst the mountain fastnesses of a local Ab-dara.
The Auzbegs were utterly routed with great loss in men at Pul-i-sangin.
The Auzbegs were pursued to Darband-i-ahanin on the Hisar border.
Kuchum Khan was Shaibani's successor as Auzbeq Khaqan.
Mahdi, Hamza and Hamza's son Mamak were brought before Babur after the battle.
Babur retaliated in blood against Mahdi and Hamza for the deaths of his kinsmen.
Haider Mirza was 12 years old when he witnessed the battle at Pul-i-sangin.
Haider Mirza watched the battle with keen eye as his own first fight.
Many local tribesmen joined Babur after the battle near Hisar.
A large body of Ismail's troops under Ahmad Beg Safawi, Ali Khan Istilju and Shahrukh Afshār joined Babur.
Babur promised to read Ismail's name in the khutba if he reconquered Transoxiana.
Babur promised to stamp Ismail's name on coins together with those of the Twelve Imams.
Babur promised to work to destroy the power of the Auzbegs.
The Persian chiefs advised a move direct on Bukhara and Samarkand.
Babur's councillors agreed that Bukhara was empty of troops and full of fools.
Ubaid Khan threw himself into Qarshi.
Babur pursued Ubaid Khan up 100 miles of caravan-road into Bukhara.
Ubaid Khan and his garrison were swept into the open land of Turkistan.
Many sultans had collected in Samarkand, some being fugitives from Pul-i-sangin.
Babur dismissed the Persian commanders when he reached Bukhara with gifts and thanks.
Haider Mirza was present and fixed Bukhara as the place of dismissal.
Babur re-entered Samarkand in mid-Rajab 917 AH (October 1511 AD).
Babur had been absent from Samarkand for 9 years before re-entering it.
Samarkand was decorated for Babur's welcome.
Babur was able to keep Samarkand for only eight months.
Auzbeq strength was compacted by Sunni zeal against Babur.
Sunni hatred of Ismail's suzerainty was intensified by dread of his dominance.
Babur's alliance with Ismail was dangerous.
Ismail's fanatical barbarism to pious and learned Sunnis was notable in Heri.
Babur dismissed his Persian helpers and behaved coldly to the Shah's envoy Muhammad Jan.
Muhammad Jan was Najm Sani's Lord of the Gate.
Muhammad Jan reported Babur as arrogant, in opposition, and unwilling to fulfill his compact.
Ismail ordered Najm Sani to bring Babur to order after hearing Muhammad Jan's report.
Babur's subservience to Ismail was clearly shown in Samarkand.
Babur was a man of his word regarding his promises to Ismail.
Sunni irritation against Babur waxed and did not wane.
Babur knew himself impotent against the Auzbegs without foreign aid.
Babur expected attack from the Auzbegs and knew it was preparing.
An envoy reported that Bābur was arrogant, in opposition, and unwilling to fulfill his compact.
Bābur attempted success unaided against `Ubaid Khān and was worsted.
Bābur had promised and was a man of his word.
Sunnī irritation against Bābur waxed and did not wane.
Bābur knew himself impotent against the Aūzbegs unless he had foreign aid.
Bābur expected attack from the Aūzbegs and knew it was preparing.
Bābur was confronted by great passions including ambition, religious fervour, sectarian bigotry and fear.
Khān-zāda was a member of Bābur's family who rejoined him after marriage with an Aūzbeg.
Bābur's half-sister Yādgār-sulṯān had fallen to the share of Ḥamza Sulṯān's son `Abdu'l-laṯīf in 908 AH.
Yādgār-sulṯān returned to her own people after her half-brother defeated her husband's family.
`Ubaid Khān vowed that if successful, he would thenceforth strictly observe Musalmān Law.
`Ubaid Khān made his vow at the shrine of Khwāja Aḥmad Yasawī.
Bābur's vow was set against the Hindū whom he saw as a heathen.
`Ubaid's vow was set against Bābur whom he saw as a heretic.
Bābur went from Samarkand to meet `Ubaid Khān in Ṣafar (April-May).
Bābur marched to put the Aūzbeg down with a small force only, against the advice of prudent men.
Muḥammad Mazīd Tarkhān advised against Bābur going out unprepared and without reinforcement.
Bābur paid no attention to the advice of prudent men and marched for Bukhārā.
Bābur was rendered more daring by news that the enemy had retired some stages.
`Ubaid Khān was in great force and many Aūzbegs perished in the encounter.
Bābur was compelled to take refuge in Bukhārā after the encounter near Kul-i-malik.
The encounter at Kul-i-malik took place in Ṣafar 918 AH. (April-May 1512 AD.).
It was not possible for Bābur to maintain a footing in Samarkand.
Bābur collected his family and train and betook himself to Ḥiṣār.
Māhīm and her children Humāyūn, Mihr-jahān and Bārbūl went with Bābur to Ḥiṣār.
The motherless Ma`ṣūma went with Bābur to Ḥiṣār.
Gul-rukh with her son Kāmrān went with Bābur to Ḥiṣār.
Ḥaidar did not travel with Bābur on the journey to Ḥiṣār, being still invalided in Samarkand.
Bābur was still under 30 years of age after experiencing such gains and such losses.
The Aūzbegs re-occupied Bukhārā and Samarkand without harm done to the towns-people after Bābur's departure.
Bābur with Mīrzā Khān's help closed the streets of Ḥiṣār by massive earth-works.
The sulṯāns were convinced that the defenders of Ḥiṣār were ready to spend the last drop of their blood in holding it.
The sulṯāns retired from Ḥiṣār without attack.
Bairām Beg had sent a force of 300 men only to Bābur.
Najm S̤ānī had some 11,000 men on his corrective mission against Bābur.
Najm S̤ānī heard of the defeat at Kul-i-malik and the flight to Ḥiṣār when he reached the Khurāsān frontier.
Najm S̤ānī gathered other troops from Harāt and elsewhere and advanced to Balkh.
Najm S̤ānī stayed in Balkh for 20 days with Bairām Beg.
Repeated request for help is said to have come from Bābur to Najm S̤ānī.
Help was given to Bābur, some sources say without the Shāh's permission.
Najm S̤ānī paid Bābur the civility of going several miles out from his camp to give him honouring reception.
The combined armies took Khuzār and moved on to Qarshī.
Bābur wished to pass by Qarshī as it had been passed by on his previous march for Bukhārā.
Najm S̤ānī refused to pass on from Qarshī, saying it must be taken because it was `Ubaidu'l-lāh Khān's nest.
`Ubaid's uncle Shaikhīm Mīrzā was in Qarshī.
Qarshī was captured and the Aūzbeg garrison was put to the sword.
Despite Bābur's earnest entreaties, all the towns-people of Qarshī, 15,000 persons it is said, were massacred.
Banā'ī was amongst the victims massacred at Qarshī.
The massacre at Qarshī roused the utmost anger against Najm S̤ānī.
The massacre at Qarshī disgusted Bābur, not only through its merciless slaughter but because it made clear the disregard in which he was held by Najm S̤ānī.
An Aūzbeg force approached under Tīmūr and Abū-sa`īd when Najm S̤ānī got within a few miles of Bukhārā.
Najm S̤ānī sent Bairām Beg to attack the Aūzbeg force.
The Aūzbeg force drew off to the north and threw themselves into Ghaj-davān.
The combined armies followed the Aūzbeg force to Ghaj-davān.
Najm S̤ānī's move placed him across the Zar-afshān, on the border of the desert with which the Aūzbegs were familiar.
The siege at Ghaj-davān lasted some duration.
At first there were minor engagements at Ghaj-davān, ending with varying success.
Provisions and provender became scarce during the siege at Ghaj-davān.
Najm S̤ānī's officers urged retirement from Ghaj-davān.
Bābur urged retirement from Ghaj-davān.
Najm S̤ānī would listen to none of the officers or Bābur urging retirement.
`Ubaid Khān rode out from Bukhārā at the head of excellent troops.
`Ubaid Khān joined the Ghaj-davān garrison.
The united Aūzbegs posted themselves in the suburbs where walled lanes and gardens narrowed the field.
A battle was fought on Tuesday Ramẓān 3rd (Nov. 12th) in which Najm S̤ānī's army was routed and he himself slain.
Najm S̤ānī's men may have been weakened by privation and had wished to retire.
Bābur, who was the reserve, did not fight at all in the battle at Ghaj-davān.
The belief that Bābur had failed Najm S̤ānī persisted at the Persian Court.
Bābur's inaction was made a reproach to his son Humāyūn in 951 AH. (1544 AD.).
Bābur was subjected to great danger by a sudden attack made upon him by the Mughūls where he lay at night in his camp outside Ḥiṣār.
Firishta says Bābur had reproached the Mughūls for their misconduct.
Bābur was roused from his sleep by the noise of his guards' resistance to the Mughūl attack.
Bābur escaped with difficulty and without a single attendant into the fort.
The conspirators plundered Bābur's camp and withdrew to Qarā-tīgīn.
Bābur was in no position to oppose the Mughūls.
Bābur left a few men in Ḥiṣār and went to Mīrzā Khān in Qūndūz.
After Bābur left, Ḥiṣār endured a desolating famine, a phenomenal snowfall and the ravages of the Mughūls.
`Ubaid Khān avenged Bābur on the Mughūl horde.
`Ubaid Khān encamped outside the position the Mughūls had taken up in Wakhsh defended by river, hills and snow.
`Ubaid Khān waited till a road thawed, then fell upon the Mughūls and avenged the year's misery they had inflicted on the Ḥiṣārīs.
The Mughūls' villainy lost Ḥiṣār to Bābur and gained it for the Aūzbeg.
The Mughūls had for chiefs men who when Sa`īd went to Andijān, elected to stay with Bābur.
Ayūb Begchīk was one of the three Mughūl chiefs named by Ḥaidar.
Ayūb Begchīk repented his disloyalty to Bābur.
Ayūb Begchīk told Sa`īd Khān that his faithlessness to Bābur in Ḥiṣār was lacerating his bowels and killing him with remorse.
Ayūb Begchīk had broken an oath at the instigation of the Mughūl chiefs.
Ḥaidar left Bābur in Rajab (Sep.-Oct.) to go to Sa`id in Andijān.
Ḥaidar made a beginning of his 19 years spell of service when he left Bābur.
Bābur may have spent 919 AH. in Khishm.
During two or three months of 919 AH., Bābur had one of the Shāh's retainers in his service, Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd.
Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd had fled from Ghaj-davān to Balkh.
Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd heard at Balkh that the Balkhīs favoured an Aūzbeg chief whose coming was announced.
Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd went to Bābur after hearing the news at Balkh.
In Jumāda 11 (August), Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd heard that the Aūzbeg sultan had left Balkh.
Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd returned to Balkh but was not admitted.
The Balkhīs feared reprisals for their welcome to the Aūzbeg.
Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Maḥmūd went on into Khurāsān and was there killed.
Balkh was recaptured for the Shāh by Deo Sulṯān.
Ḥaidar writes of Bābur as though he were in Qūndūz in 920 AH.
Bābur suffered the greatest misery and want in 920 AH.
Bābur bore his misery and want with his accustomed courtesy and patience.
Bābur, at last, despairing of success in recovering Ḥiṣār, went back to Kābul.
Bābur made a stay in Khwāst during which his daughter Gul-rang was born.
Bābur reached Kābul at the end of 920 AH., after the privation of winter.
When Bābur re-occupied Samarkand in 917 AH., he had given Kābul to his half-brother Nāṣir Mīrzā.
Nāṣir Mīrzā received Bābur with warm welcome and protestations of devotion and respect.
Nāṣir Mīrzā spoke of having guarded Kābul for Bābur.
Nāṣir Mīrzā asked permission to return to his own old fief Ghaznī.
Nāṣir Mīrzā's behaviour made a deep impression on Bābur.
Nāṣir Mīrzā died shortly after his return to Ghaznī.
Disputes arose amongst the various commanders who were in Ghaznī after Nāṣir Mīrzā's death.
Sherīm T̤aghāī was one of the commanders involved in the disputes in Ghaznī.
The main strength of the tumult in Ghaznī was given by the Mughūls.
Bābā of Pashāghar, an old servant, took part in the tumult in Ghaznī.
Ḥaidar did not know precisely the cause of the dispute in Ghaznī.
Ḥaidar attributes the dispute in Ghaznī to possession taken by Satan of the brains of the chiefs.
A regular battle ensued from the disputes in Ghaznī.
Dost Beg distinguished himself in the regular battle in Ghaznī.
Qāsim Beg's son Qaṃbar-i-`alī hurried down from Qūndūz and did his good part to win the battle for Bābur.
Many of the rioters in Ghaznī were killed, others fled to Kāshghar.
Sherīm T̤aghāī was one of the rioters who fled to Kāshghar.
Sa`īd Khān gave Sherīm T̤aghāī no welcome in Kāshghar.
Sherīm T̤aghāī fell back on Bābur after being rejected by Sa`īd Khān.
Bābur showed Sherīm T̤aghāī his usual benevolence.
Bābur turned his eyes from Sherīm T̤aghāī's offences and looked only at his past services.
Sherīm T̤aghāī died shortly after returning to Bābur.
922 AH. may have been spent in and near Kābul in the quiet promoted by the dispersion of the Mughūls.
Bābur's son Muḥammad known as `Askarī was born in 922 AH.
Muḥammad `Askarī was born in camp.
Muḥammad `Askarī was the son of Gulrukh Begchīk.
Muḥammad `Askarī was the full-brother of Kāmrān.
Babur attributed a dispute to possession of the chiefs' brains by Satan, resulting in vain-glory and wickedness.
Dost Beg distinguished himself in a regular battle.
Qasim Beg's son Qambar-i-'ali came from Qunduz and helped Babur win the battle.
Sherim Taghai fled to Kashghar after the battle.
Sa'id Khan did not welcome Sherim Taghai in Kashghar.
Babur showed Sherim Taghai his usual benevolence and forgave his offences.
Babur's son Muhammad, known as 'Askari, was born in camp in 922 AH.
'Askari was the son of Gulrukh Begchik and full-brother of Kamran.
Babur visited Balkh in 923 AH.
Muhammad-i-zaman was the son of Badi'u'z-zaman Bai-qara.
Muhammad-i-zaman was in Shah Ismail's service in 920 AH.
Babur invited Muhammad-i-zaman to Kabul.
Mahdi Khwaja dissuaded Muhammad-i-zaman from going to Kabul.
Babur was angered by Muhammad-i-zaman's non-arrival.
Babur pursued Muhammad-i-zaman to punish him but did not reach Ghurjistan.
Babur treated Muhammad-i-zaman with kindness after his capture.
Babur gave his daughter Ma'suma in marriage to Muhammad-i-zaman.
Babur sent Muhammad-i-zaman to Balkh after the marriage.
Muhammad-i-zaman was 21 years old at the time of his marriage.
Ma'suma was 9 years old at the time of her marriage.
Babur marched for Qandahar in 923 AH.
Babur became ill near Qandahar, which ended the campaign peacefully.
Babur had not forgiven Muqim for taking Kabul 14 years earlier.
Babur took away Mahchuchak, Muqim's daughter, in 913 AH.
There were many unemployed Mirzas in Kabul in 923 AH.
Shah Beg was a prisoner of Shah Ismail in the Harat country.
Sambal's patience led to Shah Beg's release.
Shah Hasan, Shah Beg's son, came to Babur after quarreling with his father in 924 AH.
Shah Hasan stayed with Babur for some two years.
Shah Hasan was married to Khalifa's daughter Gul-barg.
Babur captured Chaghān-sarāī in 924 AH.
Babur captured the fortress at the head of the valley of Baba-qara in 924 AH.
A violent earthquake occurred in the dale of Chandawal on January 3rd, 925 AH.
Babur attacked the fort of Bajaur on January 4th, 925 AH.
The Bajauris refused to surrender the fort of Bajaur.
Dost Beg led the left wing in the attack on Bajaur.
Mulla 'Abdul-maluk of Khwast advanced under the fort ramparts on his horse.
Mulla Tirik-i-'ali and a servant of Tingri-birdi crossed swords with the enemy and brought in their heads.
The Bajauris had never before seen matchlocks.
Ustad 'Ali-quli shot down five men with his matchlock on the first day.
Wali the Treasurer brought down two men with his matchlock.
Approximately 7, 8, or 10 Bajauris fell to matchlock fire on the first day.
The fort of Bajaur was taken in two or three astronomical hours on January 7th, 925 AH.
More than 3000 Bajauris were killed in the massacre.
Babur bestowed the country of Bajaur on Khwaja Kalan.
Babur assigned a large number of braves to reinforce Khwaja Kalan.
Khwaja Kalan requested that remaining prisoners be pardoned and reunited with their families.
Babur sent heads of sultans to Kabul, Badakhshan, Qunduz, and Balkh with news of the victory.
Shah Mansur Yusuf-zai was an envoy from his tribe and witnessed the victory.
Babur gave Shah Mansur a coat and wrote threatening orders to the Yusuf-zai.
Babur ordered a tower of heads to be set up on January 11th, 925 AH.
Babur visited the Bajaur fort on January 12th, 925 AH.
There was a wine-party in Khwaja Kalan's house during Babur's visit.
Wine and fruit in Bajaur came from adjacent parts of Kafiristān.
Babur bestowed a standard on Khwaja Kalan on January 16th, 925 AH.
Babur composed a verse about parting with his friend Khwaja Kalan.
Sl. 'Ala'u'd-din of Sawad came to wait on Babur on January 19th, 925 AH.
Babur hunted on the hill between Bajaur and Chandawal on January 20th, 925 AH.
Babur observed that the bughu-maral had become quite black except for their tails.
A black sarig-qush was taken during the hunt on January 20th, 925 AH.
An eagle took a deer during the hunt on January 20th, 925 AH.
Babur marched for Sawad on January 21st, 925 AH with the intention of attacking the Yusuf-zai Afghans.
Shah Mansur Yusuf-zai brought intoxicating confections to Babur.
Babur ate one portion of the confection and became remarkably intoxicated.
Gadai Taghai and 'Abdul-lah the librarian also ate portions of the confection.
Babur was too intoxicated to attend a council of begs at Evening Prayer.
Babur noted that the confection produced less intoxication in his later years.
An impost of 4000 ass-loads of rice was laid on the Kahrāj people on January 22nd, 925 AH.
Sl. Wais of Sawad was sent to collect the rice impost.
The Kahrāj people had never before borne such a burden and were brought to ruin.
Snow fell ankle-deep near Kahrāj on January 22nd, 925 AH, which was rare for the area.
Hindu Beg was sent to raid Panj-kūra on January 25th, 925 AH.
Panj-kūra lies more than half-way up the mountain.
The people of Panj-kūra had fled before the raid.
Babur's men brought beasts and masses of corn from Panj-kūra.
Quj Beg was sent to raid on January 26th, 925 AH.
Babur dismounted at the village of Mandish on January 27th, 925 AH to get corn for the army.
Several children born of Humayun's mother had not lived.
Hind-al was not yet born at the time of the campaign.
Mahim wrote to Babur requesting that he give her Hind-al, whether boy or girl.
Babur sent Yusuf-i-'ali the stirrup-holder to Kabul with letters bestowing Hind-al on Mahim.
Babur had a stone platform constructed in the Mandish-country large enough to hold the tents of the advance-camp.
All household and soldiers carried stones for the platform one by one.
Babur asked for a daughter of Malik Shah Mansur to conciliate the Yusuf-zai horde.
Malik Shah Mansur was a well-wisher of Babur and came to him as an envoy.
News came that Malik Shah Mansur's daughter was on her way with the Yusuf-zai tribute.
Babur held a wine-party and invited Sl. 'Ala'u'd-din of Sawad.
Sl. 'Ala'u'd-din was given a seat and special dress of honour at the wine-party.
Taus Khan, Shah Mansur's younger brother, brought Malik Shah Mansur's daughter to Babur's ground.
Yusuf-i-'ali the taster was sent to get the Bi-sut people and take them to Bajaur-fort.
Written orders were sent to Kabul for the army there to join Babur.
Babur asked for a daughter of Malik Shāh Manṣūr to conciliate the Yūsuf-zāī horde.
Babur held a wine-party at Evening Prayer to which Sl. `Alā'u'd-dīn was invited and given a seat and special dress of honour.
Babur marched from the valley on Sunday the 28th.
Babur sent Yūsuf'i-`alī the taster to get the Bī-sūt people on the march and take them to Bajaur-fort.
Babur dismounted at the confluence of the waters of Bajaur and Panj-kūra on Friday the 3rd of Ṣafar.
Babur attended a drinking-party in Khwāja Kalān's house in Bajaur.
Babur summoned the begs and Dilazāk Afghān headmen on Tuesday the 7th of the month for consultation.
Babur decided to march along the Aṃbahar and Pānī-mānī road rather than into Sawād due to lack of corn.
Babur bestowed horses and robes on Sl. Wais and Sl. `Alā'u'd-dīn of Sawād and gave them leave to go.
Babur left Shāh Manṣūr's daughter in Bajaur-fort until the return of the army.
Babur gave leave to Khwāja Kalān and sent heavy baggage and worn-out horses into Lamghān.
Babur put Khwāja Mīr-i-mīrān in charge of the camel baggage-train.
Babur crossed the Ambahar-pass and another great pass while riding light for the raid.
Babur sent Aūghān-bīrdī forward to learn how things were with the Afghāns.
Aūghān-bīrdī overcame an Afghān and cut his head off but dropped it on the road.
Babur marched on, crossed the Sawād-water, and remounted at Bed-time Prayer to ride swiftly on.
Babur sent raiders ahead who killed Afghāns and brought prisoners, cattle and flocks.
Babur dismounted near Kātlāng and sent a guide to meet the baggage-train.
Babur ordered the tomb of the heretic qalandar Shahbāz destroyed and levelled with the ground.
Babur had long desired to move on Hindūstān since coming into Kābul.
Babur thought a raid into Bhīra might yield spoils for his soldiers.
Babur's well-wishers advised against entering Hindūstān without proper preparation.
Babur disregarded the advice and set off for the ford through the water of Sind.
Babur sent Mīr Muḥammad the raftsman to examine the Sind-river above and below the ford.
Babur went to hunt rhinoceros on the Sawātī side on February 16th.
Babur's hunters killed a rhinoceros calf that was scorched by fire.
Some Nīl-ābīs brought Babur a horse in mail and 300 shāhrukhīs as an offering at the ford.
Babur crossed the Sind-river with his army on February 17th.
Babur marched on and dismounted near the water of Kacha-kot.
Sayyid Qāsim Lord of the Gate overcame a few Gujūrs and brought in 4 or 5 of their heads.
Babur crossed the Kacha-kot-water and the Sangdakī-pass on February 18th.
Many horses were left on the road during the long march on February 19th due to weakness.
The Koh-i-jūd mountain-range lies fourteen miles north of Bhīra.
Two tribes, the Jūd and Janjūha, are descendants from one parent-source and rule the Koh-i-jūd.
The Jūd and Janjūha rule is friendly and brotherly with fixed portions determined by ancient custom.
The agreement on the Koh-i-jūd is to give one shāhrukhī for each yoke of oxen and seven for headship in a household.
The Koh-i-jūd runs for 14 miles along the Bhīra country and extends to the foot of Dīn-kot on the Sind-river.
The principal headman of the Jūd and Janjūha gets the title of Rāī.
The Janjūha headmen are maternal uncles of Langar Khan.
Malik Hast was the ruler of the people near the Sūhān-water.
Malik Hast's name originally was Asad but Hindūstānīs dropped a vowel making it Hast.
Malik Hast was approximately 22 or 23 years old.
Babur ordered that no hurt or harm be done to the flocks, herds, cotton-ends and broken needles of the hillmen.
Babur had always desired to possess Hindūstān.
Babur pictured Bhīra, Khūsh-āb, Chīn-āb and Chīnīūt as his own because they had once been held by the Turk.
Babur was resolved to get these countries into his hands whether peacefully or by force.
Babur dismounted at Kalda-kahār amongst fields of densely-growing corn on February 20th.
Kalda-kahār is some 20 miles north of Bhīra, a level land shut in amongst the Jūd mountains.
A lake in Kalda-kahār is some six miles round, formed from the in-gatherings of rain from all sides.
Babur made a garden at Kalda-kahār called the Bāgh-i-ṣafā.
Babur rode from Kalda-kahār at dawn on February 21st.
Babur reached the top of the Hamtātū-pass where local people brought him a humble gift.
Babur sent messengers to speak the Bhīra people fair and assure them of his protection.
Babur sent seven or eight men ahead under Qurbān of Chīrkh and `Abdu'l-malūk of Khwāst.
Babur arrayed his forces in right and left and centre after crossing the pass.
Servants of Daulat Khān and notables of Bhīra brought Babur a horse and camel as an offering.
Babur dismounted on the east of Bhīra on the bank of the Bahat on February 21st.
Tīmūr Beg had gone into Hindūstān and his descendants held Bhīra, Khūsh-āb, Chīn-āb and Chīnīūt.
After the death of Sl. Mas`ūd Mīrzā and his son `Alī Asghar Mīrzā, the sons of Mīr `Alī Beg got possession of Kābul, Zābul and the Hindūstān countries.
In Sl. Abū-sa`īd Mīrzā's time, Kābul and Zābul went from their hands but the Hindūstān countries remained.
In 910 AH (1504 AD), when Babur first came into Kābul, Sayyid `Alī Khān governed Bhīra, Khūsh-āb and Chīn-āb.
Sayyid `Alī Khān read the khuṯba for Sikandar son of Buhlūl and was subject to him.
When Babur led an army out in 910 AH, Sayyid `Alī Khān left Bhīra in terror and crossed the Bahat-water.
Sayyid `Alī Khān seated himself in Sher-kot, one of the villages of Bhīra.
The Afghāns became suspicious of Sayyid `Alī Khān on Babur's account.
Sayyid `Alī Khān made the countries over to Daulat Khān, the governor in Lāhūr.
Daulat Khān gave the countries to his eldest son `Alī Khān.
Sl. Mas`ūd Mīrzā was the son of Sūyūrghatmīsh Mīrzā, son of Shāhrukh Mīrzā, son of Tīmūr.
Sl. Mas`ūd Mīrzā was known as Sl. Mas`ūd Kābulī because the government of Kābul and Zābul depended on him.
In 910 AH, Babur crossed Khaibar with the wish to enter Hindūstān and reached Parashāwūr.
Bāqī Chaghānīānī insisted on a move against Lower Bangash.
Babur's forces raided Afghāns in Lower Bangash and the Bannū plain.
Tātār Khān, father of Daulat Khān, was one of six or seven sardārs who made Buhlūl Pādshāh.
Tātār Khān held the country north of the Satluj and Sahrind with revenues exceeding 3 krūrs.
Sl. Sikandar (Lūdī) took Tātār Khān's countries from his sons and gave only Lāhūr to Daulat Khān.
Babur visited Bhīra on February 22nd.
Sangur Khān Janjūha came and made offering of a horse to Babur and did obeisance.
Babur summoned the headmen and chauderis of Bhīra on Wednesday the 22nd of the month.
Babur agreed on a sum of 400,000 shāhrukhīs as the price of peace with Bhīra.
Babur made an excursion in a boat and ate a confection on February 23rd.
Babur sent Ḥaidar the standard-bearer to the Bilūchīs located in Bhīra and Khūsh-āb.
The Bilūchīs made an offering of an almond-coloured tīpūchāq horse to Babur.
Babur learned that some soldiery were behaving without sense and laying hands on Bhīra people.
Babur sent persons who caused senseless soldiers to meet their death-doom or had their noses slit.
Babur received a dutiful letter from the Khūshābīs on Friday.
Babur appointed Shāh Ḥasan to go to Khūsh-āb on Saturday the 25th of the month.
Heavy rain fell on Sunday causing water to cover all the plain.
A small brackish stream between Bhīra and the gardens became like a great river before Mid-day Prayer.
Babur rode out to watch the water coming down during the rain and storm.
Babur received an offering of an almond-coloured horse.
Some soldiers were behaving without sense and laying hands on Bhīra people.
Babur ordered senseless soldiers to be executed or have their noses slit.
A dutiful letter came from the Khūshābīs on Friday, February 25th.
Shāh Ḥasan was appointed to go to Khūsh-āb.
Heavy rain fell on Sunday, February 27th, covering the plain with water.
Babur crossed the flooded water with his horse swimming.
The army was much alarmed and abandoned tents and heavy baggage.
Boats were brought from the river to help the army cross with their tents and baggage.
Babur spent a night in Bhīra-fort, which they called Jahān-nūma.
The country was divided into four districts to resolve payment disputes.
Khalīfa, Qūj Beg, Nāṣir's Dost, Sayyid Qāsim, and Muḥibb-i-`alī were appointed to oversee the districts.
Babur intended to picture the countries once occupied by the Turk as his own without over-running or plundering.
Babur sent an envoy to Sultan Ibrāhīm to ask for countries that had depended on the Turk.
Mullā Murshid was appointed as envoy on Thursday, March 3rd.
Babur sent a goshawk to Sultan Ibrāhīm.
Babur believed people in Hindūstān, especially the Afghāns, lacked sense and wisdom.
Daulat Khān kept Babur's envoy several days in Lāhūr without seeing him.
The envoy returned to Kābul a few months later without bringing a reply.
Hind-āl was born on Friday, March 4th.
Babur took the news of Hind-āl's birth as an omen during the expedition into Hindūstān.
Babur named his son Hind-āl, meaning Taking of Hind.
Babur rode out for an excursion on March 5th and drank `araq in a boat.
Babur sat in a tālār on the flat top of the boat with a few people.
Babur became disgusted by the bad flavour of `araq and preferred ma'jūn instead.
Babur instructed his companions to allow both drinkers and ma'jūn eaters without making allusions to each other.
The party became intolerable due to wild talk from the drinkers and was broken up.
The country of Bhīra was given to Hindū Beg on Monday, March 7th.
The Chīn-āb country was bestowed on Ḥusain Aīkrak on Tuesday, March 8th.
Minūchihr Khān came and waited on Babur after starting from Hindūstān.
Tātār Khān Kakar had detained Minūchihr Khān and made him a son-in-law by giving him his daughter.
Tātār Kakar and Hātī Kakar were two descendants of one forefather and paternal-uncles' sons.
Tātār Kakar's place was named Parhāla.
Hātī Kakar had made Bābū Khān's fief Kālanjar look towards himself.
Tātār Kakar had seen Daulat Khān and looked to him with complete obedience.
Hātī Kakar had not seen Daulat Khān and his attitude towards him was bad and turbulent.
Hātī Kakar killed Tātār Kakar and took his country, wives, and possessions.
Babur drank `araq on a boat excursion with Dost Beg, Mīrzā Qulī, and others.
Babur was very drunk and could not recall galloping loose-rein into camp with torches.
Babur vomited a good deal after reaching his quarters.
On March 11th, Babur rode out and crossed the water by boat to view orchards and sugar-cultivation lands.
Babur had water drawn from a wheel with buckets and asked particulars about getting it out.
Minūchihr Khān was given a confection so strong that two people had to give him their arms to keep him standing.
Babur slept in the boat that night and went back to camp near dawn.
On March 12th, the Sun entered the Ram.
Shāh Ḥasan returned from Khūsh-āb having settled peaceably and bringing part of the assessed money.
Babur appointed Shāh Muḥammad and Dost Beg as Keepers of the Seal to reinforce Hindū Beg in Bhīra.
Khūsh-āb was bestowed on Langar Khān with a standard.
Babur settled that Langar Khān was to help Hindū Beg.
Babur appointed Minūchihr Khān, Naẕar-i-`alī Turk, Sangar Khān Janjūha, and Malik Hast Janjūha to help Hindū Beg.
Babur marched for Kābul from Bhīra on Sunday, March 13th.
It rained amazingly on March 13th, affecting people with and without rain-cloaks equally.
The Janjūhas represented that Hātī Kakar was a bad man who robbed on roads and brought men to ruin.
Babur agreed that Hātī Kakar should either be driven out or severely punished.
Babur left Khwāja Mīr-i-mīrān and Nāṣir's Mīrīm with the camp on March 14th.
Babur moved on Hātī Kakar, who was in Parhāla after killing Tātār.
Babur rode the night through with a Gujūr servant named Sar-u-pā as guide.
Babur sent Beg Muḥammad Mughūl back to the camp at dawn.
Babur put on mail at breakfast-time and moved forward faster.
Parhāla stands amongst ravines with two roads, both one-man roads.
Parhāla has no breast-work or battlement but has perpendicular sides shooting down 7, 8, or 10 yards.
Hātī Kakar had 30 to 40 men in armour with horses in mail and a mass of foot-soldiers.
Dost Beg led a reserve forward and made a strong attack, dismounting a number of Hātī's men.
Hātī Kakar took to flight and could not make a stand in the narrow places.
Hātī Kakar could not make the fort fast when he got back into it.
Hātī Kakar rode light and made his flight good.
Dost Beg did very well in the attack and received recompense added to renown.
Babur dismounted at Tātār Kakar's dwelling in the fort.
Amīn-i-muḥammad Tarkhān Argkūn and Qarācha were sent to meet the camp for their fault.
Babur marched on Thursday, March 17th, and dismounted at Andarāba on the Sūhān.
Andarāba was a fort said to have depended from of old on ancestors of Malik Hast.
Hātī Kakar had killed Malik Hast's father and destroyed the fort.
Hātī Kakar sent his kinsman Parbat to Babur with tribute and an accoutred horse.
Parbat met the camp and came on in the company of the train.
Langar Khān came up from Bhīra on matters of business.
Hātī's kinsman Parbat was robed in an honorary dress and given letters of encouragement for Hātī.
Nīl-āb and the Qārlūq Hazāra had been given to Humāyūn, who was 12 years old.
Babur ordered that the camp-camels should be counted, and it came out at 570.
Babur had heard of the qualities of the saṃbhal plant and saw it on the ground.
The saṃbhal plant grows sparsely on the hill-skirt but abundantly further along the skirt-hills of Hindūstān.
Babur marched from camp at beat of drum one hour before day on March 20th.
Babur crossed the Sangdakī-pass and the torrent on March 20th.
Babur drank jul-āb on June 2nd.
Babur drank a working-draught on June 5th.
A wedding gift of 1000 shāhrukhī was given for Qāsim Beg's youngest son Ḥamza's marriage to Khalīfa's eldest daughter.
Babur was abstaining from wine at one point.
Babur decided to watch a wine party while remaining sober to observe the interaction between sober and drunk people.
Babur used to go about in a litter while he was ill.
Babur drank wine-mixture on several earlier days, then stopped because it did no good, then drank it again during convalescence.
Babur appointed Mullā Maḥmūd to read extracts from the Qorān in his presence on June 23rd.
Babur had difficulty getting the clans to march into Kābul from across the Hindū-kush.
Babur believed Kābul was a confined country not easily providing summer and winter quarters for Turkish and Mughūl flocks and herds.
Babur allowed the clans to cross over to the Qūndūz and Bāghlān side.
Babur sent his Dīwān to Pūlād Sulṯān with a verse expressing longing for someone.
The `Abdu'r-raḥman Afghāns on the Gīrdīz border were unsatisfactory in their tribute and behaviour.
The `Abdu'r-raḥman Afghāns were hurtful to caravans.
Babur rode out on July 27th to over-run the `Abdu'r-raḥman Afghāns.
Ḥusain Ḥasan foolishly charged alone at Afghāns and was killed by them.
Babur was angry at the begs who stood by while Ḥusain was overcome.
Babur ordered that the begs who failed to help Ḥusain should lose their rank and pargana.
Babur ordered that the begs' beards should be shaved off and they should be exhibited in towns as punishment.
Mūmin Ātāka speared an Afghān and brought in his head.
Abū'l-ḥasan the armourer went forward without mail and killed an Afghān.
Babur ordered a tower of Afghān heads to be set up.
All 40 or 50 Afghāns were killed by Babur's forces.
Babur had never been along the Rustam-maidān road before.
Babur found Rustam-plain to be amongst mountains and not very charming.
Babur observed that from the Rustam-maidān range, rain-clouds of Hindūstān could be seen beyond the Karmāsh mountains.
Babur perpetrated a ma'jūn at Muḥammad Āghā's village on July 30th.
Babur's forces used a drug thrown into water to catch fish.
Babur reached Kābul on July 31st.
Babur deprived Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Faẓlī and Khusrau's servants of place and rank for their shortcomings when Ḥusain was overcome.
Babur gave an honorary dress to Bābā Qashqa Mughūl at a wine-party.
Babur mounted for an excursion to the Koh-dāman, Bārān and Khwāja Sih-yārān on August 11th.
Babur ate a confection at Istālīf on August 12th.
Babur had a wine-party at Istālīf on August 13th.
A great snake was killed near Khwāja Sih-yārān that contained a smaller snake inside it.
The smaller snake contained a whole mouse inside it.
Babur had a wine-party at Khwāja Sih-yārān on August 14th.
Babur's forces caught many fish at the infall of the Parwān-water using a fish-drug.
Darwīsh-i-muḥammad had not yet committed the sin of wine and was in obedience.
Qūtlūq Khwāja had abandoned soldiering to become a darwīsh.
Qūtlūq Khwāja was very old with a completely white beard.
Qūtlūq Khwāja drank wine at parties despite being a darwīsh and old man.
Babur's custom was not to press wine on a non-drinker.
Babur had a wine-party after the Mid-day Prayer on August 17th.
A raft carrying Babur struck a hill and began to sink on August 18th.
Rauḥ-dam and Tīngrī-qulī were thrown into the water when the raft struck.
A China cup, spoon, and ṯambour went into the water.
Shāh Beg's Shāh Ḥasan fell backward on the raft and pulled Mīrzā Qulī Kūkūldāsh with him.
Mīrzā Qulī stuck his knife into the raft mat as he fell into the water.
Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān was thrown into the water during the raft incident.
Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān presented Babur with a seven-coloured cup to replace the one lost in the water.
Babur gathered tooth-picks with his own hands below Aīndīkī on August 19th.
Babur bestowed a special honorary dress and saddled horse on Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān on August 22nd.
Babur made Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān kneel as a retainer.
Babur had not had his head shaved for 4 or 5 months before August 24th.
Babur had his head shaved on August 24th.
Babur had a wine-party on August 24th.
Mīr Khūrd was made to kneel as Hind-āl's guardian on August 26th.
Mīr Khūrd made an offering of 1000 shāhrukhīs.
Tūlik Kūkūldāsh fought and beat Aūzbeg raiders in Badakhshān.
Babur broke his fast in Qāsim Beg's house on September 2nd.
Babur broke his fast in Khalīfa's house on September 3rd.
Babur was resolved and determined to check and ward off the Yūsuf-zāī on September 8th.
Babur became angry at his equerry Bābā Jān for leading forward a poor horse.
Tūlik Kūkūldāsh fought and defeated Aūzbeg raiders in Badakhshān.
Babur broke his fast in Qāsim Beg's house on the night of Saturday the 8th.
Babur broke his fast in Khalīfa's house on Sunday night.
Khwāja Muḥ. `Alī and Jān-i-nāṣir were summoned from their districts for the good of the army.
Kāmrān's maternal uncle Sl. `Alī Mīrzā had previously gone to Kāshghar.
Babur resolved to check and ward off the Yūsuf-zāī.
Babur struck his equerry Bābā Jān in the face in anger, dislocating his fist below the ring-finger.
Babur could not write for some time due to his injured hand.
Babur's maternal-aunt Daulat-sulṯān Khānīm sent letters and presents from Kāshghar.
Bū Khān and Mūsa, chiefs of the Dilazāk, brought tribute and did obeisance.
Babur bestowed a turban and a head-to-foot garment on Qūj Beg.
Honey was obtained from a tree on the Bārīk-āb ground.
Babur's original plan was to put down the Yūsuf-zāī in Sawād.
Dilazāk chiefs urged Babur to go to Hash-naghar instead of Sawād.
Babur decided to overrun the Afghāns in Hash-naghar and put its forts in order.
Shāh Mīr Ḥusain was given 15 days leave to prepare his equipment.
Tīngrī-bīrdī and Sl. Muḥammad Dūldāī overtook Babur's party.
Ḥamza arrived from Qūndūz.
The new moon of the Feast was seen at Qīrīq-arīq.
Wine was brought from Nūr-valley.
From his childhood, Babur had the rule not to press wine on a non-drinker.
Khwāja Muḥ. `Alī pressed Darwīsh-i-muḥammad to drink wine despite Babur's principle.
Babur told Darwīsh-i-muḥammad that a colocynth-apple was a melon of Hindūstān.
Darwīsh-i-muḥammad experienced a bitter taste from the colocynth-apple that lasted until night.
Langar Khān arrived to wait on Babur after being at his own place.
Langar Khān brought an offering of a horse and confections.
Sl. Bāyazīd reported that Afrīdī Afghāns were seated in Bāra with their goods and families.
Babur paid no attention to Sl. Bāyazīd's report about the Afrīdī Afghāns.
Babur wrote a couplet on the margin of a letter to Khwāja Kalān in Bajaur.
Babur crossed the Khaibar-pass and reached `Alī-masjid.
Babur's scouts reported that the Afghāns had heard of his approach and were in flight.
Much less corn was found than had been promised.
The plan of fitting-up Hash-naghar came to nothing.
The Dilazāk Afghāns were ashamed of the failed plan.
Babur decided to overrun the Afrīdī Afghāns and fit up Pūrshāwūr-fort.
Hindū Beg Qūchīn and the Mīr-zādas of Khwāst overtook Babur's party.
Babur ate ma'jūn with Darwesh-i-muḥammad Sārbān, Muḥammad Kūkūldāsh, Gadāī T̤aghāī and `Asas.
Babur invited Shāh Ḥasan to join the ma'jūn party.
Babur called Langar Khān Nīa-zāī to join the raft party.
Sl. `Alī Abū'l-hāshim reported that Sl. Sa`īd Khān had designs on Badakhshān.
Babur decided not to victual Pūrshāwūr fort and instead go to Badakhshān.
Langar Khān was appointed to help Muḥ. `Alī Jang-jang.
The Khiẓr-khail Afghāns used to shoot at laggards and those dismounted apart to get their horses.
Babur decided it was lawful and right to punish the Khiẓr-khail Afghāns.
Muḥ Ḥusain the armourer was ordered to keep all Khiẓr-khailīs prisoner in Kābul.
Babur ordered a detailed account of news from Badakhshān to be sent to him.
The Khiẓr-khail were understood to have their seat from Bahār and Mīch-grām to Karā-sū.
Most of the goods and small children of the Khiẓr-khailīs fell into the army's hands.
The Wazīrī Afghāns brought 300 sheep as tribute after the punitive raid.
Babur wrote nothing for some time after his hand was dislocated.
Babur wrote a little on Sunday the 14th of the month.
Babur gave pardon to the Khirilchī and Samū-khail Afghāns.
Babur fixed the tribute of the Khirilchī and Samū-khail at 4000 sheep.
Babur gave coats to the chiefs of the Khirilchī and Samū-khail.
The Bāgh-i-wafā's lawns were one sheet of trefoil.
The pomegranate-trees in the Bāgh-i-wafā were yellowed to autumn splendour.
The pomegranates in the Bāgh-i-wafā were excellent but not equal to the best ones of Wilāyat.
Babur had the best content from the Bāgh-i-wafā at this time.
The whole camp had pomegranates in abundance during Babur's stay.
Babur bestowed the fruit of two trees on Shāh Ḥasan.
Babur gave the fruit of one tree each to several begs.
Babur ordered that at least 20 trees growing round the reservoir be reserved for a winter visit to Lamghān.
Gadāī bihjat used disturbing words while drunk at a wine-party.
Gadāī T̤aghāī carried Gadāī bihjat out from the wine-party.
Babur made an excursion up the valley-bottom of the Bārīk-āb towards Qūrūq-sāī.
Babur ordered a sheep to be made into kabābs.
Babur and his party amused themselves by setting fire to branches of holm-oak.
Mullā `Abdu'l-malik dīwāna was sent ahead to take news of Babur's coming into Kābul.
Ḥasan Nabīra came from Mīrzā Khān's presence.
Sayyid Qāsim became so drunk that two of his servants mounted him and got him into camp with difficulty.
Muḥ. Bāqir's Dost became so drunk that people could not get him on his horse.
Amīn-i-muḥammad suggested cutting off Muḥ. Bāqir's Dost's head rather than leaving him to be taken.
After 100 efforts, Muḥ. Bāqir's Dost was mounted and brought to camp.
Babur reached Kābul at midnight.
Qulī Beg had been to Sl. Sa'īd Khān's presence in Kāshghar as Babur's envoy.
Bīshka Mīrzā Itārchī was added as envoy to Babur and brought gifts from Kāshghar.
Babur went by himself to Qābil's tomb and took his morning there.
Babur drank wine by the side of the reservoir in the Violet-garden.
Babur gave wine to Tīngrī-qulī Beg and Mahndī for the first time at a mid-day party.
Babur stayed the night at the Hot-bath.
Babur bestowed honorary dresses on Hindūstānī traders headed by Yaḥya Nūḥānī.
Babur bestowed a dress and gifts on Bīshka Mīrzā and gave him leave to go.
Babur held a party in the little Picture-hall over the Chār-bāgh gate with 16 persons present.
Babur went to Istālīf to see the harvest.
Babur and his party ate ma'jūn at Istālīf.
Much rain fell during Babur's excursion to Istālīf.
Most of the begs and household came into Babur's tent outside the Bāgh-i-kalān.
Babur took his morning and got drunk at dawn at Istālīf.
Babur rode from Istālīf at the Mid-day Prayer.
Babur ate a confection on the road to Bih-zādī.
The harvest-crops were very beautiful at Bih-zādī.
Babur drank wine while viewing the harvest under autumnal trees.
Khalīfa's Mullā Maḥmūd was summoned to join the wine-party.
`Abdu'l-lāh became very drunk and recited a line affecting Khalīfa.
Mullā Maḥmūd blamed `Abdu'l-lāh for repeating the line in jest.
`Abdu'l-lāh came to his senses and talked sweetly after being troubled in mind.
Babur's excursion to view the harvest ended near the Evening Prayer in the Chār-bāgh.
Babur ate a confection with a few special people in the Violet-garden.
Babur went on a boat with Humāyūn and Kāmrān.
Humāyūn made a very good shot at a duck.
Babur rode out of the Chār-bāgh at midnight.
Babur sent his night-watch and groom back.
Babur crossed Mullā Bābā's bridge and got out by the Dīūrīn-narrows.
Babur reached Tardī Beg Khāk-sār's kārez near sunrise.
Babur took 100 shāhrukhīs with him for the private party.
Babur told Tardī Beg to get wine and other things ready.
Tardī Beg went for wine towards Bih-zādī.
Babur sent his horse by Tardī Beg's slave to the valley-bottom.
Babur sat down on the slope behind the kārez.
Tardī Beg brought a pitcher of wine which Babur and he drank by turns.
Muḥammad-i-qāsim Barlās and Shāh-zāda followed Tardī Beg and joined the party.
Tardī Beg told Muḥammad-i-qāsim and Shāh-zāda that Hul-hul Anīga wished to drink wine with them.
Babur rode out of the Chār-bāgh at midnight on Saturday the 18th.
Babur took 100 shāhrukhīs with him on this ride.
Babur had a fancy for a private and unrestrained party.
Babur had never seen a woman drink wine.
Babur's hand had been hurt at some point before this excursion.
Babur shot a deer in the shoulder with an easy bow, the arrow going in to half up the feather.
Babur had expected Humāyūn to go with him to Lamghān.
Humāyūn inclined to stay behind and was given leave from Kūra-pass.
Babur wished and had the inclination to return to obedience in his 40th year.
Babur was drinking to excess because less than a year was left before his 40th year.
Babur had not occupied himself with composing music for some time.
Babur composed an air in four-time.
Babur had sent for Khwāja Kalān and made Bajaur over to Shāh Mīr Ḥusain's charge.
Khwāja Kalān was a friend of Babur's.
Babur taught Mullā `Alī Khān a talisman he knew.
Half of one of Babur's front teeth broke off, then the other half broke off while he was eating food.
Babur made Ḥamza Khān, Malik of `Alī-shang, over to the avengers-of-blood for his evil deeds in shedding innocent blood.
Babur's diary breaks off for five years and ten months after this point.
Babur went thrice to Qandahār during the unrecorded period.
Babur went thrice into India during the unrecorded period.
Babur went once to Badakhshān during the unrecorded period.
Babur went once to Balkh during the unrecorded period.
Babur punished refractory tribesmen at least twice during the unrecorded period.
Babur received embassies from Hindūstān during the unrecorded period.
Babur produced the Mubīn, a Turkī poem of 2000 lines.
Babur meant to fill the gap in his autobiography but never did so.
Babur died before he reached the unrecorded part of his book.
Babur gained the southern key of Kābul and Hindūstān during the unrecorded period.
Babur pressed the Arghūns out from his rear during the unrecorded period.
Babur became a power in the Panj-āb during the unrecorded period.
The Rājpūt Rānā of Chitor proffered Babur alliance against Ibrāhīm.
Babur's intervention was sought in the warrings of the Afghāns.
Babur's earlier following was purged of Mughūl rebellion.
Babur's following was augmented by various Mīrzās-in-exile.
Babur was a ruler amongst the Mughuls but not their supreme Khan.
Shah Shuja Beg Arghun held Kandahar, Shal, Mustang, Siwistan, and part of Sind at various times.
Shah Shuja Beg died in 930 AH (1524 AD).
Shah Shuja Beg was succeeded by his son Hasan.
Hasan read the khutba for Babur.
Khan Mirza Miranshahi held Badakhshan from Babur with headquarters in Qunduz.
Khan Mirza died in 927 AH (1520 AD).
Humayun succeeded Khan Mirza in his appointment at age 13.
Muhammad-i-zaman Bai-qara held Balkh perhaps directly from Babur or through Ismail.
Ala'u'd-din Alam Khan Ludi was the brother of Sultan Sikandar Ludi.
Ala'u'd-din Alam Khan Ludi desired to supersede his nephew Ibrahim.
Daulat Khan Yusuf-khail held Lahor for Ibrahim Ludi at the beginning of the period.
Babur made an expedition into Hindustan in 926 AH.
The march from Kabul was made through Bajaur where refractory tribesmen were brought to order.
Babur's expeditions crossed the Indus at the usual place until the last one of 932 AH (1525 AD).
Bhira was traversed where Babur had his own Commanders.
Sialkat was occupied without resistance.
Sayyidpur (possibly Sidhpur) was taken by assault and put to the sword.
Babur's planned operations beyond Sidhpur were frustrated by news of Shah Beg's incursion into his territory.
Shah Beg held Kandahar by uncertain tenure.
Shah Beg had been pushing his way into Sind by way of the Bolan-pass for several years.
Shah Beg's relations with Babur were ostensibly good.
Shah Beg sent envoys to Babur twice in the previous year.
Shah Beg's son Hasan had been in Babur's company for more than a year and was unreconciled with his father.
Shah Beg's relations with Shah Ismail were deferential and appeared vassal-like.
Shah Beg made no serious attempt on Kabul.
Kandahar was difficult to capture.
Babur had not taken Kandahar in 913 AH by siege or assault but by default after one day's fight in the open.
Kandahar had a treble line of bastioned walls and a high citadel in the centre.
The town of Kandahar was in three parts, each on a separate eminence and capable of mutual defence.
Babur laid regular siege to Kandahar with mining and battering of the walls in 926 AH.
Famine was desolating the country around Kandahar during the siege.
The garrison was reduced to great distress during the siege.
Pestilence broke out within the walls of Kandahar and spread to Babur's camp.
Babur returned to Kabul in the month of Tir (June) due to pestilence.
Shah Beg pushed on in Sind during the months of respite.
Mehtar Sambhal, Shah Beg's former slave and now commander, revictualled Kandahar.
Khwand-amir finished his history in 1524-29 AD.
Khwand-amir's account of Kandahar affairs was contemporary.
Khwand-amir was in close touch with several actors in the Kandahar affairs.
Amir Ghiyas'u'd-din, a patron of Khwand-amir, was put to death in 927 AH in Harat because of suspicion that he was an ally of Babur.
Khwand-amir's nephew Ghiyas'u'd-din was in Kandahar and was the bearer of its keys to Babur the next year.
Khwand-amir was with Babur himself a few years later in Hindustan.
Mir Massum wrote in 1600 AD, 70 to 75 years after Khwand-amir.
Mir Massum held office in Kandahar.
Mir Massum added an inscription commemorative of Akbar's dominion to Babur's own at Chihil-zina.
Babur was resolved in 926 AH to uproot Shah Shuja Beg from Kandahar.
Babur often rode his piebald horse close to the moat and urged his men to fiery onset during the siege.
Khan Mirza died in Badakhshan.
The news of Khan Mirza's death caused Babur great grief.
Babur appointed Humayun to succeed Khan Mirza.
The Governor of Harat at this time was Shah Ismail's son Tahmasp, between six and seven years old.
Amir Khan took chief part in diplomatic intervention with Babur on behalf of Harat.
Amir Khan was associated with Amir Ghiyas'u'd-din in diplomatic intervention.
The Persian officers were less anxious about Shah Beg than for their own position in Khurasan.
Shah Ismail's position was weakened by ill-success against the Sultan of Rum.
Amir Khan feared that a victorious Babur might move on Khurasan.
Babur's objective was Hindustan, and Kandahar was a help on the way.
Bai-qara exiles were with Babur and their ambition was to recover their former seat.
Shah Beg's requests for intervention began in 926 AH.
The remonstrance of the Persian officers with Babur began in 926 AH.
Shah Beg's couriers followed one another with entreaty that the Amirs would contrive for Babur to retire.
Shah Beg promised obeisance and yearly tribute.
Babur answered that Shah Beg's promise was a vain thing on which no reliance could be placed.
Babur said he would take Kandahar and send Shah Beg a prisoner to Harat.
Babur said he would be ready to give the keys of the town and possession of the Garm-sir to anyone appointed to receive them.
Amir Khan's attention was distracted by Ubaidu'l-lah Khan's attack on Harat in the spring of 1521 AD.
Negotiations appear to have been resumed later.
Khwand-amir claims that Babur left Kandahar in 927 AH as a result of negotiations.
Mir Massum says Babur went down to Kandahar before the year's tribute in grain had been collected.
Babur destroyed the standing crops around Kandahar.
Shah Beg proposed terms after being wearied under reiterated attack and preoccupied by operations in Sind.
Terms were made with stipulation for the town to be Shah Beg's during one year more and then to be given over to Babur.
The Arghun families were removed to Shal and Siwi.
Khwand-amir's dates agree with the fixed dates of the period and the course of events.
Several of Massum's dates are seriatim five lunar years earlier than Khwand-amir's.
Babur's first capture of Kandahar was in 913 AH (1507 AD).
Shah Beg had a success at Kahan in the beginning of 925 AH (1519 AD).
Shah Hasan spent the whole of 925 AH with Babur.
Shah Hasan is not mentioned as having left before the second month of 926 AH.
The keys of Kandahar were surrendered in 928 AH (1522 AD).
The date 928 AH is inscribed at Chihil-zina.
Babur and Mahim visited Humayun in his government, probably to Faizabad.
Babur and Mahim stayed with Humayun for a few days.
Babur's retirement in 927 AH was due to remonstrances from Harat.
Shah Beg went to Siwi leaving Abdul-baqi in charge of Kandahar.
Abdul-baqi sent to Babur and brought him down to Kandahar.
Abdul-baqi gave Babur the keys of the town by the hand of Khwand-amir's nephew Ghiyas'u'd-din.
Messengers had come and gone between Babur and Harat in 928 AH.
Two men employed by Amir Khan are mentioned as messengers between Babur and Harat.
A courier of Babur's brought a tributary gift to Harat announcing that the town was in Babur's hands.
Bābur sought to possess Qandahār.
Bābur's retirement in 927 AH was attributed to remonstrances from Harāt.
Shāh Beg was worn out by the siege of Qandahār.
Bābur received the keys of Qandahār in 928 AH.
Bābur ordered a monument of victory excavated at Chihil-zīna behind Qandahār.
Bābur approved of Shāh Beg sending him the keys of Qandahār.
Shāh Beg died on Sha`bān 22nd or 23rd.
Bābur wrote the Turkī poem _Mubīn_ in 928 AH.
The _Mubīn_ contains 2000 lines.
Bābur wrote the _Mubīn_ for the instruction of Kāmrān.
The _Mubīn_ is a versified and highly orthodox treatise on Muḥammadan Law.
Bābur had made a Collection of poetical compositions by 925 AH.
Bābur sent a copy of his Memoirs to Khwāja Kalān on March 7th 1520.
Daulat Khān _Lūdī_ and `Alāu'd-dīn `Ālam Khān _Lūdī_ appealed to Bābur for help against Ibrāhīm.
Daulat Khān had been summoned to Ibrāhīm's presence but was afraid to go.
Ibrāhīm killed 25 of Sikandar's amīrs without cause.
Dilāwar Khān went to Kābul with his father's message to Bābur.
Wedding festivities were in progress when Dilāwar Khān reached Kābul.
Bābur prayed in the garden for a sign of victory in Hindūstān.
Bābur asked for a sign of victory to be a gift of mango or betel.
Daulat Khān sent Bābur half-ripened mangoes preserved in honey.
Bābur accepted the mangoes as a sign and made preparation for a move on Hindūstān.
Gul-badan was born either in 929 AH or the next year.
Gul-badan was Dil-dār's third daughter.
Gul-badan later authored an _Humāyūn-nāma_.
Bābur's fourth expedition to Hindūstān had the declared purpose of direct attack on Ibrāhīm.
Bābur's fourth expedition involved cooperation with Afghān malcontents against Ibrāhīm _Lūdī_.
Bābur forded the Indus and took the sub-montane road through the Kakar country.
Bābur crossed the Jīhlam and Chīn-āb.
Bābur moved to within 10 miles of Lāhor.
Daulat Khān fled to a colony of Bilūchīs on the approach of Ibrāhīm's army.
Bābur defeated Bihār Khān _Lūdī_ in battle with great slaughter.
Bābur's troops plundered Lāhor and burned some of the _bāzārs_.
Bābur spent four days near Lāhor.
Bābur stormed, plundered and put to the sword Dībālpūr.
The capture of Dībālpūr occurred in mid-Rabī`u'l-awwal 930 AH.
Daulat Khān and his sons did not join Bābur until the latter was at Dībālpūr.
Bābur gave Daulat Khān Jalandhar and Sulṯānpūr instead of Lāhor.
Daulat Khān was angered by not being restored to charge of Lāhor.
Dilāwar told Bābur that his father's advice was treacherous.
Bābur arrested Daulat Khān and Apāq on suspicion of treacherous intent.
Bābur released Daulat Khān and Apāq and gave them Sulṯānpūr.
Daulat Khān and Apāq fled to the hills.
Bābur garrisoned the Panj-āb with his own followers.
Bābur returned to Kābul after the fourth expedition.
Bābur posted Mīr `Abdu'l-`azīz as Master of the Horse in Lāhor.
Bābur posted Bābā Qashqa _Mughūl_ in Dībālpūr with `Ālam Khān.
Bābur posted Khusrau Kūkūldāsh in Sīālkot.
Bābur posted Muḥammad `Alī _Tājik_ in Kalanūr.
Ismā`īl _Ṣafawī_ died on Rajab 19th at the age of 38.
Ismā`īl _Ṣafawī_ was broken by defeat from Sulṯān Salīm of Rūm.
Ismā`īl _Ṣafawī_ was succeeded by his son T̤ahmāsp, a child of ten.
Shāh Shujā` _Arghūn_ died on Sha`bān 22nd.
Shāh Beg was succeeded in Sind by his son Ḥasan.
Ḥasan read the _khuṯba_ for Bābur.
Ḥasan married Khalīfa's daughter Gul-barg.
Betrothal between Ḥasan and Gul-barg had been made during Ḥasan's visit to Bābur in Kābul.
Khalīfa's son Muḥibb-i-`alī married Nāhīd the daughter of Qāsim Kūkūldāsh and Māh-chūchūk _Arghūn_.
Bābur had plantains brought from Hindūstān for the Bāgh-i-wafā at Adīnapūr.
Daulat Khān came down from the hills with a small body of followers after Bābur's return to Kābul.
Daulat Khān seized his son Dilāwar.
Daulat Khān took Sulṯānpūr.
Daulat Khān defeated `Ālam Khān in Dībālpūr.
Daulat Khān detached 5000 men against Sīālkot.
Bābur's begs of Lāhor attacked and overcame Daulat Khān's 5000 men.
Ibrāhīm sent an army to reconquer the Panj-āb.
Daulat Khān won over part of Ibrāhīm's army to himself.
`Ālam Khān fled from his reverse at Dībālpūr straight to Kābul.
Bābur promised `Ālam Khān further help under the condition that `Ālam Khān take Ibrāhīm's place on the throne of Dihlī.
Bābur was to hold Lāhor and all to the west of it in full suzerainty.
Bābur furnished `Ālam Khān with a body of troops.
Bābur gave `Ālam Khān a royal letter to the Lāhor begs.
Bābur promised to follow `Ālam Khān swiftly.
The Aūzbeg Sulṯāns and Khāns laid siege to Balkh.
Bābur went to Balkh and raised the siege.
Bābur returned to Kābul in time to set out for his fifth expedition to Hindūstān.
Humāyūn had a considerable body of troops in Badakhshān.
Humāyūn's non-arrival delayed Bābur's progress in the fifth expedition.
Bābur set out for Hindūstān on Friday the 1st of the month of Ṣafar at the date 932.
Bābur crossed the small rise of Yak-langa.
Bābur dismounted in the meadow to the west of the water of Dih-i-ya`qūb.
`Abdu'l-malūk the armourer had gone seven or eight months earlier as Bābur's envoy to Sulṯān Sa`īd Khān.
Yāngī Beg Kūkūldāsh conveyed letters and small presents from the Khānīms and the Khān.
Bābur stayed two days in camp for the convenience of the army.
Bābur ate a confection at Bādām-chashma.
Khwāja Ḥusain sent gold _ashrafīs_ and _tankas_ to the value of 20,000 _shāhrukhīs_ from the Lāhor revenues.
Babur set out for Hindūstān on Friday the 1st of Ṣafar in the year 932 AH.
Babur crossed the small rise of Yak-langa during his journey.
Babur dismounted in the meadow to the west of the water of Dih-i-ya`qūb.
`Abdu'l-malūk the armourer had gone seven or eight months earlier as Babur's envoy to Sulṯān Sa`īd Khān.
Babur received letters and small presents from the Khānīms and the Khān through Yāngī Beg Kūkūldāsh.
Babur's army stayed two days in camp for convenience.
Babur ate a confection at Bādām-chashma.
Gold ashrafīs and tankas valued at 20,000 shāhrukhīs were sent from Lāhor revenues by Khwāja Ḥusain.
Babur had a violent discharge at Gandamak which passed off easily by God's mercy.
Babur delayed at Bāgh-i-wafā waiting for Humāyūn and the army.
Babur described Bāgh-i-wafā as most beautifully placed.
Most people drank on drinking-days and took their morning at Bāgh-i-wafā.
On non-drinking days there were parties for ma`jūn at Bāgh-i-wafā.
Babur wrote harsh letters to Humāyūn lecturing him severely because of his long delay.
Babur spoke very severely to Humāyūn when he arrived.
Khwāja Kalān arrived from Ghaznī.
Babur and his party drank while traveling on a raft to Qūsh-guṃbaz.
Babur ate confection while on a raft.
Babur's encamping-ground was supposed to be Qīrīq-ārīq but no sign of the camp could be seen.
Babur thought the army might have dismounted at Garm-chashma.
Babur had a raft tied up and slept while traveling.
Babur landed at Yada-bīr at day-break.
Several verse-makers were on the raft with Babur including Shaikh Abū'l-wajd, Shaikh Zain, Mullā `Alī-jān, and Tardī Beg Khāksār.
Babur asked the verse-makers to compose verses on certain lines.
Babur made a couplet at the expense of Mullā `Alī-jān.
Babur used to compose verses of good or bad, grave or jest quality before writing the Mubīn.
While composing the Mubīn, Babur realized he should not waste his tongue on low words.
Babur refrained from satirical and jesting verse after composing the Mubīn.
Babur was repentant of his earlier jesting verses.
Babur made a jesting couplet about Mullā `Alī-jān while forgetting his repentance.
Babur had fever and discharge in Bīgrām followed by cough and spitting blood.
Babur knew his illness was a reproof for violating his oath about not composing jesting verses.
Babur broke his pen as a sign of repentance.
Babur gave his mind rest from empty thinking and unlawful occupation.
Babur always dismounted on a rise overlooking the camp at `Alī-masjid.
The camp-fires at `Alī-masjid made a wonderful illumination at night.
There had always been drinking at `Alī-masjid.
Babur rode out before dawn near Bīgrām.
Babur preferred a confection and kept a fast on one day.
Babur formed a hunting-circle looking down-stream from the Siyāh-āb in front of Bīgrām.
A rhino was found in jungle near Bīgrām.
Babur and his party brought the rhino out and pursued it for two miles.
Many arrows were shot at the rhino.
The rhino was brought down without making a good set at man or horse.
Two other rhinos were killed.
Babur had wondered how a rhino and an elephant would behave if brought face to face.
A rhino came out in front of some elephants but did not face them when the mahauts drove them towards it.
Several begs and household members were appointed to take charge of boats at the Nīl-āb crossing.
Babur ordered a list to be made of all who were with the army, name by name.
Babur had fever and discharge in Bīgrām which led to cough and spitting blood.
Babur's anxiety about his illness passed off in two or three days by God's mercy.
It rained when Babur left Bīgrām.
Daulat Khān and Ghāzī Khān collected an army of 20 to 30,000 men.
Daulat Khān and Ghāzī Khān took Kilānūr and intended to move on Lāhor.
Babur sent Mumin-i-`alī the commissary to tell the Lāhor begs not to fight until he arrived.
Babur reached the water of Sind with two night-halts on the way.
Babur crossed the Sind-water on Saturday the 1st of the first Rabī`.
The number of those who came with Babur's army was reported as 12,000.
Rainfall had been scant in the plains but good in cultivated lands along the hill-skirts.
Babur took the road for Sīālkot along the skirt-hills.
Babur came upon a torrent with pools that were frozen over opposite Hātī Kakar's country.
The ice was about as thick as the hand.
Such ice was unusual in Hindūstān.
No sign or trace of ice was seen in the years Babur was in the country.
Babur made five marches from the Sind-water before dismounting at the camping-ground of the Bugīāls.
Babur stayed a day in camp to let people get provisions.
`Araq was drunk on that day.
Mullā Muḥ. Pargharī told many stories and was very talkative.
Mullā Shams was very riotous and did not finish until night.
Slaves and servants scattered over jungle and plain while seeking provisions.
Kīchkīna tūnqiṯār died while seeking provisions.
Babur crossed the Bihat-water at a ford below Jīlam.
Walī Qīzīl came to see Babur.
Walī Qīzīl was the Sīālkot reserve and held the parganas of Bīmrūkī and Akrīāda.
Babur took a position of censure and reproach toward Walī Qīzīl regarding Sīālkot.
Walī Qīzīl excused himself saying Khusrau Kūkūldāsh did not send him word.
Babur asked Walī Qīzīl why he did not join the begs in Lāhor.
Babur did not trouble about Walī Qīzīl's fault because work was at hand.
Sayyid T̤ūfān and Sayyid Lāchīn were sent to Lāhor with orders not to join battle.
Babur ordered the Lāhor begs to meet him at Sīālkot or Parsrūr.
It was said that Ghāzī Khān had collected 30 to 40,000 men.
Daulat Khān, old as he was, had girt two swords to his waist.
Babur believed ten friends are better than nine.
Babur moved forward after sending the two men to the begs.
Babur dismounted on the bank of the Chīn-āb.
Buhlūlpūr was khalṣa.
Babur left the road to visit Buhlūlpūr.
Buhlūlpūr's fort is situated above a deep ravine on the bank of the Chīn-āb.
Buhlūlpūr pleased Babur much.
Babur thought of bringing Sīālkot to Buhlūlpūr.
Babur went to camp by boat from Buhlūlpūr.
Babur dismounted at Sīālkot on Friday the 14th of the first Rabī`.
Jats and Gujūrs pour down in countless hordes from hill and plain for loot in bullock and buffalo.
Babur considered Jats and Gujūrs ill-omened and senseless oppressors.
Jats and Gujūrs began their senseless work after Babur took the country.
Jats and Gujūrs fell in tumult on poor and needy folks coming out of Sīālkot town.
Babur ordered two or three Jat thieves cut to pieces.
Nūr Beg's brother Shāham was sent to the begs in Lāhor from Sīālkot.
A trader reported that `Ālam Khān had let Sulṯān Ibrāhīm defeat him.
`Ālam Khān took leave of Babur in Kābul in 931 AH.
`Ālam Khān went off by double marches regardless of those with him.
Babur rode for Balkh after giving `Ālam Khān leave to go.
`Ālam Khān insisted the Lāhor begs reinforce him and move on Dihlī and Āgra.
The Lāhor begs refused `Ālam Khān because he had fought and lost to Ghāzī Khān.
`Ālam Khān sent his son Sher Khān to speak with Daulat Khān and Ghāzī Khān.
`Ālam Khān took Dilāwar Khān and Maḥmūd Khān with him.
Daulat Khān with Ghāzī Khān was to take all the begs posted in Hindūstān.
`Ālam Khān was to take Dilāwar Khān and Ḥājī Khān and capture Dihlī and Āgra.
Ismā`īl Jilwānī and other amīrs came and saw `Ālam Khān.
Sulaimān Shaikh-zāda came near Indrī.
`Ālam Khān's total force touched 30 to 40,000 men.
`Ālam Khān's forces laid siege to Dihlī but could neither take it by assault nor hurt the garrison.
Sulṯān Ibrāhīm got an army to horse against `Ālam Khān's forces when he heard of their assembly.
`Ālam Khān's forces planned to attack at night so each man would obey his own orders.
`Ālam Khān's forces started twice from a distance of 12 miles but neither advanced nor retired.
`Ālam Khān's forces delivered an attack when one watch of night remained.
`Ālam Khān's forces set fire from every end and made a disturbance.
Jalāl Khān Jig-hat came with other amīrs and saw `Ālam Khān.
Sulṯān Ibrāhīm did not bestir himself until shoot of dawn from his own enclosure.
`Ālam Khān's people were busy with plunder and booty.
Sulṯān Ibrāhīm's people moved out against `Ālam Khān's party with one elephant.
`Ālam Khān's party could not make stand against the elephant and ran away.
`Ālam Khān crossed over into the Mīān-dū-āb and crossed back again near Pānīpat.
`Ālam Khān got 4 laks from Mīān Sulaimān in Indrī on some pretext.
`Ālam Khān was deserted by Ismā`īl Jilwānī, Biban, and his own oldest son Jalāl.
`Ālam Khān was deserted by Saif Khān, Maḥmūd Khān, and Shaikh Jamāl Farmulī before the fighting.
Babur called Daulat Khan 'Father' and showed him more honor and respect than he could have asked.
Babur saved Daulat Khan and his sons from a door-to-door life amongst the Baluchis.
Babur freed Daulat Khan's family and harem from Ibrahim's prison-house.
Babur gave Daulat Khan three krors on Tatar Khan's lands.
Babur ordered that Daulat Khan's two swords be hung from his neck as punishment for his rebellion.
Babur had Daulat Khan forced to kneel when he hesitated to do so.
Babur used an interpreter to speak to Daulat Khan in his own language.
Babur spent a night in a tent pitched on a rise in front of the Gate of Milwat.
Babur shot arrows as chastisement when there was disturbance at the Gate of Milwat.
Babur inspected Ghazi Khan's book-room at Milwat.
Babur gave some precious books from Ghazi Khan's collection to Humayun.
Babur sent some precious books from Ghazi Khan's collection to Kamran in Qandahar.
Babur believed Ghazi Khan was a man devoid of nice sense of honour for abandoning his family.
Babur quoted from the Gulistan to criticize Ghazi Khan's escape.
Babur bestowed Dibalpur on Baqi shaghawul and sent him to help Balkh.
Babur sent gifts taken from Milwat to his younger children and train in Kabul.
Babur received letters from Araish Khan and Mulla Muhammad Mazhab conveying good wishes for his campaign.
Babur sent royal letters in response expressing his favor to Araish Khan and Mulla Muhammad Mazhab.
Babur sent begs to give Alam Khan honorable meeting when he arrived ruined and stripped bare.
Babur sent horses to Alam Khan when he arrived.
Babur replied to letters from Ismail Jilwani and Biban with royal letters.
Babur observed that many starved and naked Hindustanis died from cold and rain near Rupar.
Babur sent Sawadi night-guards as envoys to Ibrahim's camp.
Babur ordered a Char-bagh to be made at the mouth of a broad valley near the Kakar torrent.
Babur appointed Humayun to act against Hamid Khan.
Babur joined the whole of the right wing to Humayun's command.
Babur observed that Afghans remain very rustic and tactless.
Babur gave Humayun a special head-to-foot and special horse from the royal stable after his victory.
Babur promised guerdon to Humayun for his victory.
Babur ordered Ustad Ali-quli and the matchlockmen to shoot all prisoners as an example.
Babur considered Humayun's victory against Hamid Khan an excellent omen.
Babur was advancing against Sultan Ibrahim, son of Sultan Sikandar, son of Buhlul Ludi Afghan.
Sultan Ibrahim possessed the throne of Delhi and the dominions of Hindustan.
Sultan Ibrahim's standing-army was called a lak (100,000).
Sultan Ibrahim had about 1,000 elephants including begs' elephants.
Humāyūn won a military victory against enemy forces.
Humāyūn's troops killed between 100 and 200 enemy soldiers and captured 7 or 8 elephants.
Babur rewarded Humāyūn with special clothing, a horse from the royal stable, and a promise of additional rewards.
Humāyūn brought 100 prisoners and 7 or 8 elephants to Babur after his victory.
Babur ordered all prisoners to be executed by matchlockmen as an example.
This was Humāyūn's first battle experience.
Babur considered Humāyūn's first battle an excellent omen.
Babur gave Humāyūn the fortress of Ḥiṣār-fīrūza with all its dependencies and a large sum of money as reward.
Babur ate ma`jūn on a particular day.
Babur had a platform fixed in a boat for excursions on the river.
Babur sometimes made marches down the river bank.
Babur gave Sarsāwa to Tardī Beg simply because he praised it.
Babur believed that what God has fixed in eternity cannot be changed.
Babur believed that anxiety and fear were not reproachable in soldiers far from home.
Babur estimated the opposing army at 100,000 men with about 1000 elephants.
Babur believed Ibrāhīm was ruled by avarice and an insatiable craving to accumulate wealth.
Babur considered Ibrāhīm an unproved brave who provided nothing for military operations.
Babur compared Ibrāhīm unfavorably to the Aūzbeg khāns and sulṯāns in military skill.
Babur had previously fortified Ḥiṣār by closing the lanes when Aūzbeg khāns attacked.
Babur left Samarkand in 918 AH (1512 AD).
Babur sent 4 or 5000 men to deliver a night-attack on Ibrāhīm's camp.
Babur sent Humāyūn to meet the returning night-attack force and followed with the rest of the army.
A false alarm occurred in Babur's camp lasting about 20 minutes.
Babur put on mail and armed himself when the enemy advanced in fighting array.
Babur organized his forces into right, left, and centre divisions with turning-parties for flanking maneuvers.
Babur used carts and mantelets with matchlockmen as defensive fortifications.
The Battle of Pānī-pat began when the sun was spear-high and lasted until noon.
Babur's forces killed approximately 5,000 to 6,000 men near Ibrāhīm's position.
Babur's estimate was 15,000 to 16,000 total enemy dead, though later reports suggested 40,000 to 50,000.
Ibrāhīm's body was found in a heap of dead and his head was brought to Babur.
Babur appointed Humāyūn to ride to Āgra to secure the city and guard the treasure.
Babur appointed Mahdī Khwāja to secure Dihlī and watch the treasuries.
Babur made an excursion into the fort of Dihlī and spent the night there.
Babur visited the tombs of Khwāja Quṯbu'd-dīn, Ghiyāṣu'd-dīn Balban, and Alāu'd-dīn Khiljī.
Babur visited the Minār and various reservoirs and gardens in Dihlī.
Babur drank `araq on a boat at his camp.
Babur appointed Red Walī as Military Collectorate of Dihlī.
Babur made Dost Dīwān administrator of the Dihlī district.
Babur had the treasuries sealed and placed under the charge of appointed officials.
Babur had the khuṯba read in his name in Dihlī.
Babur distributed money to the poor and needy in Dihlī.
Babur made an excursion to Tūghlūqābād during his march to Āgra.
Humāyūn noticed a lack of discipline in the Āgra garrison.
Humāyūn posted men to watch the roads out of Āgra.
Water is not a necessity in cultivating crops and orchards in Hindustan.
Autumn crops in Hindustan grow by the downpour of rains themselves.
Spring crops in Hindustan grow even when no rain falls.
Young trees in Hindustan are given water constantly during two or three years, after which they need no more.
In Lahore, Dibalpur and those parts, people water by means of a wheel.
In Agra, Chandwar, Biana and those parts, people water with a bucket.
The bucket method of watering is laborious and filthy.
The towns and country of Hindustan are greatly wanting in charm.
The towns and lands of Hindustan are all of one sort.
Most places in Hindustan are on the dead level plain.
Under the monsoon-rains the banks of some rivers and torrents in Hindustan are worn into deep channels.
In many parts of the plains of Hindustan thorny jungle grows.
Except for the rivers and here and there standing-waters, there is little running-water in Hindustan.
Towns and countries in Hindustan subsist on the water of wells or on such as collects in tanks during the rains.
Hamlets and villages in Hindustan are depopulated and set up in a moment.
If the people of a large town in Hindustan flee from it, they do it in such a way that not a sign or trace of them remains in a day or a day and a half.
The population of Hindustan is unlimited.
The elephant is one of the wild animals peculiar to Hindustan.
The elephant inhabits the borders of the Kalpi country.
Elephants become more numerous in their wild state the further east one goes.
Captured elephants are brought from the Kalpi tract.
In Karrah and Manikpur elephant-catching is the work of 30 or 40 villages.
The elephant is an immense animal and very sagacious.
If people speak to an elephant, it understands.
If people command anything from an elephant, it does it.
The value of an elephant is according to its size.
Elephants are sold by measure.
In Hindustan elephants are not seen above 4 or 5 qari high.
An elephant eats and drinks entirely with its trunk.
If an elephant loses its trunk, it cannot live.
An elephant has two great teeth (tusks) in its upper jaw, one on each side of its trunk.
An elephant uses its tusks to bring down walls and trees.
An elephant uses its tusks to fight and do hard tasks.
Elephant tusks are called ivory and are highly valued by Hindus.
The elephant has no hair.
The elephant is much relied on by Hindus, accompanying every troop of their armies.
An elephant crosses great rivers with ease, carrying a mass of baggage.
Three or four elephants have dragged without trouble the cart of the mortar that it takes four or five hundred men to haul.
One elephant eats the corn of two strings of camels.
The rhinoceros is a large animal, equal in bulk to perhaps three buffaloes.
The rhinoceros has a single horn on its nose, more than nine inches long.
The rhinoceros' hide is very thick.
An arrow shot from a stiff bow, drawn with full strength right up to the arm-pit, if it pierce a rhinoceros at all, might penetrate 4 inches.
The rhinoceros resembles the horse more than it does any other animal.
The rhinoceros is more ferocious than the elephant and cannot be made obedient and submissive.
There are masses of rhinoceros in the Parashawar and Hashnagar jungles.
There are masses of rhinoceros between the Sind-river and the jungles of the Bhira country.
There are masses of rhinoceros on the banks of the Saru-river in Hindustan.
The rhinoceros strikes powerfully with its horn.
The wild-buffalo is much larger than the domestic buffalo.
The wild-buffalo's horns do not turn back in the same way as domestic buffalo horns.
The wild-buffalo is a mightily destructive and ferocious animal.
The nila-gau (blue-bull) may stand as high as a horse but is somewhat lighter in build.
The male nila-gau is bluish-gray.
The nila-gau has two rather small horns.
The nila-gau has a tuft of hair on its throat, nine inches long.
The nila-gau's hoof is cleft like the hoof of cattle.
The female nila-gau is of the colour of the bughu-maral.
The female nila-gau has no horns and is plumper than the male.
The hog-deer may be of the size of the white deer.
The hog-deer has short legs.
The hog-deer's horns are like a bughu's but smaller.
The hog-deer casts its horns every year like the bughu.
The hog-deer is rather a poor runner and does not leave the jungle.
A certain deer has a black back and white belly.
This deer's horns are longer than the huna's, but more crooked.
Hindus call this deer kalahara.
The female of this deer is light-coloured.
Hindus use the kalahara deer to catch wild deer by fastening a noose on its horns.
The kalahara deer are singularly combative.
Wild deer's horns become entangled in the net that is fast to the tame kalahara deer's horns.
After capture, Hindus tame wild deer and use them in their turn to take others.
Hindus set tamed deer to fight at home.
Deer fight very well.
