There is a smaller deer on the Hindustan hill-skirts, as large maybe as the one year's lamb of the arqarghalcha.
The gini-cow is a very small animal, perhaps as large as the quchqar (ram) of those countries.
The gini-cow's flesh is very tender and savoury.
The monkey is called bandar by Hindus.
There are many kinds of monkeys.
One kind of monkey is taken to those countries (Tramontana).
Jugglers teach monkeys tricks.
One kind of monkey is in the mountains of Nur-dara, in the skirt-hills of Safid-koh neighbouring on Khaibar, and from there downwards all through Hindustan.
This kind of monkey is not found higher up.
This monkey's hair is yellow, its face white, its tail not very long.
Another kind of monkey is not found in Bajaur, Sawad and those parts.
This monkey is much larger than the one taken to those countries (Tramontana).
This monkey's tail is very long, its hair whitish, its face quite black.
This monkey is in the mountains and jungles of Hindustan.
Yet another kind of monkey is quite black in hair, face and limbs.
The nawal (nul) may be somewhat smaller than the kish.
The nawal climbs trees.
Some call the nawal the mush-i-khurma (palm-rat).
The nawal is thought lucky.
A mouse called galahri (squirrel) is just always in trees, running up and down with amazing alertness and speed.
The peacock is a beautifully coloured and splendid animal.
The peacock's form is not equal to its colouring and beauty.
The peacock's body may be as large as the crane's but it is not so tall.
On the head of both cock and hen peacock are 20 to 30 feathers rising some 2 or 3 inches high.
The hen peacock has neither colour nor beauty.
The head of the cock peacock has an iridescent collar.
The cock peacock's neck is of a beautiful blue.
Below the neck, the cock peacock's back is painted in yellow, parrot-green, blue and violet colours.
The flowers on the cock peacock's back are much the smaller.
Below the back as far as the tail-tips of the cock peacock are larger flowers painted in the same colours.
The tail of some peacocks grows to the length of a man's extended arms.
The peacock has a small tail under its flowered feathers, like the tail of other birds.
The ordinary tail and primaries of the peacock are red.
The peacock is in Bajaur and Sawad and below them.
The peacock is not in Kunur and the Lamghanat or any place above them.
The peacock's flight is feebler than the pheasant's.
The peacock cannot do more than make one or two short flights.
The peacock frequents the hills or jungles.
Jackals abound in the jungles the peacock frequents.
Hindus call the peacock mor.
The peacock's flesh is lawful food, according to the doctrine of Imam Abu Hanifa.
The peacock's flesh is like that of the partridge and not unsavoury.
The peacock's flesh is eaten with instinctive aversion, in the way camel-flesh is.
The parrot is in Bajaur and countries lower down.
The parrot comes into Ningnahar and the Lamghanat in the heats when mulberries ripen.
The parrot is not in Ningnahar and the Lamghanat at other times.
The parrot is of many, many kinds.
One sort of parrot is that which people carry into those (Tramontane) countries.
People make parrots speak words.
Another sort of parrot is smaller.
This smaller parrot is also made to speak words.
The smaller parrot is called the jungle-parrot.
The jungle-parrot is numerous in Bajaur, Sawad and that neighbourhood.
5 or 6000 jungle-parrots fly in one flock.
Between the jungle-parrot and the larger parrot the difference is in bulk.
In colouring the jungle-parrot and the larger parrot are just one and the same.
Another sort of parrot is still smaller than the jungle-parrot.
This parrot's head is quite red.
The top of this parrot's wings is red also.
The tip of this parrot's tail for two hands'-thickness is lustrous.
The head of some parrots of this kind is iridescent.
This parrot does not become a talker.
People call this parrot the Kashmir parrot.
Another sort of parrot is rather smaller than the jungle-parrot.
This parrot's beak is black.
Round this parrot's neck is a wide black collar.
This parrot's primaries are red.
This parrot is an excellent learner of words.
Babur used to think that whatever a parrot or a sharak (mina) might say of words people had taught it, it could not speak of any matter out of its own head.
One of Babur's immediate servants Abul-qasim Jalai reported a singular thing to him about a parrot.
A parrot said, 'Uncover my face; I am stifling.'
A parrot said, 'Men are going past, are you not going on?'
Babur believes that so long as a person has not heard with his own ears, he may not believe.
Another kind of parrot is of a beautiful full red.
This parrot has other colours also.
This parrot is a very beautiful bird, both in colour and form.
People are understood to make this parrot also speak words.
This parrot's defect is a most unpleasant, sharp voice, like the drawing of broken china on a copper plate.
The sharak is numerous in the Lamghanat and abounds lower down, all over Hindustan.
Like the parrot, the sharak is of many kinds.
The kind of sharak that is numerous in the Lamghanat has a black head.
This sharak's primaries are spotted.
This sharak's body is rather larger and thicker than that of the chughu-chuk.
People teach the sharak to speak words.
Another kind of sharak is called pendawali.
The pendawali is brought from Bengal.
The pendawali is black all over and of much greater bulk than the sharak (house-mina).
The pendawali's bill and foot are yellow.
The pendawali has yellow wattles on each ear which hang down and have a bad appearance.
The pendawali learns to speak well and clearly.
Another kind of sharak is slenderer than the pendawali and is red round the eyes.
This sharak does not learn to speak.
People call this sharak the wood-sharak.

At the time when Babur had made a bridge over Gang (Ganges), crossed it, and put his adversaries to flight, a kind of sharak was seen, in the neighbourhood of Laknau and Aud (Oude), for the first time.
This sharak had a white breast, piebald head, and black back.
This kind of sharak does not learn to speak.
The luja is another bird.
The luja is called (Ar.) bu-qalamun (chameleon) because, between head and tail, it has five or six changing colours.
The luja's colours are resplendent like a pigeon's throat.
The luja is about as large as the kabg-i-dari.
The luja seems to be the kabg-i-dari of Hindustan.
As the kabg-i-dari moves on the heads of mountains, so does the luja.
The luja is in the Nijr-au mountains of the countries of Kabul, and in the mountains lower down.
The luja is not found higher up.
When the luja birds, at the onset of winter, descend to the hill-skirts, if they come over a vineyard, they can fly no further and are taken.
The flesh of the luja bird is very savoury.
The partridge is not peculiar to Hindustan but is also in the Garm-sir countries.
Some kinds of partridge are only in Hindustan.
The durrāj (Francolinus vulgaris) may be of the same bulk as the kiklīk.
The cock partridge's back is the colour of the hen-pheasant.
The cock partridge's throat and breast are black, with quite white spots.
A red line comes down on both sides of both eyes of the partridge.
The partridge is named from its cry which is something like 'Shir dāram shakrak'.
The partridge pronounces 'shir' short.
The partridge says 'dāram shakrak' distinctly.
Astarabad partridges are said to cry 'Bāt mīnī tūtīlār' (Quick! they have caught me).
The partridge of Arabia and those parts is understood to cry, 'Bi'l shakar tadawm al ni`am' (with sugar pleasure endures).
The hen-bird partridge has the colour of the young pheasant.
Partridges are found below Nijr-au.
Another kind of partridge is called kanjāl.
Babur observed and documented various bird species found in Hindustan.
Babur believed that partridges derive their names from their distinctive cries.
Babur noted that the flesh of certain birds like bustards is delicious and excellent.
Babur observed that some birds become very tame when kept in captivity.
Babur witnessed a water bird swallow a six-nailed shoe and a whole fowl with feathers intact.
Babur saw a gharial (crocodile-like creature) from a distance on the Saryu river.
Babur was present when a gharial took one or two slave-women and three or four camp-followers between Ghazipur and Banaras.
Babur observed that Hindustan fish are very savory and have no unpleasant odor.
Babur noted that fish in Hindustan rivers can leap a yard or more above water.
Babur observed that frogs in Hindustan can run 6 or 7 yards on the surface of water.
Babur considered the mango to be the best fruit of Hindustan.
Babur believed that many mangoes praised as superior to all fruits except melons are overrated.
Babur noted that mangoes are usually plucked unripe and ripened in the house.
Babur observed that mango trees grow very large.
Babur noted that mango trees in Bengal and Gujarat are said to grow handsome.
Babur documented the plantain tree's structure and flowering process in detail.
Babur observed that the Bengal plantain variety is said to be very sweet.
Babur noted that the mahwa tree is very large and provides most of the wood in Hindustan houses.
Babur observed that mahwa flowers can be dried and eaten like raisins.
Babur noted that oil is extracted from mahwa seeds.
Babur documented the characteristics and flavors of various Hindustan fruits.
Babur observed that jack-fruit is sickeningly sweet and very adhesive.
Babur noted that jack-fruit grows not only on branches but on trunk and root.
Babur saw a lote-fruit variety in Bandir (Gwalior) that was really good.
Babur documented the seasonal cycles of the lote-tree's leaf-shedding and fruiting.
Babur observed that the karaunda grows in bushes on plains rather than mountains.
Babur noted that the amlā fruit is astringent and ill-flavored but makes good confiture.
Babur had chirunji trees growing in his gardens.
Babur valued detailed observation and documentation of natural phenomena.
Chīka grows on mountains.
Karaūndā grows on plains.
Pānīyālā is larger than the plum.
Gūlar fruit grows out of the tree-trunk.
Āmlā is a five-sided fruit.
Āmlā looks like an unblown cotton-pod.
Chirūnjī tree was understood to grow in the hills.
Babur knew later about chirūnjī because there were three or four clumps of it in his gardens.
Date-palm grows in Lamghān.
Date-palm branches grow from just one place at its top.
Date-palm trunk is rough and ill-coloured.
Date-palm fruit is like a bunch of grapes but much larger.
If a date-palm's head is cut off, it dries off.
Date-palm gives no good fruit unless a branch of the male-tree is brought into touch with the female-tree.
The head of the date-palm is called its cheese.
Date-palm cheese is tolerable eating, much like the walnut.
Date liquor is rather pleasant if drunk at once.
Date liquor is quite exhilarating if drunk after two or three days.
Babur once visited Bārī and made an excursion to villages on the bank of the Chaṃbal-river.
Babur met people collecting date-liquor in the valley-bottom.
A good deal of date-liquor was drunk but no hilarity was felt.
Much date-liquor must be drunk to produce a little cheer.
Coco-nut palm has general resemblance to the date-palm.
Coco-palm has more and more glistening leaves than date-palm.
Coco-nut has a green outer husk like the walnut.
Coco-nut husk is made of fibre on fibre.
Ropes for ships and boats are made from coco-nut husks.
Cord for sewing boat-seams is made from coco-nut husks.
Coco-nut fluid tastes like date-palm cheese in solution.
Tāṛ palm branches are quite at its top.
People hang a pot on tāṛ palm and take its juice.
Tāṛī liquor is said to be more exhilarating than date liquor.
Tāṛ palm has no leaves for about a yard along its branches.
Tāṛ palm has 30 or 40 leaves at the tip of the branch opening like the spread palm of the hand.
Tāṛ palm leaves approach a yard in length.
People write Hindī characters on tāṛ palm leaves after the fashion of account rolls.
Oranges grow well in the Lamghānāt, Bajaur and Sawād.
Lamghānāt orange is smallish and has a navel.
Lamghānāt orange is very agreeable, fragile and juicy.
Lamghānāt orange is not like the orange of Khurāsān.
Lamghānāt oranges are so fragile that many spoil before reaching Kābul from the Lamghānāt.
The distance from Lamghānāt to Kābul is 13-14 yīghāch (65-70 miles).
Astarābād orange has thick skin and scant juice.
Astarābād orange carries with less damage from there to Samarkand.
The distance from Astarābād to Samarkand is some 270-280 yīghāch.
Bajaur orange is about as large as a quince.
Bajaur orange is very juicy and more acid than other oranges.
Khwāja Kalān said that oranges gathered from a single tree in Bajaur mounted up to 7,000.
Babur always thought the word nāranj was an Arabic form.
Everyone in Bajaur and Sawād says nārang.
Lime is very plentiful and about the size of a hen's egg.
If a person poisoned drink the water in which lime fibres have been boiled, danger is averted.
Citron is called bālang by Bajaurīs and Sawādīs.
Bajaurīs and Sawādīs give the name bālang-marabbā to citron marmalade.
In Hindūstān people call the turunj bajaurī.
There are two kinds of turunj.
One kind of turunj is sweet, flavourless and nauseating.
The other kind of turunj is acid and makes excellent sherbet.
Turunj of Hindūstān and Bajaur is acid and deliciously acid.
Turunj size may be that of the Khusrawī melon.
Turunj has a thick skin, wrinkled and uneven.
Turunj tree has no trunk and is rather low.
Turunj tree grows in bushes.
Turunj tree has a larger leaf than the orange.
Sangtāra is like the citron in colour and form.
Sangtāra has both ends of its skin level.
Sangtāra is not rough and is somewhat the smaller fruit.
Sangtāra tree is large, as large as the apricot.
Sangtāra tree has a leaf like the orange's.
Sangtāra is a deliciously acid fruit.
Sangtāra makes a very pleasant and wholesome sherbet.
Sangtāra is a powerful stomachic, but not weakening like the orange.
Gal-gal lime has the shape of a goose's egg.
Gal-gal lime does not taper to the ends.
Gal-gal lime skin is smooth like the sangtāra's.
Gal-gal lime is remarkably juicy.
Jānbīrī lime is orange-shaped.
Jānbīrī lime is yellow but not orange-yellow.
Jānbīrī lime smells like the citron.
Jānbīrī lime is deliciously acid.
Sadā-fal is pear-shaped.
Sadā-fal colours like the quince.
Sadā-fal ripens sweet but not to the sickly-sweetness of the orange.
Amrd-fal is an orange-like fruit.
Lemon may be as large as the gal-gal.
Lemon is acid.
Amal-bīd was first seen after three years in Hindūstān.
A needle melts away if put inside amal-bīd.
Amal-bīd is as acid as the citron and lemon.
Jāsūn is not a grass.
Jāsūn tree is rather taller than the bush of the red-rose.
Jāsūn flower is fuller in colour than that of the pomegranate.
Jāsūn flower may be of the size of the red-rose.
When jāsūn-bud opens, a stem on which other petals grow is seen like a heart amongst its expanded petals.
Jāsūn flowers fade in just one day.
Jāsūn blossoms very well through the four months of the rains.
Jāsūn seems to flower all through the year.
Jāsūn gives no perfume despite its profusion.
Kanīr grows both red and white.
Kanīr is five petalled like the peach-flower.
Kanīr opens 14 or 15 flowers from one place.
Kanīr flowers look like one great flower when seen from a distance.
Oleander-bush is taller than the rose-bush.
Red oleander has a sort of scent, faint and agreeable.
Oleander blooms well and profusely in the rains.
Oleander is had through most of the year.
Kīūrā has a very agreeable perfume.
Musk has the defect of being dry.
Kīūrā may be called moist musk.
Kīūrā flowers are perhaps 1-1/2 to 2 qārīsh (13-1/2 to 18 inches) long.
Kīūrā has long leaves having the character of the reed gharau.
Kīūrā leaves have spines.
Kīūrā outer leaves are greener and more spiny.
Kīūrā inner leaves are soft and white.
Kīūrā tree when first coming up not yet showing any trunk is like the bush of the male-reed.
Kīūrā tree has wider and more spiny leaves than the male-reed.
Kīūrā trunk is very shapeless with roots remaining shown.
Yāsman is another flower.
White yāsman is called champa.
Champa is larger and more strongly scented than yāsman-flower.
There are four seasons in those countries.
There are three seasons in Hindūstān.
Four months are summer in Hindūstān.
Four months are the rains in Hindūstān.
Four months are winter in Hindūstān.
The beginning of Hindūstān months is from the welcome of the crescent-moons.
Every three years Hindūstān adds a month to the year.
If a month is added to the rainy season, the next is added three years later to the winter months.
Chait, Baisākh, Jeṭh and Asāṛh are the hot months.
Sāwan, Bhādoṅ, Kū,ār and Kātik are the rainy months.
Aghan, Pūs, Māgh and Phālgun are the cold months.
Jeṭh and Asāṛh are the force of the heat.
Sāwan and Bhādoṅ are the force of the rains.
Pūs and Māgh are the force of the cold.
There are six seasons in Hindūstān by this classification.
Sanīchar is Saturday.
Rabī-bār is Sunday.
Som-wār is Monday.
Mangal-wār is Tuesday.
Budh-bār is Wednesday.
Brihaspat-bār is Thursday.
Shukr-bār is Friday.
Daqīqa is about as long as six repetitions of the Fātiḥa with the Bismillāh.
A day-and-night is as long as 8640 repetitions of the Fātiḥa with the Bismillāh.
In those countries a day-and-night is divided into 24 parts, each called an hour.
An hour is divided into 60 parts, each called a minute.
A day-and-night consists of 1440 minutes.
The people of Hind divide the night-and-day into 60 parts, each called a gharī.
The people of Hind divide the night into four and the day into four.
Each part is called a pahr or pās.
A body of gharīālīs is chosen and appointed in all considerable towns of Hindūstān.
Gharīālīs cast a broad brass thing, perhaps as large as a tray and about two hands'-thickness.
This brass thing is called a gharīāl and hung up in a high place.
Gharīālīs have a vessel perforated at the bottom like an hour-cup.
The perforated cup fills in one gharī (24 minutes).
Gharīālīs put the perforated cup into water and wait till it fills.
When the cup fills the first time, gharīālīs strike the gong once with their mallets.
When the cup fills a second time, gharīālīs strike the gong twice.
Gharīālīs announce the end of a watch by several rapid blows of their mallets.
It used to be the rule to beat the sign of a watch only when the watch ended.
Sleepers chancing to wake in the night and hearing the sound of a third or fourth gharī would not know whether it was of the second or third night-watch.
Babur ordered that at night or on a cloudy day the sign of the watch should be struck after that of the gharī.
Babur ordered that after striking the third gharī of the first night-watch, the gharīālīs should pause and then strike the sign of the watch.
Babur ordered that after striking four gharīs of the third night-watch, gharīālīs should pause and then strike the sign of the third watch.
This order did very well.
Anyone happening to wake in the night and hearing the gong would know what gharī of what watch of night it was.
The gharī is divided into 60 parts, each part being called a pal.
By this each night-and-day consists of 3,500 pals.
The length of a pal is the shutting and opening of the eyelids 60 times.
In a night-and-day there would be 216,000 shuttings and openings of the eyes.
A pal is about equal to 8 repetitions of the Qul-huwa-allāh and Bismillāh.
This would be 28,000 repetitions in a night-and-day.
The people of Hind have well-arranged measures.
8 ratīs equal 1 māsha.
4 māsha equal 1 tānk.
1 tānk equals 32 ratīs.
5 māsha equal 1 miṣqāl.
1 miṣqāl equals 40 ratīs.
12 māsha equal 1 tūla.
1 tūla equals 96 ratīs.
14 tūla equal 1 ser.
40 ser equal 1 mānbān.
12 mānbān equal 1 mānī.
100 mānī are called a mīnāsa.
Pearls and jewels are weighed by the tānk.
The people of Hind have an excellent mode of reckoning.
100,000 are called a lak.
100 laks equal a krūr.
100 krūrs equal an arb.
100 arbs equal 1 karb.
100 karb's equal 1 nīl.
100 nīls equal 1 padam.
100 padams equal 1 sāng.
The fixing of such high reckonings is proof of the great amount of wealth in Hindūstān.
Most of the inhabitants of Hindūstān are pagans.
Pagans are called Hindū.
Most Hindūs believe in the transmigration of souls.
All artisans are Hindūs.
All wage-earners are Hindūs.
All officials are Hindūs.
In those countries dwellers in the wilds get tribal names.
In Hindūstān the settled people of the cultivated lands and villages get tribal names.
Hindūstān has well-arranged systems of measurement including ratīs, māsha, tānk, miṣqāl, tūla, and ser.
Pearls and jewels in Hindūstān are weighed by the tānk.
The people of Hindūstān have an excellent mode of reckoning with units called lak, krūr, arb, karb, nīl, padam, and sāng.
Most inhabitants of Hindūstān are pagans called Hindūs.
All artisans, wage-earners, and officials in Hindūstān are Hindūs.
Every artisan in Hindūstān follows the trade passed down from forefather to forefather.
Hindūstān has few charms according to Babur.
The people of Hindūstān have no good looks.
Hindūstān lacks good horses, good dogs, grapes, musk-melons, and first-rate fruits.
Hindūstān has no ice, cold water, good bread, or cooked food in the bazārs.
Hindūstān has no hot-baths, colleges, candles, or torches.
Hindūstān uses lamp-men called diwatī instead of candles and torches.
Hindūstān has no running-waters in gardens or residences.
Peasants and people of low standing in Hindūstān go about naked or wearing a lungūtā.
Hindūstān is a large country with masses of gold and silver.
The air in Hindūstān during the Rains is very fine and healthy.
It sometimes rains 10, 15, or 20 times a day during the Rains in Hindūstān.
The damp air during the Rains in Hindūstān ruins bows, armor, books, cloth, and utensils.
Hindūstān experiences a strong dust-laden wind called āndhī during the hot season.
Hindūstān has unnumbered and endless workmen of every kind.
There is a fixed caste in Hindūstān for every sort of work passed down from father to son.
Babur's revenue from Bhīra to Bihār is 52 krūrs as of 935 AH (1528 AD).
Eight or nine krūrs of Babur's revenue come from parganas of rāīs and rājas who receive allowance and maintenance.
Babur gave 70 laks from the Treasury to Humāyūn.
Babur bestowed a treasure house on Humāyūn without ascertaining its contents.
Babur gave 10, 8, 7, or 6 laks to various begs.
Babur distributed money-gifts from the Treasury to the whole army including Afghāns, Hazāras, and Arabs.
Babur gave 17 laks to Kāmran.
Babur gave 15 laks to Muḥammad-i-zamān Mīrzā.
Babur gave masses of gold, silver, jewels, and slaves to `Askarī, Hindāl, and other relations.
Babur sent valuable gifts to relations in Samarkand, Khurāsān, Kāshghar, and `Irāq.
Babur sent offerings to holy men in Samarkand, Khurāsān, Makka, and Madīna.
Babur gave one shāhrukhi for every soul in Kābul and the valley-side of Varsak.
There was remarkable dislike and hostility between the people of Āgra and Babur's men upon his first coming.
Peasantry and soldiers ran away in fear of Babur's men when he first came to Āgra.
Qāsim Saṃbhalī held Saṃbhal in rebellion against Babur.
Niẕām Khān held Bīāna in rebellion against Babur.
Ḥasan Khān Mīwātī held Mīwāt and was the sole leader of trouble and mischief against Babur.
Muḥammad Zaitun held Dūlpūr in rebellion against Babur.
Tātār Khān Sārang-khānī held Gūālīār in rebellion against Babur.
Ḥusain Khān Nuḥānī held Rāprī in rebellion against Babur.
Quṯb Khān held Itāwa in rebellion against Babur.
`Ālam Khān held Kālpī in rebellion against Babur.
Afghāns held Qanauj and the country beyond the Ganges in independent hostility against Babur.
Naṣīr Khān Nuḥānī, Ma`rūf Farmūlī, and other amīrs had been in rebellion for three or four years before Ibrāhīm's death.
The rebels made Bihār Khān the son of Daryā Khān Nuḥānī their pādshāh under the style Sulṯān Muḥammad.
Marghūb the slave held Mahāwīn against Babur.
It was the hot season when Babur came to Āgra.
All inhabitants had run away in terror when Babur came to Āgra.
There was no grain or corn available when Babur came to Āgra.
Villages engaged in thieving and highway-robbery out of hostility to Babur.
The year Babur came to Āgra was very hot with violent pestilential winds.
Masses of people died from pestilential winds when Babur came to Āgra.
The greater part of Babur's begs and best braves became unwilling to stay in Hindūstān.
Secretary Aḥmadī and Treasurer Walī behaved badly during Babur's time in Hindūstān.
Khwāja Kalān did well in the march from Kābul and in Ibrāhīm's defeat.
Khwāja Kalān changed his views a few days after the capture of Āgra.
Khwāja Kalān became the one most zealous for departure from Hindūstān.
Babur summoned all the begs and took counsel about the unsteadiness in his army.
Babur believed that supremacy and grip on the world require means and resources.
Babur believed that sovereignty and command are impossible without lands and retainers.
Babur defeated enemies through God's grace to take their broad lands.
Babur questioned why his people wanted to abandon countries taken at such risk of life.
Babur asked whether it was better to remain in Kābul as the sport of harsh poverty.
Babur's words recalled just and reasonable views to his begs' minds.
Khwāja Kalān had no heart to stay in Hindūstān.
Babur bestowed Ghaznī, Girdīz, and the Sulṯān Mas`ūdī Hazāra on Khwāja Kalān.
Babur gave Khwāja Kalān the Hindūstān pargana of G'hūram worth 3 or 4 laks.
Khwāja Mīr-i-mīrān was settled to go to Kābul with the gifts.
Mullā Ḥasan the banker and Tūka Hindu were put in custody of the gifts.
Khwāja Kalān inscribed a couplet on the wall of his residence in Dihlī expressing loathing for Hindūstān.
Babur composed a verse in response to Khwāja Kalān's couplet.
Babur sent Mullā Apāq into Kūl with royal letters of favour for soldiers and quiver-wearers.
Shaikh Gūran came to do obeisance to Babur bringing 2 to 3,000 soldiers and quiver-wearers.
Mullā Apāq had formerly been in a very low position.
Mullā Apāq gathered his brethren and brought them to Babur two or three years before this time.
Mullā Apāq brought the Aūrūq-zāī and other Afghāns of the banks of the Sind to Babur.
Yūnas-i-`alī lost his way traveling from Dihlī to Āgra and got separated from Humāyūn.
Yūnas-i-`alī met `Alī Khān Farmūlī's sons and had a small affair with them.
Yūnas-i-`alī took `Alī Khān Farmūlī's sons prisoners and brought them to Babur.
One of `Alī Khān Farmūlī's captured sons was sent to his father with Mīrzā Mughūl.
`Alī Khān Farmūlī went to Mīwāt during the time of break-up.
`Alī Khān Farmūlī came to Babur when Mīrzā Mughūl returned.
Babur promoted `Alī Khān Farmūlī and gave him parganas worth 25 laks.
Ibrāhīm had appointed amīrs under Muṣṯafa Farmūlī and Fīrūz Khān Sārang-khānī to act against rebel amīrs of the East.
Muṣṯafa Farmūlī fought the rebel amīrs and thoroughly drubbed them.
Muṣṯafa Farmūlī died before Ibrāhīm's defeat.
Shaikh Bāyazīd, Muṣṯafa's younger brother, led and watched over his elder brother's men.
Shaikh Bāyazīd came to serve Babur together with Fīrūz Khān, Maḥmūd Khān Nuḥānī, and Qāẓī Jīā.
Babur showed greater kindness and favour to Fīrūz Khān, Shaikh Bāyazīd, Maḥmūd Khān, and Qāẓī Jīā than was their claim.
Babur gave Fīrūz Khān 1 krūr, 46 laks, and 5000 tankas from Jūnpūr.
Babur gave Shaikh Bāyazīd 1 krūr, 48 laks, and 50,000 tankas from Aūd.
Babur gave Maḥmūd Khān 90 laks and 35,000 tankas from Ghāzīpūr.
Babur gave Qāẓī Jīā 20 laks.
A large party was held in the pillared-porch of Ibrāhīm's private apartments a few days after the `Īd of Shawwāl.
Babur bestowed a chār-qab, sword-belt, and a tīpūchāq horse on Humāyūn at the party.
Babur bestowed chār-qabs, sword-belts, and dagger-belts on Chīn-tīmūr Sulṯān, Mahdī Khwāja, and Muḥammad Sl. Mīrzā.
Babur gave sword-belts and dagger-belts to begs and braves according to their rank.
Babur comforted Ustād `Alī-qulī when he was in great distress about a furnace miscalculation.
Babur put a robe of honour on Ustād `Alī-qulī to bring him out of shame.
Babur looked with favour on Fatḥ Khān.
Babur gave Fatḥ Khān the parganas that had been his father `Aẕam-humāyūn's and other lands.
Babur set aside the title `Aẕam-humāyūn because it was not seemly for any person to bear it on account of Humāyūn.
Babur gave Fatḥ Khān _Sarwānī_ the title of Khān-i-jahān.
Babur invited Fatḥ Khān _Sarwānī_ to a wine-party.
Babur gave Fatḥ Khān wine and bestowed on him a turban and head-to-foot of his own wearing.
Babur allowed Fatḥ Khān to go to his own districts.
Babur arranged for Fatḥ Khān's son Maḥmūd to remain always in waiting.
Babur sent Muḥammad `Alī with an injunction to Humāyūn to appoint begs to Jūnpūr and come quickly.
Babur appointed an expedition to make a plundering excursion into the Bīāna neighbourhood.
Babur gave orders to act with `Ālam Khān in the way he approved in the matter of Bīāna.
Babur believed that most Hindūstānīs are ignorant and unskilled in military move and stand.
Babur pardoned Niẕām Khān's faults despite his impropriety.
Babur sent Niẕām Khān royal letters.
Babur bestowed on Niẕām Khān a pargana in Mīān-dū-āb worth 20 laks.
Babur sent Dost, Lord-of-the-gate to Bīāna for a time.
Babur bestowed Bīāna on Madhī Khwāja with a fixed allowance of 70 laks.
Babur joined Raḥīm-dād a few Bhīra men and Lāhorīs with Hastachī _tūnqiṯār_ and his brethren.
Babur assigned parganas in Gūālīār to those sent to secure the fort.
Babur assigned a subsistence allowance of 20 laks to Tātār Khān on Bīānwān pargana.
Babur bestowed a pargana worth a few laks on Muḥammad _Zaitūn_.
Babur made Dūlpūr a royal domain with Abū'l-fatḥ _Turkmān_ as its military-collector.
Babur appointed Chīn-tīmūr Sl. with several commanders against Ḥamīd Khān _Sārang-khānī_.
Babur had not seen Hindūstānī dishes before and ordered Ibrāhīm's cooks to be brought.
Babur kept four cooks out of 50 or 60 of Ibrāhīm's cooks.
Babur strictly ordered the tasters to compel any Hindūstānīs present while food was cooking to taste that food.
Babur ate a good deal of a dish of hare and much fried carrot on Friday after the Afternoon Prayer.
Babur felt sick after eating the poisoned Hindūstānī food.
Babur vomited after eating the poisoned food.
Babur had never vomited after food before.
Babur had the cooks put in ward.
Babur ordered some of the vomit given to a dog and the dog to be watched.
Babur ordered the taster cut in pieces.
Babur ordered the cook skinned alive.
Babur ordered one of the women thrown under an elephant.
Babur ordered the other woman shot with a match-lock.
Babur kept the old woman under guard.
Babur drank a bowl of milk on Saturday after the poisoning attempt.
Babur drank _`araq_ in which stamped-clay was dissolved on Sunday.
Babur drank milk with dissolved stamped-clay and theriac on Monday.
Babur believed the poisoning attempt was God's favour giving him new life.
Babur wrote a detailed letter about the poisoning attempt to Kābul.
Babur gave Ibrahim's mother over to Yūnas-i-`alī and Khwājagī Asad.
Babur had kept Ibrahim's son with much respect and delicacy.
Babur did not keep Ibrahim's son in Agra after the poisoning attempt.
Babur started Ibrahim's son off to Kāmrān with Mullā Sarsān.
Babur made arrangements for Humāyūn's campaign against the Eastern rebels.
Babur left Shāh Mīr Ḥusain and Sl. Junaid with a body of braves in Jūna-pūr.
Babur posted Qāẓī Jīā with the commanders in Jūna-pūr.
Babur placed Shaikh Bāyazīd in Aude.
Babur sent someone to chase fear from `Ālam Khān's heart.
Babur met Humāyūn in the Garden of Eight-paradises on Sunday the 3rd of the 2nd Rabī`.
Babur sent commanders to ride light to Bīāna to reinforce it.
Babur had kept Nāhar Khān as a hostage from the fight with Ibrāhīm.
Babur dressed Nāhar Khān in a robe of honour.
Babur made promises to Nāhar Khān for his father.
Babur gave Nāhar Khān leave to go to his father.
Babur believed it was ill-judged to let Nāhar Khān go at that time.
Babur was present at parties during the rainy season.
Babur found it abhorrent that Humāyūn sinned every few days at the parties.
Nāhar Khān was given a robe of honour and leave to go.
Ḥasan Khān joined Rānā Sangā in Toda after his son received leave from Babur.
Much rain was falling during the period of respite.
Parties were frequent during the respite period.
Humāyūn attended the parties despite finding them abhorrent to him.
Humāyūn sinned every few days during this period.
Mullā Bābā of Pashāghar and his younger brother Bābā Shaikh deserted while coming from Fort Victory to join the Hindūstān army.
Balkh fell into the hands of Kītīn-qarā through the enfeeblement of its garrison.
Mullā Bābā and Bābā Shaikh took possession of Ghūrī fort with a few Aūzbegs.
Mīr Hamah slashed at Bābā Shaikh and put him in bonds.
Bābā Shaikh's wound was severe and his head was cut off.
Babur uplifted Bābā Shaikh's head with favour and kindness.
Babur promised Bāqī shaghāwal a ser of gold for the head of each of the ill-conditioned old couple.
Babur gave Mīr Hamah one ser of gold for Bābā Shaikh's head.
Qismatī brought back several heads he had cut off.
Qismatī and Būjka beat two of the Pagan's scouting-parties and made 70 to 80 prisoners.
Ḥasan Khān Mīwātī joined Rānā Sangā.
Ustād `Alī-qulī discharged stones from a large mortar on Sunday the 8th of Jumāda I.
The stone from the large mortar was thrown 1600 paces.
Babur gave Ustād `Alī-qulī a sword-belt, robe of honour, and a horse.
Babur left Āgra on Monday the 9th of Jumāda I on his journey for the Holy War.
Babur remained three or four days in the open country to collect his army.
Little confidence was placed in Hindūstānī people.
`Ālam Khān was sent to Gūālīār to reinforce Raḥīm-dād.
Makan, Qāsim Beg, Ḥamīd and his brothers, and Muḥammad Zaitūn were sent to Sanbal.
Rānā Sangā advanced swiftly with all his army.
The Bīāna garrison made an incautious sally and was routed by the enemy.
Sangur Khān Janjūha became a martyr in the battle.
Kitta Beg was slashed across the shoulder by a pagan.
Kitta Beg suffered great pain and could not join the Holy-battle with Rānā Sangā.
Kitta Beg remained blemished after his recovery.
Qismatī, Shāh Manṣūr Barlās and others from Bīāna praised the fierceness and valour of the pagan army.
Qāsim Master-of-the-horse was sent to dig wells in the Madhākūr pargana.
Babur marched out of Āgra on Saturday the 14th of Jumāda I.
Babur considered that well-watered ground for a large camp was at Sīkrī.
Qismatī and Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān were sent ahead to look for camping-ground on the bank of the Sīkrī-lake.
Beg Mīrak Mughūl was sent out to get news of the Pagan.
The Pagan was heard of as having arrived at a place one kuroh on Babur's side of Basāwar.
Mahdī Khwāja and Muḥammad Sl. Mīrzā rejoined Babur with troops from Bīāna.
`Abdu'l-`azīz went out of Sīkrī looking neither before nor behind along the road to Kanwā.
The Rānā made 4 or 5,000 of his men fall suddenly on `Abdu'l-`azīz's party.
`Abdu'l-`azīz and Mullā Apāq had 1000 to 1500 men with them.
Many of `Abdu'l-`azīz's men were made prisoner.
Khalīfa's Muḥibb-i-`alī was sent with Khalīfa's retainers to support `Abdu'l-`azīz.
Mullā Ḥusain and others were sent to support the reinforcement.
Muḥammad `Alī Jang-jang was also sent to support the reinforcement.
The Pagan hurried off `Abdu'l-`azīz and his men and took his standard.
Mullā Ni`mat, Mullā Dāūd and the younger brother of Mullā Apāq were martyred.
The pagans overcame T̤āhir-tibrī, the maternal uncle of Khalīfa's Muḥibb-i-`alī.
Muḥibb-i-`alī was thrown down but Bāltū brought him out.
The enemy pursued for over a kuroh but stopped at the sight of Muḥ. `Alī Jang-jang's troops.
Babur put on his armour and his horse's mail and rode out at the gallop.
Babur advanced one kuroh and the foe turned aside.
Babur dismounted with a large lake on one side for the sake of water.
Babur's front was defended by carts chained together.
The space between each two carts was 7 or 8 qārī.
Musṯafa Rūmī had the carts made in the Rūmī way.
Ustād `Alī-qulī was jealous of Musṯafa Rūmī.
Musṯafa was posted to the right, in front of Humāyūn.
Khurāsānī and Hindūstānī spadesmen and miners dug a ditch where the carts did not reach.
People in the army showed signs of want of heart due to the Pagan's rapid advance and the defeat of `Abdu'l-`azīz.
Ropes of raw hide on wooden tripods were stretched to defend the camp where there were no carts.
The ropes were set 7 or 8 qārī apart.
It took 20 or 25 days before the camp defences were fully ready.
Qāsim-i-ḥusain arrived from Kābul with 500 men.
Qāsim-i-ḥusain is the son of a daughter of Sl. Ḥusain M.
Aḥmad-i-yūsuf, Qawwām-i-aūrdū Shāh and several single friends of Babur arrived from Kābul.
Muḥammad Sharīf, the astrologer, came from Kābul.
Bābā Dost the water-bearer came from Kābul with three strings of camels loaded with Ghaznī wines.
Muḥammad Sharīf insisted that Mars was in the west and that those fighting from the east side would be defeated.
Timid people became more shattered in heart after hearing Muḥammad Sharīf's words.
Babur gave no ear to Muḥammad Sharīf's wild words and made no change in his operations.
Shaikh Jamāl was sent to collect quiver-wearers from between the Ganges and Jumna and from Dihlī.
Shaikh Jamāl was ordered to over-run and plunder the Mīwāt villages.
Mullā Tark-i-`alī was ordered to join Shaikh Jamāl and neglect nothing of ruin and plunder in Mīwāt.
Maghfūr the Dīwān was given orders to the same purport.
They over-ran and raided a few villages in lonely corners.
They took some prisoners but their passage did not arouse much anxiety.
Babur reflected while riding that the wish to cease from sin had been always in his mind.
Babur's forbidden acts had set lasting stain upon his heart.
Babur vowed to obey with regard to his transgression of drinking wine.
Babur had all his flagons and cups of silver and gold brought and broken up.
The fragments of the gold and silver vessels were shared out to deserving persons and darwīshes.
`Asas was the first to agree in renouncing wine.
`Asas had already agreed about leaving his beard untrimmed.
About 300 begs and persons of the household renounced wine that night and next day.
The wine with Babur was poured on the ground.
Bābā Dost's wine was ordered salted to make vinegar.
A well was ordered to be dug at the place where the wine was poured upon the ground.
The well was built up with stone and had an almshouse beside it.
The well was finished in Muḥarram 935 AH.
Babur had vowed to remit the tamghā to all Musalmāns if he gained victory over Sangā.
Darwīsh-i-muḥammad Sārbān and Shaikh Zain reminded Babur of his vow.
Babur remitted the tamghā to all Musalmāns of his dominions.
Babur sent for the clerks and ordered them to write a farmān concerning the two important acts.
Shaikh Zain wrote the farmān with his own elegance.
Babur's nature is prone to evil according to his own reflection.
Babur received a secret inspiration to renounce wine.
Babur heard an infallible voice during his moment of renunciation.
Babur declared that he would subjugate his passions.
Babur engraved on the tablet of his heart that he turns unto God with repentance.
Many of Babur's courtiers embraced abstinence at the same assemblage.
Crowds of Babur's subjects hourly attain the auspicious happiness of renouncing wine.
Babur declared with sincerity that he would subjugate his passions.
Babur made a public resolution to abstain from wine.
Babur ordered the destruction of flagons, cups, and gold and silver utensils used for wine.
The destroyed wine vessels were distributed among the poor and needy.
Many courtiers embraced abstinence after Babur's example.
Babur issued a universal decree prohibiting the consumption, manufacture, sale, purchase, possession, and transport of strong drink in his imperial dominions.
Babur abolished the tax on Muslims throughout his territories as a thank-offering for victories and God's acceptance of his repentance.
Babur valued the advice and counsel of his commanders, particularly Khalifa.
Babur summoned his military leaders and urged them to take oath not to retreat from battle.
Babur's forces faced desertions from several commanders during a military campaign.
Babur advanced to fight on New Year's Day, the 9th of the second Jumada (March 13th).
Babur arranged his forces in right, left, and centre divisions with carts and matchlock-men.
Babur personally galloped between divisions to encourage his commanders and soldiers.
Babur sent unmailed braves ahead to engage the enemy and take an omen before the main battle.
Babur fought the Battle of Kanwa on Saturday, the 13th of the second Jumada (March 17th, 1527).
Babur's opponent in the Battle of Kanwa was Rana Sanga, a pagan chief.
Rana Sanga had previously submitted to Babur but later became proud and rebellious.
Rana Sanga assembled a coalition of ten powerful pagan chiefs against Babur.
Rana Sanga's forces were estimated at approximately 201,000 soldiers according to Hindu calculations.
Babur's forces used matchlock-men and cannoneers positioned behind chained carts as a defensive strategy.
Niẕāmu'd-dīn Ali Khalifa was instrumental in organizing and arranging Babur's military formations.
Babur positioned himself in the centre of his military array during the Battle of Kanwa.
Babur arranged matchlockmen and cannoneers along a line of chained carts in front of his army.
Babur's army was arrayed and steadfast in a manner that impressed observers.
Niẕāmu'd-dīn `Alī Khalīfa exerted himself strenuously to effect the arrangement and organization of the army.
Babur's post was in the centre of the army.
Babur positioned various commanders and nobles in the right-hand of the centre, including Chīn-tīmūr Sulṯān and others.
Babur positioned various commanders and nobles in the left-hand of the centre, including Sulṯān `Alā'u'd-dīn `Ālam Khān and others.
Babur appointed Muḥammad Humāyūn Bahādur to command the right wing.
Babur appointed various commanders to the left wing, including Sayyid Mahdī Khwāja and Muḥammad Sl. Mīrzā.
Babur posted trusted household retainers Tardīka and Malik Qāsim with Mughūls for the flank-movement of the right wing.
Babur posted Mūmin Ātāka and Rustam Turkmān with special troops for the flank-movement of the left wing.
Niẕāmu'd-dīn Sulṯān Muḥammad Bakhshī served as Chief of the Staff and posted the ghāzīs of Islām.
Babur issued an imperative order that none should quit his post or fight without command.
The battle began after one watch of the day had elapsed.
Chīn-tīmūr Sulṯān reinforced Khusrau Kūkūldāsh and Malik Qāsim and bore the pagans back toward their centre.
Muṣṯafa of Rūm had his post in the centre of the right wing and brought forward carts to break the ranks of pagans with matchlock and culverin.
Qāsim-i-ḥusain Sulṯān, Niẕāmu'd-dīn Aḥmad-i-yūsuf, and Qawām Beg hastened to help in the thick of the fight.
Babur sent Hindū Beg and other commanders to help as pagan troops continued to arrive.
Babur sent Muhammadī Kūkūldāsh, Khwājagī Asad, Yūnas-i-'alī, Shāh Manṣūr Barlās, `Abdu'l-lāh the librarian, and Dost the Lord-of-the-Gate to reinforce his forces.
Mūmin Ātāka and Rustam Turkmān moved to the rear of the pagan host.
Babur sent Khwāja Maḥmūd and `Alī Ātāka to support Mūmin Ātāka and Rustam Turkmān.
Muḥammad Sl. Mīrzā, `Ādil Sulṯān, `Abdu'l-`azīz, Qūtlūq-qadam, Muḥammad `Alī Jang-jang, and Shāh Ḥusain stretched out the arm to fight and stood firm.
Babur sent Khwāja Kamālu'd-dīn Ḥusain with a body of dīwānīs to support the left wing.
Babur issued an order that the special royal corps should emerge from behind the carts and join battle on both sides of the centre.
Ustād `Alī-qulī stood in front of the centre and discharged large stones against the pagan ranks.
Babur ordered the carts of the centre to be advanced.
Babur moved towards the pagan soldiers with Victory and Fortune on his right and Prestige and Conquest on his left.
The victorious troops followed Babur from all sides when they witnessed his advance.
The pagan right wing made repeated and desperate attacks on the left wing of Babur's army.
The pagan forces eventually dispersed like carded wool before the wind.
Many pagan soldiers fell dead on the field of battle.
Ḥasan Khān of Mīwāt was killed by the force of a matchlock.
Rāwal Ūdī Sīngh of Bāgar, ruler of Dungarpūr with 12,000 horse, was slain.
Rāī Chandrabān Chūhān with 4,000 horse was slain.
Bhūpat Rāo, lord of Chandīrī with 6,000 horse, was slain.
Mānik-chand Chūhān and Dilpat Rāo, each with 4,000 horse, were slain.
Kankū, Karm Sīngh, and Dankūsī, each with 3,000 horse, were slain.
Babur assumed the title of Ghāzī (Victor in a Holy-war) after the victory.
Babur wrote a quatrain expressing his gratitude for becoming a ghāzī.
Shaikh Zain found the words 'Fatḥ-i-pādshāh-i-islām' to be a chronogram of the victory.
Mīr Gesū, a person from Kābul, also found the same words to be a chronogram and composed them in a quatrain.
Shaikh Zain had previously found the date of the victory at Dībālpūr in the words 'Wasaṯ-i-shahr Rabī`u'l-awwal'.
Mīr Gesū had found the same words for the date of the victory at Dībālpūr.
After the victory, Babur hurried the foes off, dismounting one after another.
The pagan's encirclement was approximately 2 kurohs from Babur's camp.
Babur sent Muḥammadī, `Abdu'l-`azīz, `Alī Khān and others in pursuit of the pagan forces.
Babur went about a kuroh beyond the pagan's camp before turning back because it was late in the day.
Babur regretted not pursuing the pagan forces himself and leaving the matter to others.
Babur reached Āgra on August 28th.
People were telling wild news about Shaikh Bāyazīd during this period.
Babur began religious and metrical exercises on Friday the 2nd of Ẕū'l-ḥijja.
Babur composed a couplet that he cut up into 504 measures.
Babur fell ill on the 2nd of Ẕū'l-ḥijja and the illness lasted nine days.
Babur rode out for an excursion to Kūl and Saṃbal on Thursday the 29th of Ẕū'l-ḥijja.
Humāyūn had left Darwīsh(-i-`alī) and Yūsuf-i-`alī in Saṃbal.
Humāyūn's forces fought Quṯb Sīrwānī and a party of rājas and beat them.
Shaikh Gūran entertained Babur hospitably at his house.
Babur crossed the river Gang (Ganges) on Wednesday.
Babur dismounted in Saṃbal on Thursday.
Rāo Sīrwānī set food before Babur and served him in Sikandara.
Babur galloped on alone to within a kuroh of Āgra.
Babur had fever and ague on Sunday the 16th of Muḥarram.
Babur's fever returned again and again during the next 25 or 26 days.
Babur suffered much from thirst and want of sleep during his illness.
Babur composed a quatrain while ill.
Two paternal-aunt begīms, Fakhr-i-jahān Begīm and Khadīja-sulṯān Begīm, arrived on Saturday the 28th of Ṣafar.
Babur went to above Sikandarābād to wait on his kinswomen.
Ustād `Alī-qulī discharged a stone from a large mortar on Sunday.
The mortar broke in pieces and killed eight men.
Babur rode out to visit Sīkrī on Monday the 7th of the first Rabī`.
An octagonal platform in the middle of the lake at Sīkrī was ready.
Babur started on Monday night the 14th of the first Rabī` with the intention of making Holy-war against Chandīrī.
Babur left Anwār by boat and disembarked beyond Chandwār.
Babur crossed the river on Thursday the 2nd of the latter Rabī`.
There was 4 or 5 days delay before the army got across the river.
Babur went into a boat on the Chaṃbal and passed the junction.
Shaikh Bāyazīd's misconduct and action made it certain that he had hostile intentions.
Muḥammad `Alī Jang-jang was detached from the army and sent to bring together forces from Qanūj.
Ten elephants were given to Muḥammad `Alī.
Bābā Chuhra was sent to join Muḥammad `Alī.
Babur dismounted within a kuroh of Kālpī on Wednesday the 8th of the latter Rabī`.
Bābā Sl. came to wait on Babur in the camp at Kālpī.
Bābā Sl. is a son of Khalīl Sl. who is a younger brother of Sl. Sa`īd Khān.
Bābā Sl. fled from his elder brother but repented and went back from the Andar-āb border.
Babur dismounted at `Ālam Khān's house in Kālpī where he set Hindūstānī food before him.
Babur marched from Kālpī on Monday the 13th of the month.
Babur dismounted at Īrij on Friday.
Babur dismounted at Bāndīr on Saturday.
Chīn-tīmūr Sl. was put at the head of 6 or 7000 men and sent ahead against Chandīrī on Sunday the 19th of the month.
Various begs went with Chīn-tīmūr Sl. against Chandīrī.
Babur dismounted near Kachwa on Friday the 24th of the month.
Kachwa was bestowed on the son of Badru'd-dīn.
Kachwa is a shut-in place having lowish hills all round it.
A large lake encloses Kachwa on three sides.
The inhabitants of Kachwa use very small boats to flee on the lake.
There are two other lakes before Kachwa is reached.
Babur waited a day in Kachwa to appoint overseers and spadesmen to level the road.
Between Kachwa and Chandīrī the country is jungly.
Babur left Kachwa and halted one night before dismounting within 3 kurohs of Chandīrī.
The citadel of Chandīrī stands on a hill.
Below the citadel are the town and outer-fort.
Babur dismounted beside Bahjat Khān's tank on Tuesday the 28th of the month.
Babur assigned posts round the walled town to centre, right, and left on Tuesday the 28th.
Ustād `Alī-qulī chose ground that had no fall for his stone-discharge.
Chandīrī formerly belonged to the Sulṯāns of Mandāū.
Sl. Maḥmūd took possession of Mandū and its neighbouring parts.
Muḥammad Shāh laid hands on Chandīrī and put it under Sl. Sikandar's protection.
Muḥammad Shāh survived Sl. Sikandar and died in Sl. Ibrāhīm's time.
Muḥammad Shāh left a very young son called Aḥmad Shāh.
Sl. Ibrāhīm drove out Aḥmad Shāh and replaced him with a man of his own.
Rānā Sangā led out an army against Sl. Ibrāhīm.
Ibrāhīm's begs turned against him at Dūlpūr.
Chandīrī fell into the Rānā's hands.
The Rānā gave Chandīrī to Medinī Rāo.
Medinī Rāo was in Chandīrī with 4 or 5000 other pagans.
There was friendship between Medinī Rāo and Ārāīsh Khān.
Ārāīsh Khān was sent with Shaikh Gūran to speak to Medinī Rāo with favour and kindness.
Babur promised Shamsābād in exchange for Chandīrī.
No adjustment of matters was reached with Medinī Rāo.
Babur marched from Bahjat Khān's tank intending to assault Chandīrī on Tuesday the 6th of the first Jumāda.
Khalīfa brought a letter saying the troops appointed for the East had been beaten and abandoned Laknau.
Babur said there is no ground for perturbation or alarm and that nothing comes to pass but what is predestined of God.
Babur said he would assault the fort the next day.
The enemy strengthened the citadel and posted men in the outer-fort.
Babur's men went up from all round the fort at night.
The few men in the outer-fort did not fight and fled into the citadel.
Babur ordered his men to arm, go to their posts, and attack on Wednesday the 7th of the first Jumāda.
Babur rode out with drum and standard but dismissed them till the fighting should grow hot.
Babur went to watch Ustād `Alī-qulī's stone-discharge.
The stone-discharge effected nothing because the ground had no fall and the fort-walls were extremely strong.
A double-walled road runs down one side of the hill to water.
The double-walled road was the one place for attack.
Babur's braves did not turn back despite the pagans throwing down stones and flaming fire.
Shāhīm the centurion got up where the double-walled wall touches the wall of the outer fort.
The double-walled road was taken.
The pagans fled in haste from the citadel.
The pagans came out again quite naked and renewed the fight.
The pagans put many of Babur's men to flight.
The pagans put all their ladies and beauties to death.
About 2 or 300 pagans entered Medinī Rāo's house.
Almost all the pagans in Medinī Rāo's house killed one another.
Babur captured Chandīrī in 2 or 3 garīs without drum and standard.
A pillar of pagan-heads was ordered set up on a hill north-west of Chandīrī.
A chronogram of the victory was found in the words Fatḥ-i-dāru'l-ḥarb.
Babur composed a verse about the victory of Chandīrī.
Chandīrī is situated in rather good country having much running-water round about it.
Chandīrī's citadel has a tank cut out of the solid rock.
There is another large tank at the end of the double-walled road.
All houses in Chandīrī are built of stone.
Houses of chiefs in Chandīrī are laboriously carved.
Houses of the lower classes in Chandīrī are of stone but not carved.
Houses in Chandīrī are covered with stone-slabs instead of earthen tiles.
There are three large tanks in front of the fort made by former governors.
Chandīrī has a small river called Betwa.
The Betwa is about 3 kurohs from Chandīrī.
The Betwa's water is noted in Hindūstān as excellent and pleasant drinking.
The Betwa's bed has pieces of sloping rock fit for making houses.
Chandīrī is 90 kurohs by road to the south of Āgra.
In Chandīrī the altitude of the Pole-star is 25 degrees.
Babur went round the fort and dismounted beside Mallū Khān's tank on Thursday.
Babur had come to Chandīrī meaning to move against Rāīsīng, Bhīlsān, and Sārangpūr after taking it.
Babur intended to move on Rānā Sangā in Chītūr after taking the pagan lands.
The begs were summoned and decided to first see to the rebellion of the malignants.
Chandīrī was given to Aḥmad Shāh, a grandson of Sl. Nāṣiru'd-dīn.
50 laks from Chandīrī were made khalṣa.
Mullā Apāq was entrusted with Chandīrī's military-collectorate.
Mullā Apāq was left to reinforce Aḥmad Shāh with from 2 to 3000 Turks and Hindūstānīs.
Babur marched from Mallū Khān's tank on Sunday the 11th of the first Jumāda with the intention of return north.
Babur dismounted on the bank of the Burhānpūr-water.
Yakka Khwāja and Ja`far Khwāja were sent from Bāndīr to fetch boats from Kālpī to the Kanār-passage.
Babur dismounted at the Kanār-passage on Saturday the 24th of the month.
Babur ordered the army to begin to cross the river.
The expeditionary force abandoned Qanūj and came to Rāprī.
A strong body of the enemy assaulted and taken Shamsābād.
There was delay of 3 or 4 days before the army got across the river.
Babur sent scouting braves ahead to get news of opponents.
Ma`rūf's son fled on seeing the dark mass of the news-gatherers.
Bīban, Bāyazīd and Ma`rūf crossed Gang and seated themselves on its eastern bank opposite Qanūj.
Bīban, Bāyazīd and Ma`rūf thought to prevent Babur's passage of the Ganges.
Babur passed Qanūj and dismounted on the western bank of Gang on Thursday the 6th of the latter Jumāda.
Some of Babur's braves took boats by force and brought in 30 or 40.
Mīr Muḥammad the raftsman was sent to find boats.
An expeditionary force abandoned Qanūj and came to Rāprī.
There was a delay of 3 or 4 days before the army crossed the river.
Ma`rūf's son fled upon seeing the dark mass of news-gatherers.
Bīban, Bāyazīd and Ma`rūf crossed the Ganges and seated themselves on its eastern bank opposite Qanūj.
On February 27th, Babur passed Qanūj and dismounted on the western bank of the Ganges.
Braves took boats by force, bringing in 30 or 40 boats.
Mīr Muḥammad the raftsman found a place convenient for making a bridge about 2 miles below the camp.
Ustād `Alī-qulī placed the mortar for stone-discharge near where the bridge was to be.
Muṣṯafa Rūmī had the culverin-carts crossed over to an island below the bridge.
Malik Qāsim Mughūl and a few men crossed the river and fought excellently.
Bābā Sl. and Darwīsh Sl. crossed with insufficient numbers of 10 to 15 men and were blamed for their crossing.
Malik Qāsim attacked the enemy's camp and drew out the enemy with a mass of men and an elephant.
Malik Qāsim died when an elephant swamped his boat.
Ustād `Alī-qulī discharged 8 stones on the first day and 16 on the second day.
The Ghāzī-mortar was used in the battle with Rānā Sangā the pagan.
Another larger mortar burst after discharging one stone.
On March 11th, the bridge was almost finished.
On March 12th, the bridge was ready and a small body of foot-soldiers and Lāhorīs went over.
On March 13th, the royal corps and the right and left hands of the centre crossed on foot.
The whole body of Afghāns attacked with armed men, mounted soldiers, and elephants.
Seven or eight heads were brought in from the fighting on March 13th.
Fighting went on till the Other Prayer on March 13th.
That night all who had gone across were made to return.
Babur did not make the rest of the army cross because he thought they would beat the enemy on Sunday.
Last year Babur marched out of Sīkrī to fight Rānā Sangā on Tuesday, New-year's-day, and crushed that rebel on Saturday.
This year Babur marched to crush the rebels on Wednesday, New-year's-day.
On Sunday March 15th, the carts were taken over and the army was ordered to cross.
At dawn on March 15th, news came from scouts that the enemy had fled.
Chīn-tīmūr Sl. was ordered to lead his army in pursuit.
Babur crossed at the Sunnat Prayer.
On Sunday March 15th, Babur dismounted on the bank of standing-water within 2 miles of Bangarmāwū.
Those appointed to pursue the Afghāns dismounted in Bangarmāwū and were scurrying off at the Mid-day Prayer.
On March 16th, Tūkhtā-būghā Sl., a son of Babur's mother's brother the Younger Khān, came and waited on Babur.
On March 21st, Babur visited Laknau and crossed the Gūī-water.
Babur bathed in the Gūī-water on March 21st.
Babur's right ear was obstructed after bathing in the Gūī-water, causing pain for a few days.
One or two marches from Aūd, someone came from Chīn-tīmūr Sl. saying the enemy was seated on the far side of the river Sīrd.
Babur detached a reinforcement of 1000 braves under Qarācha.
On March 28th, Babur dismounted 2 or 3 miles from Aūd above the junction of the Gagar and Sīrd.
Shaikh Bāyazīd was on the other side of the Sīrd opposite Aūd, sending letters to the Sulṯān.
The Sulṯān got to know Shaikh Bāyazīd's deceitfulness.
Qarācha crossed the river to where there were some 50 horsemen with 3 or 4 elephants.
The 50 horsemen could make no stand and fled.
Bī-khūb Sl., Tardī Beg, Bābā Chuhra, and Bāqī shaghāwal crossed over following the Sulṯān.
Those who crossed first pursued Shaikh Bāyazīd till the Evening Prayer.
Shaikh Bāyazīd flung himself into the jungle and escaped.
Chīn-tīmūr dismounted late on the bank of standing-water and rode on at midnight after the rebel.
Chīn-tīmūr went as much as 40 miles and came to where Shaikh Bāyazīd's family and relations had been.
Shaikh Bāyazīd's family must have fled.
Bāqī shaghāwal and a few braves drove the enemy like sheep before them and brought in some Afghān prisoners.
Babur stayed a few days near Aūd to settle the affairs of Aūd.
People praised the land lying along the Sīrd 7 or 8 miles above Aūd as hunting-ground.
Mīr Muḥammad the raftsman was sent out to look at the crossings over the Gagar-water and the Sīrd-water.
On April 2nd, Babur rode out intending to hunt.
There is a break in the narrative between April 2nd and September 18th, 1528 AD.
The break is likely due to a loss of pages from Babur's autograph manuscript.
During the 5-1/2 months of the lacuna, Babur was at Jūn-pūr, Chausa, and Baksara.
Babur swam the Ganges during the period of the lacuna.
Babur bestowed Sarūn on a Farmūlī Shaikh-zāda.
Babur negotiated with Rānā Sangā's son Bikramājīt.
Babur ordered a Chār-bāgh laid out.
Babur was ill for 40 days during the period of the lacuna.
Babur visited Dūlpūr.
Babur recalled `Askarī.
Babur sent Khwāja Dost-i-khāwand on family affairs to Kābul.
Babur was much pre-occupied by the disturbed state of Kābul.
On September 18th, `Askarī whom Babur had summoned for the good of Multān waited on him in the private-house.
On September 19th, the historian Khwānd-amīr, Maulānā Shihāb the enigmatist, and Mīr Ibrāhīm the harper waited on Babur.
Khwānd-amīr, Maulānā Shihāb, and Mīr Ibrāhīm had come out of Herī long before.
On September 20th, Babur crossed the Jūn with the intention of visiting Gūālīār.
Babur went into the fort of Āgra to bid farewell to Fakhr-i-jahān Begīm and Khadīja-sulṯān Begīm.
Fakhr-i-jahān Begīm and Khadīja-sulṯān Begīm were to start for Kābul in a few days.
Muḥammad-i-zamān Mīrzā asked for leave and stayed behind in Āgra.
On September 21st, Babur nooned on the bank of the Gambhīr-water.
Mullā Rafī` prepared powders mixed with the flour of parched grain for raising the spirits.
The powders were found very distasteful and unsavoury.
On September 21st, Babur dismounted a mile west of Dūlpūr at a place where a garden and house had been ordered made.
Babur had ordered the beak of a hill cut down to ground-level.
Babur ordered a house to be cut out in the hill if there remained sufficient height.
The hill was not found high enough for a house.
Ūstād Shāh Muḥammad the stone-cutter was ordered to level the hill and cut out an octagonal, roofed tank.
North of the tank the ground is thick with trees, mangoes, and jāman.
Babur had ordered a well made, 10 by 10, amongst the trees.
The well's water goes to the octagonal tank.
Sl. Sikandar's dam is flung across the valley north of the tank.
Houses have been built on Sikandar's dam.
Above the dam the waters of the Rains gather into a great lake.
On the east of the lake is a garden.
Babur ordered a seat and four-pillared platform to be cut out in the solid rock on the east side of the lake.
Babur ordered a mosque built on the western side of the lake.
On September 22nd and 23rd, Babur stayed in Dūlpūr on account of various works.
On September 24th, Babur rode on and crossed the Chaṃbal-river.
Babur made the Mid-day Prayer on the bank of the Chaṃbal-river.
Babur passed the Kawārī-water.
The Kawārī-water was high through rain.
Babur crossed the Kawārī-water by boat, making the horses swim over.
On September 25th, Babur rode on and took his nooning at a village on the road.
On September 25th, Babur dismounted a mile north of Gūālīār in a Chār-bāgh ordered made last year.
On September 26th, Babur visited the low hills to the north of Gūālīār.
Babur visited the Praying-place at Gūālīār.
Babur went into the fort of Gūālīār through the Gate called Hātī-pūl.
The Hātī-pūl joins Mān-sing's buildings.
Babur dismounted at the buildings of Rāja Bikramājīt in which Raḥīm-dād had settled himself.
On September 26th, Babur elected to take opium because of ear-ache.
Another reason Babur took opium was the shining of the moon.
On September 27th, opium sickness gave Babur much discomfort.
Babur vomited a good deal from opium sickness.
Despite sickness, Babur visited the buildings of Mān-sing and Bikramājīt thoroughly.
The buildings of Mān-sing and Bikramājīt are wonderful buildings, entirely of hewn stone.
The buildings are in heavy and unsymmetrical blocks.
Of all the Rājas' buildings, Mān-sing's is the best and loftiest.
Mān-sing's building is more elaborately worked on its eastern face than on the others.
The eastern face of Mān-sing's building may be 40 to 50 yards high.
The eastern face is entirely of hewn stone, whitened with plaster.
Mān-sing's building is four storeys high in parts.
The lower two storeys of Mān-sing's building are very dark.
Babur went through the lower storeys with candles.
On one side of Mān-sing's building are five cupolas.
Between each two cupolas is a smaller one, square after the fashion of Hindūstān.
On the larger cupolas are fastened sheets of gilded copper.
On the outside of the walls is painted-tile work.
The painted-tile work shows the semblance of plantain-trees with green tiles.
In a bastion of the eastern front is the Hātī-pūl.
Babur took opium because of ear-ache.
The shining of the moon was another reason Babur took opium.
Opium sickness gave Babur much discomfort the day after taking it.
Despite sickness, Babur visited the buildings of Mān-sing and Bikramājīt.
Babur visited the buildings of Mān-sing's son Bikramājīt.
Bikramājīt's buildings do not match his father's.
Babur rode to a college that Raḥīm-dād had made by the side of a large tank.
Babur enjoyed a flower-garden that Raḥīm-dād had laid out.
Raḥīm-dād planted a great number of flowers in his garden, many being beautiful red oleanders.
Babur took some oleanders from Gūālīār to Āgra and had them planted in gardens there.
Babur visited places in Gūālīār he had not yet seen.
Babur saw the building called Bādalgar which is part of Mān-sing's fort.
Babur went through the Hātī-pūl and across the fort to a place called Urwā.
Urwā is a valley-bottom on the western side of the fort.
Three sides of Urwā are solid rock.
Babur ordered the idols in Urwā destroyed.
Babur enjoyed the window of the Sultānī-pūl.
Babur went to Raḥīm-dād's flower-garden at the Evening Prayer and slept there.
People from Rānā Sangā's second son Bikramājīt came to negotiate with Babur.
Bikramājīt's mother was named Padmāwatī.
Bikramājīt was in the fort of Rantanbūr.
Babur promised Shamsābād in exchange for Rantanbūr to Bikramājīt's envoys.
Babur gave Bikramājīt's envoys a nine days' tryst for Bīāna.
Babur dressed Bikramājīt's envoys in robes of honour.
Babur visited the idol-houses of Gūālīār.
Raḥīm-dād prepared a feast of cooked-meat for Babur.
Raḥīm-dād made an offering of goods and coin worth 4 laks to Babur.
Babur went to see a waterfall 6 kurohs to the south-east of Gūālīār.
Babur sat down above the waterfall and ate ma'jūn.
Babur went up-stream to visit the waterfall's source.
Babur stayed while musicians played and reciters repeated things.
Babur visited the garden of lemons and pumeloes in a valley-bottom amongst the hills above a village called Sūkhjana.
Sūkhjana is Ṣalāḥu'd-dīn's birth-place.
Babur rode before dawn from the Chār-bāgh and crossed the Kawārī-water.
Babur passed the Chaṃbal-water at sunset.
Babur entered Dulpūr-fort between the Evening and Bed-time Prayers.
Babur visited a Hot-bath which Abū'l-fatḥ had made.
Babur ordered a roofed-tank to be cut in the solid rock.
Babur ordered a water-chamber to be made at a place where it would be cut in the solid rock.
Babur had pain in his ear during the night of Wednesday.
Babur was uncertain whether his ear pain was caused by cold.
Babur rode for Sīkrī.
The garden-wall and well-buildings at Sīkrī were not getting on to Babur's satisfaction.
Babur threatened and punished the overseers at Sīkrī.
Babur got into Āgra during the first watch.
Babur saw Khadīja-sulṯān Begīm in the fort at Āgra.
Khadīja-sulṯān Begīm had stayed behind when Fakhr-i-jahān Begīm started for Kābul.
Babur went to the Garden-of-eight paradises.
Babur went to see three great-aunt begīms: Gauhar-shād Begīm, Badī`u'l-jamāl Begīm, and Āq Begīm.
Babur also saw Sl. Maṣ`ūd Mīrzā's daughter Khān-zāda Begīm.
Babur saw Sulṯān-bakht Begīm's daughter.
Babur saw his yīnkā chīcha's grand-daughter, Zaināb-sulṯān Begīm.
The kinswomen had come past Tūta and dismounted at a small standing-water on the edge of the suburbs.
Babur came back direct by boat.
Babur sent Hāmūsī son of Dīwa to negotiate with Bikramājīt about the surrender of Ranthanbūr.
Babur promised to set Bikramājīt in his father's place as Rānā of Chitūr if he stood fast to his spoken word.
The treasure of Iskandar and Ibrāhīm in Dihlī and Āgra was at an end.
Babur ordered each stipendiary to drop 30 in every 100 of his allowance into the Dīwān.
Babur sent letters to Herī with news that his heart was at ease in Hindūstān about the rebels and pagans.
Babur wrote a summons to Farīdūn the qabūz-player on the margin of a royal letter with his own hand.
Babur made a beginning of eating quicksilver.
Kāmrān went into Kābul on the 17th of Ẕū'l-ḥijja.
Kāmrān let Khwāja Dost-i-khāwand go on to Fort Victory on the 28th of Ẕū'l-ḥijja.
Shāh-zāda T̤ahmāsp overcame and killed Rīnīsh Aūzbeg in Dāmghān.
Shāh-zāda T̤ahmāsp made a general massacre of Rīnīsh's people.
Humāyūn was said to have had a son by the daughter of Yādgār T̤aghāī.
Kāmrān was said to be marrying in Kābul, taking the daughter of his mother's brother Sl. `Alī Mīrzā.
Sayyid Daknī of Shīrāz was made to wear a dress of honour.
Sayyid Daknī was given presents.
Sayyid Daknī was ordered to finish the arched well.
Babur had such heat in his body that he had difficulty getting through the Congregational Prayer.
Babur had fever.
Babur trembled less on Sunday.
During the night of Tuesday the 27th of the month Ṣafar, it occurred to Babur to versify the Wālidiyyah-risāla.
Babur laid it to heart that if he were freed from his disease, it would be a sign that his poem was accepted.
Babur began to versify the tract using the metre of Maulānā 'Abdu´r-raḥīm Jāmī's Subḥatu'l-abrār.
Babur made thirteen couplets in the same night.
Babur tasked himself not to make fewer than 10 couplets a day.
One day had been omitted from Babur's task of making couplets.
Babur experienced intense heat in his body on Friday the 23rd of the month.
Babur attended the Congregational Prayer in the Mosque with difficulty due to illness.
Babur had fever and trembled less by Sunday.
During the night of Tuesday the 27th of Safar, Babur decided to versify the Wālidiyyah-risāla of Khwāja 'Ubaidu'l-lāh.
Babur sought protection from his Reverence Khwāja 'Ubaidu'l-lāh to be freed from disease.
Babur believed that being freed from disease would be a sign that his poem was accepted.
Babur used the metre of Maulānā 'Abdu'r-raḥīm Jāmī's Subḥatu'l-abrār for his versification.
Babur composed thirteen couplets on the first night of versification.
Babur tasked himself to make no fewer than 10 couplets per day.
Babur omitted one day from his daily couplet-writing task.
In previous years, Babur's illnesses persisted for at least a month or 40 days.
Babur was freed from illness by Thursday the 29th of the month, except for slight depression.
Babur completed the versification of the tract on Saturday the 8th of the first Rabī'.
Babur composed 52 couplets in a single day.
Babur sent royal orders for armies to prepare for service.
Babur received news of the birth of Humāyūn's son named Al-amān.
Babur believed that Humāyūn's son's name Al-amān was uncommon in its use of the article 'Al'.
Babur was concerned that common people might mispronounce Al-amān as alāmā or aīlāmān.
Babur received news of Shāh-zāda Ṭahmāsp's victory over the Aūzbegs.
Babur ordered Kāmrān and the Kābul begs to join Humāyūn.
Babur instructed Humāyūn to move toward Ḥiṣār, Samarkand, or Herī as fortune favored.
Babur believed that slothful life in retirement does not befit sovereign rule.
Babur established a rule that when Humāyūn had six parts, Kāmrān should have five.
Babur advised Humāyūn to live well with his younger brother Kāmrān.
Babur expressed that elders must bear the burden in family matters.
Babur complained that Humāyūn had sent few words and no person from him for two or three years.
Babur criticized Humāyūn's letter-writing for being obscure and difficult to read.
Babur corrected Humāyūn's spelling errors in his letter.
Babur advised Humāyūn to write without elaboration using plain, clear words.
Babur instructed Humāyūn to take counsel with prudent and experienced begs.
Babur advised Humāyūn to summon his younger brother and the begs twice daily to his presence.
Babur had long-standing house-friend intimacy with Khwāja Kalān.
Babur made Kābul a crown-domain.
Babur ordered that no one should covet Kābul.
Babur praised Humāyūn for winning the heart of Sl. Wais.
Babur instructed Humāyūn not to move out until there was a good muster of the army.
Babur summoned the mīrzās, sulṯāns, and amīrs for counsel regarding campaign plans.
Babur decided that the army must move in some direction that year.
Babur ordered `Askarī to go in advance toward the East.
Babur sent Ghīāṣu'd-dīn the armourer to the amīrs of the East with a 16-day rendezvous.
Babur instructed the amīrs to consult with well-wishers before moving in any direction.
Babur gave `Askarī the insignia and rank of a royal commander.
Babur presented `Askarī with a jewelled dagger, belt, royal dress, flag, horse-tail standard, drum, horses, elephants, camels, and mules.
Babur ordered `Askarī to take his seat at the head of a Dīwān.
Babur visited Sl. Muḥammad Bakhshī's house on Sunday the last day of Rabī' I.
Babur ate ma`jūn at Sl. Muḥammad Bakhshī's house.
Babur left Sl. Muḥammad Bakhshī's house at the 3rd watch.
Babur ordered the road between Āgra and Kābul to be measured.
Babur ordered towers to be erected at every 9th kuroh along the Āgra-Kābul road.
Babur ordered post-horses to be kept at every 18th kuroh along the road.
Babur arranged for payment of post-masters, grooms, and horse-corn along the road.
Babur held a feast at which Qīzīl-bāsh, Aūzbeg, and Hindū envoys were present.
Babur sat on the north side of an octagonal pavilion at the feast.
Babur had fierce camels and fierce elephants fight on an island during the feast.
Babur had wrestlers grapple during the feast.
Babur presented gifts of gold-embroidered surtouts and dresses of honour to various envoys and officials.
Babur presented silver and gold to the chief envoys at the feast.
Babur organized a spectacle featuring fierce elephants, rams, and wrestlers fighting on an island.
Babur presented fine muslin surtouts spotted with gold-embroidery to Khwāja `Abdu'sh-shahīd and Khwāja Kalān.
Babur gave silken head-wear and gold-embroidered surtouts to Kūchūm Khān's envoy and Ḥasan Chalabi's younger brother.
Babur presented gold-embroidered jackets and silk coats to the envoys of Abū-sa`īd, Mihr-bān Khānīm, and Shāh Ḥasan.
Babur gave a silver stone's weight of gold and a gold stone's weight of silver to the two Khwājas and two chief envoys.
Babur rewarded Mīr Muḥammad the raftsman with a dagger for building an excellent bridge over the Ganges River.
Babur presented gifts to matchlockmen Champion Ḥājī Muḥammad and Champion Buhlūl.
Babur gave gifts to the servants of his daughter Ma`ṣūma and his son Hind-āl.
Babur distributed gifts to people from Andijān and those from Sūkh and Hushīār.
Babur ordered Hindūstānī players to perform their tricks at a gathering.
Babur observed that Hindūstānī performers demonstrated feats not shown by Tramontane performers.
Babur visited the Eight-Paradises by boat on Sunday, Rabi` II. 7th.
Babur's son `Askarī departed from the Hot-bath to march eastward on Monday, Rabī` II. 8th.
Babur went to see buildings for a reservoir and well at Dūlpūr on Tuesday, Rabī` II. 9th.
Babur rode from the Āgra garden at 9.22 a.m. and entered the Dūlpūr garden at 7.40 p.m.
Babur ordered the well-wheel at Dūlpūr to run without rest for 15 days and nights due to a smell in the water.
Babur gave gifts to stone-cutters and laborers at Dūlpūr.
Babur received news of a battle between the Qīzīl-bāsh and Aūzbeg near Jām-and-Khirgird on `Āshūr-day.
Babur learned that the Aūzbegs numbered 300,000 in the battle near Jām.
Babur learned that the Turkmāns were estimated at 40 to 50,000 or possibly 100,000 in the battle near Jām.
Babur received news that nine sulṯāns including Kūchūm Khān and `Ubaid Khān were captured in the battle near Jām.
Babur learned that 50,000 Aūzbegs and 20,000 Turkmāns were slain in the battle near Jām.
Babur sent Ghīāṣu'd-dīn the armourer to Jūna-pūr with a tryst of 16 days.
Babur received word from Sl. Junaid that no significant problems had emerged in his region.
Babur learned that Mullā Muḥammad Maẕhab had been sent as envoy to Bengal after a battle with Sangā the Pagan.
Babur ate ma`jūn and sat with a special few in the private house on Friday, Rabī` II. 19th.
Babur learned from Mullā Maẕhab that the Bengalī's attitude was understood to be loyal and single-minded.
Babur summoned Turkish and Indian amīrs to the private house on Sunday, Rabī` II. 21st for counsel.
Babur decided to delay westward military action to ensure peace about the East.
Babur sent Ghīāṣu'd-dīn the armourer to convey written orders to eastern amīrs with a tryst of 20 days.
Babur appointed Chīn-tīmūr Sl. to address Balūchī incursions.
Babur ordered Chīn-tīmūr Sl. to gather amīrs from beyond Sihrind and Samāna and proceed against the Balūchīs equipped for 6 months.
Babur appointed `Abdu'l-ghaffār as messenger to carry orders to Chīn-tīmūr Sl. and the assembled begs.
Babur instructed `Abdu'l-ghaffār to report any slackness or carelessness by any person in the army.
Babur crossed the Jūn at 2.15 a.m. on the eve of Sunday, Rabi` II. 28th and started for the Lotus-garden of Dūlpūr.
Babur assigned places on the border of the Dūlpūr garden for begs and the household to build camping-grounds.
Babur ordered a Hot-bath to be arranged in the s.e. of the Dūlpūr garden on Thursday, Jumāda I. 3rd.
Babur ordered a plinth erected and a Bath arranged with a reservoir 10 X 10 at Dūlpūr.
Babur received news that Iskandar's son Maḥmūd had taken Bihār town.
Babur decided to get the army to horse upon hearing that Bihār had been taken.
Babur rode out from the Lotus-garden at 8.15 a.m. on Friday, Jumāda I. 4th and reached Āgra at Evening Prayer.
Babur met Muḥammad-i-zamān Mīrzā and Chīn-tīmūr on the road to Āgra.
Babur summoned counselling begs on Saturday, Jumāda I. 5th and settled to ride eastwards on Thursday, Jumāda I. 10th.
Babur received letters from Kābul reporting that Humāyūn had mustered the army and set out with 40,000 men for Samarkand.
Babur learned that Sl. Wais' younger brother Shāh-qūlī entered Ḥiṣār.
Babur learned that Tarsūn Muḥammad left Tirmiẕ and took Qabādīān.
Babur learned that Humāyūn sent Tūlik Kūkūldāsh and Mīr Khẉurd with many men and Mughūls to help Tarsūn Muḥammad.
Babur set out for the East on Thursday, Jumāda I. 10th after the 3rd _garī_ at approximately 7.10 a.m.
Babur crossed the Jūn by boat a little above Jalīsīr.
Babur ordered the standard, drum, stable and army-folk to remain on the other side of the water opposite the Gold-scattering-garden.
Babur ordered that persons coming for an interview should cross by boat.
Babur received the Bengal envoy Ismā`īl Mītā on Saturday, Jumāda I. 12th.
Babur received Naṣrat Shāh's offering and letter from the Bengal envoy.
Babur gave the Bengal envoy a dress of honour.
Babur waited on the honoured Khwāja `Abdu'l-ḥaqq on Monday, Jumāda I. 14th.
Babur received Ḥasan Chalabī on Tuesday, Jumāda I. 15th.
Babur spent some days in the Chār-bāgh on account of his aims for the army.
Babur left the Chār-bāgh ground on Thursday, Jumāda I. 17th after the 3rd _garī_ at 7.10 a.m.
Babur traveled by boat and dismounted 14 miles from Āgra at the village of Anwār.
Babur gave the Aūzbeg envoys their leave on Sunday, Jumāda I. 20th.
Babur presented Kūchūm Khān's envoy Amīn Mīrzā with a dagger with belt, cloth of gold, and 70,000 _tankas_.
Babur gave Abū-sa`īd's servant Mullā T̤aghāī and the servants of Mihr-bān Khānim dresses of honour with gold-embroidered jackets and money.
Babur gave Khwāja `Abdu'l-ḥaqq leave to stay in Āgra on Monday, Jumāda I. 21st.
Babur gave Khwāja Kalān leave to go to Samarkand on Monday, Jumāda I. 21st.
Babur sent Mullā Tabrīzī and Mīrzā Beg T̤aghāī with gifts to Humāyūn and Kāmrān in congratulation on the birth of Humāyūn's son and Kāmrān's marriage.
Babur sent each Mīrzā gifts of 10,000 _shāhrukhīs_, a coat he had worn, and a belt with clasps.
Babur sent Hind-āl an inlaid dagger with belt, an inlaid ink-stand, a stool worked in mother-o'pearl, a tunic and a girdle through Mullā Bihishtī.
Babur sent Hind-āl the alphabet of the Bāburī script and fragments written in that script.
Babur sent Humāyūn the translation and verses made in Hindūstān.
Babur sent Kāmrān the translation and verses together with head-lines in the Bāburī script.
Babur entrusted the buildings in hand at Āgra and Dūlpūr to the charge of Mullā Qāsim, Ustād Shāh Muḥammad, Mīrak, Mīr Ghīāṣ, Mīr Sang-tarāsh and Shāh Bābā on Tuesday, Jumāda I. 22nd.
Babur rode out from Anwār on Wednesday, Jumāda I. 23rd near the first watch at 6 a.m.
Babur dismounted at the Mid-day Prayer in the village of Ābāpūr, one _kuroh_ from Chandawār.
Babur sent `Abdu'l-malūk the armourer as envoy to the Shāh joined with Ḥasan Chalabī on the eve of Thursday, Jumāda I. 24th.
Babur sent Chāpūq joined with the Aūzbeg envoys to the Aūzbeg khāns and sulṯāns.
Babur moved from Ābāpūr while 4 _garīs_ of the night remained at 4.30 a.m.
Babur passed Chandawār at the top of the dawn and got into a boat.
